topics.title: Digital System Design Options and Tradeoffs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital System Design Options and Tradeoffs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 1 — Digital System Design Options and Tradeoffs

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Digital System Design Options and Tradeoffs* refers to the systematic engineering methodology of selecting the most appropriate hardware implementation platform (ASIC, ASSP, FPGA, CPLD, Microcontroller, DSP, or General-Purpose Processor) for a given digital application by quantitatively comparing non-recurring engineering (NRE) cost, per-unit cost, performance (speed/throughput), power consumption, design flexibility, re-programmability, and time-to-market constraints.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PERAT755 (FPGA Based System Design)**, this topic forms the foundational decision-making framework that every VLSI/Embedded System engineer must master *before* committing to a particular silicon realization path. A digital system is a deterministic collection of logic gates, flip-flops, memory elements, and interconnect structures that processes binary information according to a well-defined Boolean specification. The "design option" therefore refers to the *physical medium* on which this Boolean specification is realized.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations:**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) places strong emphasis on **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Module 1 questions frequently test a student's ability to *justify* a design choice, *compare* alternatives, and *compute* the break-even volume for an FPGA-to-ASIC migration. Marks are awarded for reasoning, not mere listing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vehicle Selection" Metaphor

Imagine you need to transport 50 kg of goods across a city. Your options are:

| Real-World Vehicle | Digital System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A custom-built sports car (extremely fast, very expensive, one-of-a-kind) | **Full-Custom ASIC** (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) |
| A factory-produced sedan (cheaper per unit once tooled, optimized for a class of users) | **ASSP** (Application-Specific Standard Product) |
| A bicycle (cheap, flexible, you decide the route, slower) | **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array) |
| A pre-built Lego set with reconfigurable blocks (modular, reusable) | **CPLD** (Complex Programmable Logic Device) |
| A remote-controlled toy car (predefined functions, easy to use) | **Microcontroller / Microprocessor** |
| A specialized food-processor (tuned for one repetitive task) | **DSP** (Digital Signal Processor) |

Just as you would not use a sports car to deliver milk daily or a bicycle to win a Formula-1 race, the engineer must not blindly jump into FPGA design without weighing alternatives.

### 1.3 The Canonical Design Options — Formal Taxonomy

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus structure, the following six implementation families constitute the standard "menu" of digital system design options:

1. **General-Purpose Microprocessor / Microcontroller (MPU/MCU)** — Software-driven, fixed hardware, instruction-fetch-execute cycle.
2. **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC)** — Hard-wired silicon, mask-programmed, cannot be altered post-fabrication.
3. **Application-Specific Standard Product (ASSP)** — Mass-produced ASIC sold as a commodity (e.g., USB controller chips, Ethernet PHYs).
4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — Specialized Harvard-architecture processor with MAC units and SIMD capabilities.
5. **Complex Programmable Logic Device (CPLD)** — Coarse-grained, non-volatile, low-density programmable logic.
6. **Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)** — Fine-grained, high-density, SRAM/Flash/Anti-fuse-based reconfigurable logic with embedded hard-IP blocks (DSP slices, BRAM, transceivers, processor cores).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently tested):**
> An **ASIC is a chip customized for *one* customer** (e.g., Apple's A-series SoC), whereas an **ASSP is a chip customized for a *function* and sold to many** customers (e.g., a Broadcom HDMI receiver). Both use mask-based fabrication; the difference lies in the *business model* and *target audience*.

### 1.4 The Seven Pillars of Design Tradeoff

Every digital system design decision pivots around seven quantifiable design metrics. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to fluently define and apply these:

1. **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) Cost** — One-time charge for masks, tools, and design verification. Expressed in **USD (millions)**.
2. **Unit Cost (Recurring Cost)** — Per-chip fabrication + packaging + test cost.
3. **Performance (Speed/Throughput)** — Measured in **MHz, FPS, GOPS, or latency in ns**.
4. **Power Consumption** — Static + Dynamic, measured in **mW or W**.
5. **Design Flexibility / Re-programmability** — Ability to alter functionality post-deployment.
6. **Time-to-Market** — Wall-clock time from spec to production, measured in **months**.
7. **Volume (Production Quantity)** — Number of units produced over the product lifetime, a dimensionless count.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
> The PERAT755 syllabus explicitly mandates coverage of *design metrics and trade-offs in digital system design*, with specific reference to **why FPGAs dominate prototyping, low-volume, and high-flexibility niches**, while **ASICs dominate high-volume, power-critical, performance-critical mass production**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architectural Anatomy of Each Design Option

### 2.1.1 General-Purpose Microprocessor / Microcontroller (MPU/MCU)

The MPU/MCU executes a stored *program* (instruction sequence) on a fixed datapath. The silicon is pre-designed by the chip vendor; the user only writes firmware.

- **Architecture:** Von Neumann (MCU) or Harvard (DSP/MCU variants).
- **Realization:** CMOS standard cells with embedded Flash, SRAM, peripherals.
- **Strengths:** Lowest NRE (often zero in small volumes), infinite flexibility via software, rich ecosystem, low power in sleep modes.
- **Weaknesses:** Sequential execution limits throughput; cannot exploit fine-grained parallelism; performance is bounded by clock × IPC (Instructions Per Cycle).
- **Best Suited For:** Control-dominated tasks, low-data-rate applications, cost-sensitive consumer products.

### 2.1.2 ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)

The ASIC is a fully customized chip where every transistor, wire, and metal layer is purpose-designed for a single application.

- **Architecture:** Custom logic + standard cells + IP blocks; no programmable interconnect.
- **Realization:** Photolithographic mask sets (7–14 masks for modern nodes).
- **Strengths:** Highest performance, lowest power (no programmable interconnect overhead), lowest per-unit cost at high volume.
- **Weaknesses:** Extremely high NRE (USD 10M–USD 500M+ for sub-7nm), long fabrication lead time (3–6 months), zero post-silicon flexibility, high risk of respin.
- **Best Suited For:** High-volume consumer electronics (smartphones, SSDs), automotive ECUs, IoT SoCs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Full-Custom vs Semi-Custom ASIC:**
> *Full-custom ASIC* — Every transistor hand-crafted (used in analog/RF mixed-signal).
> *Semi-custom ASIC* — Uses pre-designed standard-cell libraries and gate arrays (used in digital logic). The KTU syllabus primarily addresses the **semi-custom** variety.

### 2.1.3 ASSP (Application-Specific Standard Product)

The ASSP is functionally identical to an ASIC from a silicon perspective, but the *end customer is the OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer)*, not the chip designer. The chip is sold as a commodity component.

- **Examples:** Wi-Fi chipsets, USB controllers, HDMI receivers, GPS basebands.
- **Strengths:** Mass-production economies of scale → extremely low per-unit cost; pre-verified silicon.
- **Weaknesses:** No customization, vendor lock-in, may include unwanted features (area/power overhead).

### 2.1.4 DSP (Digital Signal Processor)

The DSP is a specialized microprocessor with architectural features that accelerate numerical signal-processing kernels.

- **Architecture:** Modified Harvard (separate program/data memories), hardware MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) units, zero-overhead looping, SIMD extensions.
- **Strengths:** Highly efficient for FFTs, filters, convolutions; deterministic interrupt latency; low power per MAC operation.
- **Weaknesses:** Still sequential at heart; less flexible than a general-purpose CPU; cannot match ASIC throughput for ultra-high-bandwidth applications (e.g., 5G baseband).
- **Best Suited For:** Audio codecs, motor control, communications baseband, biomedical signal processing.

### 2.1.5 CPLD (Complex Programmable Logic Device)

The CPLD bridges the gap between small SPLDs (Simple PLDs) and FPGAs.

- **Architecture:** Array of PAL/GAL-like macrocells connected via a central programmable interconnect matrix; non-volatile (Flash/EEPROM-based).
- **Strengths:** Non-volatile configuration (instant-on, no external boot PROM), deterministic timing, very low power, excellent for glue logic and control-plane tasks.
- **Weaknesses:** Limited logic density (typically < 1000 macrocells), coarse-grained architecture, not suitable for complex datapaths.
- **Best Suited For:** Board-level glue logic, address decoding, state-machine controllers, boot configuration of larger FPGAs/ASICs.

### 2.1.6 FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)

The FPGA is the *programmable workhorse* of modern digital design — an array of configurable logic blocks (CLBs), programmable interconnect, and hard-IP blocks (DSP slices, Block RAM, transceivers, PLL, PCIe cores, even hard processor cores in SoC FPGAs).

- **Configuration Technologies:**
  - **SRAM-based** (e.g., Xilinx 7-series, Intel Cyclone) — Volatile, requires external boot Flash, finest process node available.
  - **Flash/Anti-fuse-based** (e.g., Microsemi IGLOO2, QuickLogic) — Non-volatile, lower power, radiation tolerant.
- **Strengths:** Lowest NRE, infinite in-field re-programmability, parallel hardware execution, parallel design verification (no respin risk), rich IP ecosystem.
- **Weaknesses:** Higher per-unit cost than ASIC at high volume; ~10–20× silicon area overhead (programmable interconnect dominates); higher static power; lower raw clock frequency than a hard-wired ASIC.
- **Best Suited For:** Prototyping, low-to-medium volume production, design verification (ASIC emulation), aerospace/defense, ASIC prototyping, high-performance computing accelerators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "FPGA vs ASIC" Performance Gap — A Quantitative Insight:**
> A well-designed ASIC in a 28nm node typically operates at **2× to 5× the clock frequency** of an equivalent 28nm FPGA implementation, while consuming **1/10th the power** and **1/100th the silicon area**. The gap *narrows* at smaller nodes (16nm, 7nm) because FPGAs adopt the latest process immediately, but it never fully closes.

## 2.2 The Seven-Metric Design Tradeoff Matrix

The KTU 2024 examiner expects a comparative table. The following is the high-yield cheat-sheet that must be memorized for Module 1:

| Design Metric | MPU/MCU | DSP | CPLD | FPGA | ASIC | ASSP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NRE Cost (USD) | Very Low (<10K) | Low (<50K) | Very Low (<5K) | Low (<100K) | Very High (10M–500M) | High (Vendor pays) |
| Per-Unit Cost | Very Low (1–5) | Low (5–20) | Low (2–10) | Medium (10–500) | Very Low (0.5–5 at high volume) | Very Low |
| Performance (MHz) | Low–Medium (10–500) | Medium (100–1000) | Low–Medium (50–200) | Medium–High (100–500) | Very High (500–3000+) | Very High |
| Power Consumption | Low (sleep-friendly) | Medium | Very Low | Medium–High | Very Low | Very Low |
| Flexibility | Infinite (software) | High (software) | Medium (reprogrammable) | Very High (in-field) | None (post-silicon) | None |
| Time-to-Market | Very Short | Short | Short | Short–Medium | Very Long (12–24 mo) | Long (vendor-dependent) |
| Best Volume Range | 1 – ∞ | 1K – 1M | 100 – 100K | 10 – 100K | > 1M | > 10M |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table correctly:** A horizontal scan tells you the "best metric" for each option; a vertical scan tells you the relative ranking for each metric. This dual-axis view is what examiners reward.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Volume vs. NRE Break-Even Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following three equations are the **core mathematical tools** of this topic. Every Part-B question in KTU examinations on this module revolves around one of them.

**Equation 1 — Total Cost of an FPGA-based Design:**

$$C_{total}^{FPGA} = C_{NRE}^{FPGA} + V \cdot C_{unit}^{FPGA}$$

where $V$ is the production volume and $C_{unit}^{FPGA}$ is the per-unit FPGA cost.

**Equation 2 — Total Cost of an ASIC-based Design:**

$$C_{total}^{ASIC} = C_{NRE}^{ASIC} + V \cdot C_{unit}^{ASIC}$$

**Equation 3 — Break-Even Volume $V_{BE}$:**

The volume at which the total cost of an FPGA solution equals the total cost of an ASIC solution.

$$C_{NRE}^{FPGA} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{unit}^{FPGA} = C_{NRE}^{ASIC} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{unit}^{ASIC}$$

Solving for $V_{BE}$:

$$V_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE}^{ASIC} - C_{NRE}^{FPGA}}{C_{unit}^{FPGA} - C_{unit}^{ASIC}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often write $C_{unit}^{FPGA} - C_{unit}^{ASIC}$ in the denominator. Since $C_{unit}^{FPGA} > C_{unit}^{ASIC}$ at the same volume, this yields a *positive* denominator. Reversing it gives a *negative* volume — physically meaningless. Always cross-check the sign before submitting.

**Equation 4 — Total Cost including Mask Set:**

$$C_{total}^{ASIC} = C_{mask} + C_{design} + C_{test} + C_{pkg} + V \cdot (C_{wafer} \cdot \frac{A_{die}}{A_{reticle} \cdot Y})$$

where $Y$ is the manufacturing yield.

**Equation 5 — Manufacturing Yield (Bose-Einstein / Poisson model):**

$$Y = e^{-D \cdot A_{die}}$$

where $D$ is the defect density (defects/cm²) and $A_{die}$ is the die area in cm².

**Equation 6 — Dynamic Power (CMOS):**

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor, $C_{L}$ is the load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ is the supply voltage, and $f$ is the clock frequency.

**Equation 7 — Static Power (Leakage):**

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Why FPGA Loses the Power Battle:**
> The programmable interconnect in an FPGA contributes **>60% of total dynamic power** because every wire is driven by a pass-transistor or multiplexer. In an ASIC, wires are dedicated metal traces with no switching overhead, yielding the $V_{DD}^{2}$ power advantage captured in Equation 6.

## 2.4 The Time-to-Market Economic Argument

In commercial product design, time-to-market has a measurable dollar value. A product delayed by $T$ months loses revenue equal to:

$$\Delta Revenue = T \cdot R_{monthly} \cdot M$$

where $R_{monthly}$ is the monthly revenue rate and $M$ is the market-share erosion factor (typically 0.1–0.3 per month delay in consumer electronics). FPGAs win decisively in this metric because they eliminate the 3–6 month mask fabrication lead time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Break-Even Volume $V_{BE}$

This is the most frequently asked 14-mark derivation in KTU 2024 university exams. We derive the formula from first principles, expanding every algebraic transition.

**Step 1 — Statement of the problem (textual logic):**

> A system designer must choose between an FPGA-based prototype-turned-production solution and an ASIC-based volume-production solution. The FPGA solution has a per-unit cost of $\vert C_{u,F} \vert$ and a one-time NRE cost of $\vert C_{NRE,F} \vert$. The ASIC solution has a per-unit cost of $\vert C_{u,A} \vert$ and a one-time NRE cost of $\vert C_{NRE,A} \vert$ (where $C_{NRE,A} \gg C_{NRE,F}$). The total cost of each solution as a function of production volume $V$ is the sum of NRE plus the volume times per-unit cost. We seek the volume $V_{BE}$ where the two total-cost lines intersect.

**Step 2 — Formulate the total-cost equations:**

Total cost using FPGA:

$$C_{T,F}(V) = C_{NRE,F} + V \cdot C_{u,F}$$

Total cost using ASIC:

$$C_{T,A}(V) = C_{NRE,A} + V \cdot C_{u,A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the break-even condition:**

By definition, at the break-even point the two total costs are equal:

$$C_{NRE,F} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{u,F} = C_{NRE,A} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{u,A}$$

**Step 4 — Group terms containing $V_{BE}$ on one side:**

Move the volume terms to the left-hand side and the NRE terms to the right-hand side:

$$V_{BE} \cdot C_{u,F} - V_{BE} \cdot C_{u,A} = C_{NRE,A} - C_{NRE,F}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $V_{BE}$ on the left-hand side:**

$$V_{BE} \cdot (C_{u,F} - C_{u,A}) = C_{NRE,A} - C_{NRE,F}$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by the per-unit cost difference:**

$$V_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE,A} - C_{NRE,F}}{C_{u,F} - C_{u,A}}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the sign of the denominator:**

Because FPGA per-unit cost is always greater than ASIC per-unit cost in equivalent technology nodes, $C_{u,F} - C_{u,A} > 0$. The numerator $C_{NRE,A} - C_{NRE,F}$ is also positive. Hence $V_{BE}$ is a **positive integer quantity** — physically meaningful as a production volume.

**Step 8 — Numerical Worked Example (typical KTU problem):**

> A startup designs a video-processing system. An FPGA solution costs **USD 1,000 per unit** with negligible NRE (USD 5,000 for design tools). The ASIC solution requires a **USD 2,000,000 NRE** (mask + design), with a per-unit cost of **USD 8**. Find the break-even volume.

Substituting:

$$V_{BE} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 - 5{,}000}{1{,}000 - 8} = \frac{1{,}995{,}000}{992} \approx 2{,}010 \text{ units}$$

**Step 9 — Engineering interpretation:**

Below ~2,010 units, the FPGA solution is cheaper overall. Above ~2,010 units, the ASIC solution dominates. This is why FPGAs are the default choice for production volumes under ~10K units.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Total Die Cost Per Wafer

**Step 1 — Wafer-level cost model:**

The cost of a processed silicon wafer (before dicing) is $C_{wafer}$. A single wafer of diameter $D_{wafer}$ (in cm) contains a number of dies given by:

$$N_{dies\_per\_wafer} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D_{wafer}/2)^2}{A_{die}} - \frac{\pi \cdot D_{wafer}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot A_{die}}}$$

The second term is a perimeter correction accounting for the curved wafer edge. For typical 300mm wafers and small dies, the first term dominates.

**Step 2 — Apply the yield model:**

The fraction of dies that are functional after manufacturing is the yield $Y$. The effective number of *good* dies per wafer is:

$$N_{good} = N_{dies\_per\_wafer} \cdot Y$$

**Step 3 — Die cost equation:**

$$C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{good}} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{dies\_per\_wafer} \cdot Y}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in the exponential yield model:**

$$C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{dies\_per\_wafer}} \cdot e^{D \cdot A_{die}}$$

**Step 5 — Physical insight:**

As $A_{die}$ increases, the exponential term grows, causing $C_{die}$ to grow super-linearly. This is why **smaller dies are always cheaper per unit** (subject to the wafer-area constraint) and why *designers are motivated to shrink logic* in high-volume ASICs.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Option

| Application Domain | Recommended Option | Justification (KTU-style reasoning) |
|---|---|---|
| Washing machine controller | 8-bit MCU (e.g., 8051) | Low NRE, low cost, control-dominated task, infinite software flexibility |
| High-throughput 100G Ethernet switch | ASIC (Network Processor) | Extreme throughput, power-critical, high volume (datacenters) |
| Aerospace flight computer | FPGA (rad-hard) | In-field re-programmability, radiation tolerance, mid-volume |
| Smartphone touchscreen controller | ASSP | Mass-produced, low per-unit cost, pre-verified |
| Wireless baseband signal chain | DSP (or SoC FPGA) | MAC-intensive, deterministic latency |
| Boot address decoder on a SoC | CPLD | Non-volatile, deterministic timing, low density |
| ASIC prototyping / emulation | FPGA (large, e.g., Virtex UltraScale) | Closest silicon behavior, fast iteration |
| IoT sensor node | MCU (Cortex-M0+) | Ultra-low power, low cost, software-defined |
| AI inference accelerator (100 TOPS) | ASIC (e.g., Google's TPU) | Massive parallelism, power efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to *justify* a choice, always mention **at least three** of the seven metrics: cost, performance, power, flexibility, time-to-market, volume, and NRE. Examiners allocate ~2 marks per justified metric.

## 3.4 The "Rule of Thumb" Decision Tree (Quantitative Triggers)

1. **Volume < 1,000 units** → **FPGA** is almost always the correct choice.
2. **Volume 1,000 – 100,000 units** → **FPGA vs Structured ASIC** trade-off (e.g., eFPGA, gate-array ASICs).
3. **Volume > 100,000 units AND performance is critical** → **Full ASIC**.
4. **Volume > 1,000,000 units AND cost is paramount** → **ASSP** (if available off-the-shelf) or **ASIC**.
5. **Algorithm is evolving / in-field updates required** → **FPGA or MCU**, *never* ASIC.
6. **Time-to-Market < 6 months** → **FPGA or MCU**, *avoid* ASIC.
7. **Power budget < 100 mW AND high performance needed** → **ASIC** (FPGA static power rules it out).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Digital System Design Option Decision Tree

The following Mermaid block renders a hierarchical decision tree that maps the seven design metrics to the recommended implementation platform. The flow is intentionally *graph-based* (not flowchart-based) to highlight the multi-criteria nature of the decision.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNodeA["START Digital System Specification"]
    metricNode1["NRE Budget Available"]
    metricNode2["Production Volume Target"]
    metricNode3["Performance Required MHz"]
    metricNode4["Power Budget mW"]
    metricNode5["Time to Market Months"]
    metricNode6["In Field Reprogrammability Needed"]

    optNodeMCU["Option 1 MCU or MPU"]
    optNodeDSP["Option 2 DSP Processor"]
    optNodeCPLD["Option 3 CPLD Glue Logic"]
    optNodeFPGA["Option 4 FPGA SoC"]
    optNodeASIC["Option 5 Full Custom ASIC"]
    optNodeASSP["Option 6 ASSP Off The Shelf"]

    rootNodeA --> metricNode1
    rootNodeA --> metricNode2
    rootNodeA --> metricNode3
    rootNodeA --> metricNode4
    rootNodeA --> metricNode5
    rootNodeA --> metricNode6

    metricNode1 -->|"NRE under 50K"| optNodeMCU
    metricNode1 -->|"NRE under 100K"| optNodeFPGA
    metricNode1 -->|"NRE over 5M"| optNodeASIC

    metricNode2 -->|"Volume under 1K"| optNodeFPGA
    metricNode2 -->|"Volume 1K to 100K"| optNodeFPGA
    metricNode2 -->|"Volume over 1M"| optNodeASIC

    metricNode3 -->|"under 100 MHz"| optNodeMCU
    metricNode3 -->|"100 to 500 MHz"| optNodeFPGA
    metricNode3 -->|"over 1 GHz"| optNodeASIC

    metricNode4 -->|"under 10 mW"| optNodeMCU
    metricNode4 -->|"10 to 500 mW"| optNodeFPGA
    metricNode4 -->|"Power Critical"| optNodeASIC

    metricNode5 -->|"under 3 months"| optNodeFPGA
    metricNode5 -->|"over 12 months allowed"| optNodeASIC

    metricNode6 -->|"YES Reprogram"| optNodeFPGA
    metricNode6 -->|"NO Fixed Function"| optNodeASIC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam answer:**
> The diagram is a *qualitative* aid. The arrows represent "if this metric falls in this range, the corresponding option is a strong candidate." In a written answer, you would select the *intersection* of all metric branches to arrive at the optimal platform.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Total Cost vs. Volume Tradeoff Curve

The following diagram visualizes the break-even concept graphically. Note that node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (per the Mermaid safeguard protocol).

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisOriginA["Origin Zero Volume Zero Cost"]
    curveFPGALabel["FPGA Total Cost Line Steep Slope"]
    curveASICLabel["ASIC Total Cost Line Shallow Slope"]
    pointBE["Break Even Point VBE Intersection"]
    regionA["Region 1 FPGA Wins"]
    regionB["Region 2 ASIC Wins"]

    axisOriginA --> curveFPGALabel
    axisOriginA --> curveASICLabel
    curveFPGALabel --- pointBE
    curveASICLabel --- pointBE
    pointBE --- regionA
    pointBE --- regionB
```

**Interpretation in plain words:**

- The **FPGA line** starts at a low NRE intercept on the y-axis but rises steeply (high per-unit cost slope).
- The **ASIC line** starts at a very high NRE intercept but rises slowly (low per-unit cost slope).
- The two lines **cross** at $V_{BE}$. To the left of the crossing, FPGA is cheaper. To the right, ASIC wins.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — FPGA Internal Fabric

To provide an intuitive schematic of the *target* device family of this course, the following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal functional architecture of a typical modern FPGA.

```mermaid
graph TB
    fpgaSubSysA["FPGA Chip Boundary"]
    clbArrayNode["CLB Array Configurable Logic Blocks"]
    dspSliceNode["DSP48 Slices Hardware MAC"]
    bramNode["Block RAM Dual Port Memory"]
    ioBlockNode["IOB I O Pins and PHY"]
    clockNode["CMT Clock Management Tile PLL and MMCM"]
    transNode["GTX GTH Transceivers Multi Gbps"]
    pcieNode["Hard PCIe Gen3 Endpoint"]
    procCoreNode["Hard ARM Cortex A9 or R5 Core"]
    interconnectNode["Programmable Interconnect Matrix"]
    configNode["Configuration Logic SRAM or Flash Controller"]

    fpgaSubSysA --> clbArrayNode
    fpgaSubSysA --> dspSliceNode
    fpgaSubSysA --> bramNode
    fpgaSubSysA --> ioBlockNode
    fpgaSubSysA --> clockNode
    fpgaSubSysA --> transNode
    fpgaSubSysA --> pcieNode
    fpgaSubSysA --> procCoreNode
    clbArrayNode --> interconnectNode
    dspSliceNode --> interconnectNode
    bramNode --> interconnectNode
    ioBlockNode --> interconnectNode
    configNode --> clbArrayNode
    configNode --> interconnectNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU Module 1:** The internal composition of an FPGA (CLBs, DSP slices, BRAM, hard IP) is what makes FPGAs competitive with ASICs in *modern* designs. The "tradeoff" of using FPGAs has narrowed significantly because vendors embed ASIC-grade hard-IP blocks that bypass the programmable interconnect overhead for common functions (DSP, memory, transceivers).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — The FPGA Design Flow Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    specNodeA["1 System Specification"]
    rtlNode["2 RTL Design VHDL or Verilog"]
    synthNode["3 Logic Synthesis"]
    placeRouteNode["4 Place and Route"]
    bitgenNode["5 Bitstream Generation"]
    programNode["6 Device Programming"]
    verifyNodeA["7 Functional Verification"]
    timingNode["8 Static Timing Analysis"]
    boardNode["9 Board Level Integration"]

    specNodeA --> rtlNode
    rtlNode --> synthNode
    synthNode --> placeRouteNode
    placeRouteNode --> bitgenNode
    bitgenNode --> programNode
    programNode --> verifyNodeA
    verifyNodeA --> timingNode
    timingNode --> boardNode
    boardNode -->|"Bug Found"| rtlNode
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on FPGA Re-spin Economics:** The feedback loop in the bottom of the diagram (Board → RTL) represents the ability to *fix a design bug in hours* on an FPGA, versus a multi-million-dollar re-spin on an ASIC. This single fact is the strongest economic argument for FPGA adoption in prototyping and low-volume production.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> **Define the term "Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) cost" in the context of digital system design. Explain why NRE cost is a critical factor when comparing ASIC and FPGA design options.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **NRE Cost Definition (1 mark):**
> NRE cost refers to the **one-time, non-recoverable expense** incurred during the design and tooling phase of a digital IC, *independent of the production volume*. It includes the cost of **photolithographic mask sets, EDA tool licenses, design verification, prototyping, and qualification testing**.
>
> **Why NRE is critical in ASIC vs FPGA comparison (2 marks):**
> ASIC designs incur a very high NRE (typically **USD 10M to USD 500M** at advanced nodes) due to the mask sets and extensive verification. FPGA designs have a comparatively negligible NRE (often < **USD 100K** for tools and IP licensing) because no masks are required. Consequently, the NRE is a *pivotal* parameter in the **break-even volume analysis** that determines whether an ASIC or an FPGA is economically viable for a given production volume.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> **Differentiate between an ASIC and an ASSP. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | ASIC | ASSP |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Focus** (1 mark) | Custom-designed for a *single* customer (e.g., Apple, Qualcomm) | Designed for a *function* and sold to many customers as a commodity |
| **Example** (1 mark) | Apple A17 Pro SoC (used in iPhone 15 Pro) | Broadcom BCM4360 Wi-Fi 5 chipset (used in routers, smartphones, laptops) |
| **Fabrication** (1 mark) | Mask-based, identical process | Mask-based, identical process |

The architectural/silicon realization is identical; the difference lies in the **business model and target audience**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]**

### (a) Explain the seven key design metrics used to evaluate digital system implementation options. Compare the relative strengths and weaknesses of FPGA and ASIC implementations across these metrics. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**The Seven Design Metrics (3.5 marks — 0.5 marks each):**

1. **NRE Cost** — One-time design/tooling cost.
2. **Per-Unit Cost** — Cost of one fabricated, packaged, tested chip.
3. **Performance** — Maximum clock frequency, throughput, latency.
4. **Power Consumption** — Static (leakage) + dynamic (switching) power.
5. **Flexibility** — Ability to modify functionality post-fabrication.
6. **Time-to-Market** — Time from specification to production-ready chip.
7. **Production Volume** — Total units produced over the product lifetime.

**FPGA vs ASIC Comparison (3.5 marks — 0.5 marks per metric):**

| Metric | FPGA | ASIC | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| NRE Cost | Low (<USD 100K) | Very High (USD 10M–500M) | **FPGA** |
| Per-Unit Cost | High (USD 10–500) | Very Low (USD 0.5–5) at volume | **ASIC** |
| Performance | Medium (100–500 MHz) | Very High (500 MHz–3 GHz) | **ASIC** |
| Power | Medium–High | Very Low | **ASIC** |
| Flexibility | Infinite (in-field) | Zero | **FPGA** |
| Time-to-Market | Short (weeks) | Long (12–24 months) | **FPGA** |
| Volume Suitability | 10 – 100K | > 1M | **Tie / context-dependent** |

**Conclusion:** FPGAs are optimal for low-to-medium volume, high-flexibility, short-time-to-market applications. ASICs are optimal for high-volume, performance-critical, power-sensitive applications.

### (b) A digital system design firm is evaluating two implementation options: an FPGA-based solution and an ASIC-based solution. The FPGA has a per-unit cost of **USD 150** and an NRE of **USD 20,000**. The ASIC has a per-unit cost of **USD 12** and an NRE of **USD 1,500,000**. (i) Derive the break-even volume formula. (ii) Compute the break-even volume. (iii) Comment on the design decision for a target volume of 50,000 units. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Derivation of $V_{BE}$ (3 marks):**

Equating total costs:

$$C_{NRE,F} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{u,F} = C_{NRE,A} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{u,A}$$

Rearranging:

$$V_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE,A} - C_{NRE,F}}{C_{u,F} - C_{u,A}}$$

**(ii) Computation (2 marks):**

$$V_{BE} = \frac{1{,}500{,}000 - 20{,}000}{150 - 12} = \frac{1{,}480{,}000}{138} \approx 10{,}725 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) Design Decision (2 marks):**

The target volume of 50,000 units is **far above** the break-even volume of 10,725 units. Therefore, the **ASIC solution is the economically superior choice** for this production run. The total cost saving of the ASIC over FPGA at 50,000 units is:

$$\Delta C = (C_{T,F} - C_{T,A}) \big\vert_{V=50{,}000} = (20{,}000 + 50{,}000 \cdot 150) - (1{,}500{,}000 + 50{,}000 \cdot 12) = 7{,}520{,}000 - 2{,}100{,}000 = 5{,}420{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> *Common mistake 1:* Students often confuse the break-even *formula* with the *per-unit cost equation*. Always state the break-even condition (total cost equality) *before* substituting numbers.
> *Common mistake 2:* Students forget to include the *units* in the final answer. Always write "10,725 units" not "10,725."
> *Common mistake 3:* Forgetting to **comment** on the design decision in part (b)(iii). The numerical answer alone is worth only 1 mark; the engineering *interpretation* is worth the remaining 1 mark.

---

> **Question B. [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Pattern, OR Option]**

### (a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a modern FPGA. List and briefly describe the function of at least five hard-IP blocks embedded in a contemporary FPGA. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block-level architecture diagram in **Section 4.3** of these notes. The student must clearly identify:
- The CLB array as the central logic fabric.
- The Programmable Interconnect Matrix routing signals.
- The configuration logic controlling the SRAM/Flash bits.

**Five Hard-IP Blocks (4 marks — 0.8 marks each):**

1. **DSP48 Slices** — Hardware Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) units optimized for high-speed digital signal processing; typically deliver > 1 TMAC/s per slice.
2. **Block RAM (BRAM)** — Dual-port on-chip memory (18 Kb or 36 Kb per block) used for data buffering, FIFOs, and register files.
3. **Clock Management Tiles (CMT)** — PLLs and MMCMs that generate, deskew, and phase-shift clocks for the design.
4. **Multi-Gigabit Transceivers (GTX/GTH/GTY)** — Serializers/deserializers (SerDes) operating at 1 Gbps to 28+ Gbps for PCIe, SATA, Ethernet, and backplane links.
5. **Hard Processor Cores (e.g., ARM Cortex-A9, R5)** — Embedded CPUs that turn the FPGA into a System-on-Chip (SoC FPGA), enabling software/hardware co-design.
6. **PCIe Hard IP** — Pre-verified PCI Express endpoint/root-port blocks for high-speed host connectivity.
7. **Configuration Controller (e.g., SPI, BPI, JTAG)** — Manages bitstream loading from external Flash at power-up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Student Tip:** The block diagram should be *neat, labeled, and show the interconnect structure*. A diagram without clear arrows showing how CLBs, DSPs, and BRAMs connect to the central interconnect matrix loses 1–2 marks.

### (b) Discuss the seven design tradeoffs a VLSI engineer must consider when choosing between a microcontroller (MCU), DSP, CPLD, FPGA, ASIC, and ASSP for a given application. Use a comparison table to justify your answer with at least one real-world example per option. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Framework — The Seven Tradeoff Metrics (2 marks):** Same seven metrics from Question A part (a) — NRE, per-unit cost, performance, power, flexibility, time-to-market, volume.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):** Refer to the master comparison table in **Section 2.2** of these notes. The student must reproduce (or paraphrase) the seven-metric × six-option matrix.

**Real-World Examples (2 marks — 0.33 marks per example):**

| Option | Real-World Example | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| MCU | STM32F4 in a drone flight controller | Control-dominated, software-flexible, low cost |
| DSP | Texas Instruments TMS320C6000 in an audio equalizer | MAC-intensive, deterministic latency |
| CPLD | Xilinx CoolRunner-II in a memory address decoder | Glue logic, non-volatile, deterministic |
| FPGA | Xilinx Virtex UltraScale+ in a 5G baseband prototype | High throughput, in-field re-programmable, short TTM |
| ASIC | Apple A17 Pro in iPhone 15 Pro | Extreme performance, high volume, low power |
| ASSP | Realtek RTL8111H Gigabit Ethernet controller | Off-the-shelf, mass-produced, low cost |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** The engineer's choice is a *multi-criteria optimization* problem, not a single-metric decision. A balanced weighting of all seven metrics — driven by the application's economic and technical constraints — yields the optimal design option.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> *Common mistake 1:* Drawing a block diagram of an FPGA that looks like a CPU datapath. Examiners expect to see the **CLB array, programmable interconnect, configuration logic, and hard-IP blocks** as distinct elements.
> *Common mistake 2:* Providing examples without *justification* (the "why this option fits this example" sentence). Marks are awarded for the reasoning, not the example.
> *Common mistake 3:* Missing the **synthesis line** at the end. The examiner rewards the *concluding remark* that ties all metrics together.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1):**

- The **six canonical design options** are MPU/MCU, DSP, CPLD, FPGA, ASIC, and ASSP. Memorize the order from most flexible to least flexible.

- The **seven design metrics** are NRE, per-unit cost, performance, power, flexibility, time-to-market, and volume. Every Part-B question in this module will test at least four of these.

- The **break-even volume** formula is $V_{BE} = (C_{NRE,A} - C_{NRE,F}) / (C_{u,F} - C_{u,A})$. This is the single most important equation in Module 1.

- The **denominator** of $V_{BE}$ is always positive because FPGA per-unit cost is always greater than ASIC per-unit cost in the same technology node. Verify the sign.

- **ASICs win** at high volume (>1M units) and when performance/power are critical. **FPGAs win** at low-to-medium volume and when flexibility/TTM are critical. **MCUs win** for control-dominated tasks with low NRE. **DSPs win** for MAC-intensive signal processing. **CPLDs win** for non-volatile glue logic. **ASSPs win** for commodity functions at very high volume.

- The **manufacturing yield** $Y = e^{-D \cdot A_{die}}$ shows why smaller dies are cheaper per unit. This is the fundamental economic driver behind "die shrink" in ASIC design.

- The **dynamic power** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ is the dominant power term in CMOS designs. The $V_{DD}^{2}$ dependence is why *voltage scaling* is the single most effective power-reduction technique.

- **FPGA = infinite in-field re-programmability.** **ASIC = zero post-silicon flexibility.** This single fact defines the entire Module 1 tradeoff landscape.

- **Time-to-Market** has a measurable dollar cost: $\Delta Revenue = T \cdot R_{monthly} \cdot M$. FPGAs win decisively here.

- The **internal architecture of a modern FPGA** consists of: CLB array, programmable interconnect, DSP slices, Block RAM, IOBs, CMT (PLL/MMCM), multi-gigabit transceivers, hard processor cores, and PCIe endpoints.

- **Memorize the three configuration technologies** of FPGAs: **SRAM-based** (volatile, requires boot Flash, leading-edge process), **Flash-based** (non-volatile, low power, mid-density), and **Anti-fuse-based** (one-time programmable, radiation tolerant, used in aerospace).

- **The FPGA-to-ASIC migration decision** is driven by the break-even volume. As a rule of thumb, $V_{BE}$ typically falls in the **10K–100K unit range** for most commercial applications.

- The **"Why FPGA Prototyping"** argument: ASIC emulation on FPGAs reduces respin risk from millions of dollars to thousands of dollars per iteration cycle.

- **Exam mnemonic — "C-F-P-P-T-V-F"** (Cost-NRE, Flexibility, Performance, Power, Time-to-Market, Volume) is a quick way to recall the seven design metrics in any answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Methodology and Technology Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Methodology and Technology Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Methodology** in the context of FPGA-based system design refers to the structured, hierarchical, and systematic procedure followed to translate a functional specification into a working, bitstream-programmable hardware realization on a Field Programmable Gate Array. It encompasses a top-down decomposition strategy, HDL-based entry, logic synthesis, technology mapping, place-and-route, and timing verification.

**Technology Overview** refers to the comparative study of the families of programmable logic devices — SPLD, CPLD, and FPGA — and their underlying silicon architectures (SRAM, Antifuse, Flash, LUT-based, etc.) that govern the design choices.

The two pillars of FPGA design are:
1. **Methodology** — *the "how" of design (process).*
2. **Technology** — *the "where" of design (target silicon fabric).*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture (House Construction Analogy):**
> Imagine building a house. You don't start by laying bricks randomly.
> - **Design Methodology** = The architectural blueprint + step-by-step process (foundation → walls → roof → wiring → inspection).
> - **Technology Overview** = The choice of building material (bricks, wood, steel) — each gives different strength, cost, and flexibility.
>
> Similarly, in FPGA design:
> - **Methodology** = the engineering workflow from idea to bitstream.
> - **Technology** = the underlying silicon fabric (LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP slices, interconnect).
>
> A robust methodology on weak technology wastes effort; strong technology with poor methodology produces non-functional silicon.

## 1.3 Key Metrics in FPGA Design

The following metrics govern any design decision:

- **Logic Density** — measured in **LUTs (Look-Up Tables)** and **gate equivalents** (a typical modern FPGA = **~1 M to 10 M+ system logic cells**).
- **Clock Frequency ($f_{CLK}$)** — measured in **MHz / GHz**.
- **Power Dissipation ($P$)** — static + dynamic, in **mW / W**.
- **Time-to-Market** — typical FPGA advantage: **weeks vs. 12–18 months for ASIC**.
- **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) Cost** — ~**\$0–\$5,000** for FPGA vs. **\$0.5 M – \$50 M** for ASIC.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must be able to *contrast* ASIC and FPGA design flows — this is a guaranteed 3-mark or 7-mark question in ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Design Methodology (Top-Down Approach)

The FPGA design methodology is inherently **top-down** for system-level designs and **bottom-up** for IP/library development. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **top-down** paradigm.

**Step-by-step logic of Top-Down Design:**

- **Step 1 — System Specification:** Define the I/O, throughput, latency, area, and power budgets. Output: Design Requirement Document.
- **Step 2 — Behavioral Modeling:** Write HDL (VHDL/Verilog) at Register-Transfer Level (RTL). Focus on *what* the system does, not *how*.
- **Step 3 — Functional Simulation:** Verify algorithmic correctness *before* synthesis (no timing yet).
- **Step 4 — Logic Synthesis:** Convert RTL → generic gate-level netlist (technology-independent).
- **Step 5 — Technology Mapping:** Map generic gates onto FPGA primitives (LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP).
- **Step 6 — Place and Route (P&R):** Assign physical locations to logic cells and route interconnects. This is where **timing is realized**.
- **Step 7 — Static Timing Analysis (STA):** Verify setup/hold across all timing paths.
- **Step 8 — Bitstream Generation:** Translate placed-routed design into a configuration bitstream.
- **Step 9 — Device Programming & On-Board Verification:** Load bitstream into SRAM/Flash FPGA.

## 2.2 Comparison of Design Strategies

| Strategy | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Top-Down** | System → Subsystems → Modules → Gates | System-level FPGA design (default) |
| **Bottom-Up** | Gates → Modules → Subsystems → System | Library/IP development, cell design |
| **Meet-in-the-Middle** | Combined: bottom-up cell library + top-down system | Most commercial FPGA flows (Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{clk}$ | $\dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Clock period | ns |
| $T_{su}$ | Setup time of FF | Min. data stable before clk edge | ns |
| $T_{hold}$ | Hold time of FF | Min. data stable after clk edge | ns |
| $T_{co}$ | Clock-to-output delay | FF propagation delay | ns |
| $T_{comb}$ | Combinational delay | LUT + routing delay along path | ns |
| Slack$_{\text{setup}}$ | $T_{clk} - (T_{su} + T_{comb} + T_{co})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ for setup met | ns |
| Slack$_{\text{hold}}$ | $T_{co} + T_{comb} - T_{hold}$ | Must be $\geq 0$ for hold met | ns |
| $P_{total}$ | $P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$ | Total power dissipation | W |
| $P_{dynamic}$ | $\alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$ | Switching power | W |
| Logic Density | $\Sigma \text{LUTs} + \text{FFs} + \text{BRAM} + \text{DSP}$ | FPGA resource utilization | cells |
| NRE Cost (ASIC) | $C_{mask} + C_{design} + C_{test}$ | Non-recurring engineering | USD |
| Unit Cost (FPGA) | $C_{chip} + C_{PCB} + C_{prog}$ | Per-unit recurring cost | USD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The setup/hold slack equations are the **backbone of Static Timing Analysis (STA)** in Vivado/Quartus. A negative setup slack causes **metastability** — a real cause of system failure in safety-critical domains like automotive (ISO 26262) and aerospace (DO-254).

## 2.4 ASIC vs FPGA: The Core Trade-off

| Parameter | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| **NRE Cost** | **Very High** ($\$$ millions) | **Zero / Negligible** |
| **Per-Unit Cost (volume)** | Very Low | Moderate-High |
| **Time-to-Market** | 12–18 months | 2–8 weeks |
| **Performance ($f_{max}$)** | Highest (custom layout) | Limited by fabric |
| **Power** | Optimized | Higher (programmability overhead) |
| **Flexibility** | Fixed (no field update) | Field-upgradable |
| **Volume Break-Even** | $\geq 1$ M units | $< 100$k units typical |
| **Design Risk** | High (respin cost) | Low (just reprogram) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Industry Standard):** Use **FPGA** for prototyping, low-volume, and re-configurable systems. Use **ASIC** for high-volume, power-critical, performance-critical products (e.g., smartphone SoCs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Setup Slack Equation

The setup time constraint is the **single most important timing check** in FPGA design. We derive it from first principles.

**Given:** A flip-flop (FF) launches data on one clock edge, and the data propagates through combinational logic and interconnect to a capturing FF on the next clock edge.

**Step 1 — Identify the launch event.**
At time $t = 0$, the launching FF toggles its output after the clock-to-output delay:
$$
t_{launch} = T_{co}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagate through combinational + routing path.**
The data traverses the combinational logic and the FPGA routing fabric:
$$
t_{arrive\_at\_D} = T_{co} + T_{comb}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the capture event.**
The capturing FF samples the data on the next rising edge at time $T_{clk}$. However, the data must be **stable at the D input at least $T_{su}$ before the edge**:
$$
t_{required\_before\_edge} = T_{clk} - T_{su}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the slack.**
Slack is the difference between *when the data is required* and *when the data actually arrives*:
$$
\text{Slack}_{setup} = t_{required} - t_{arrive}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expressions:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slack}_{setup} &= (T_{clk} - T_{su}) - (T_{co} + T_{comb}) \\
\text{Slack}_{setup} &= T_{clk} - T_{su} - T_{co} - T_{comb} \\
\text{Slack}_{setup} &= T_{clk} - (T_{su} + T_{co} + T_{comb})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the setup constraint:**
For correct operation, the slack **must be non-negative**:
$$
\boxed{\;T_{clk} \geq T_{su} + T_{co} + T_{comb}\;}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Example (Worked Out)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4-bit ripple counter is implemented on a Xilinx Artix-7 FPGA. The launch FF has $T_{co} = 0.15$ ns. The combinational LUT + routing path is $T_{comb} = 2.35$ ns. The capture FF has $T_{su} = 0.20$ ns. Find the maximum safe operating frequency.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the minimum clock period:**
$$
T_{clk,\,min} = T_{su} + T_{co} + T_{comb}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,\,min} &= 0.20 + 0.15 + 2.35 \\
T_{clk,\,min} &= 2.70 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum frequency:**
$$
f_{clk,\,max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,\,min}} = \frac{1}{2.70 \times 10^{-9}} = 370.37 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{\;f_{max} \approx 370 \text{ MHz}\;}
$$
In practice, designers round down (e.g., choose 333 MHz or 300 MHz) to provide timing margin and account for PVT (Process, Voltage, Temperature) variation.

## 3.3 Derivation of Dynamic Power

Dynamic power in CMOS (and therefore in FPGA logic) arises from charging and discharging load capacitances.

**Step 1 — Energy per transition:**
Energy stored in a capacitor $C$ charged to $V_{dd}$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}CV_{dd}^{2}$. Half is dissipated in the PMOS, half in NMOS during discharge.
$$
E_{transition} = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{dd}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Power per node (with switching activity $\alpha$ and frequency $f$):**
$$
P_{node} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate over all $N$ nodes:**
$$
\boxed{\;P_{dynamic} = \alpha_{avg} C_{total} V_{dd}^{2} f\;}
$$

**Interpretation for FPGA:** $\alpha_{avg}$ depends on toggle rate of logic nodes — clock trees (high $\alpha$) dominate FPGA dynamic power.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| Design Goal | Prefer | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Lowest unit cost at high volume | **ASIC** | Mask cost amortized over millions of units |
| Fastest prototype | **FPGA** | No fab, no mask — bitstream in minutes |
| In-field reconfigurability | **SRAM FPGA** | Bitstream reloadable, partial reconfiguration supported |
| Lowest power | **ASIC** or **Flash FPGA** | No SRAM configuration cell leakage |
| High DSP throughput | **FPGA with DSP slices** | Dedicated hardened MAC blocks |
| Memory-heavy design | **FPGA with BRAM/URAM** | On-chip block RAM, no external DRAM access for small data |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 FPGA Design Flow — Block-Level Architecture

The diagram below captures the canonical FPGA design flow used by **Xilinx Vivado** and **Intel Quartus** as per KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification] --> B[HDL Design Entry RTL VHDL or Verilog]
    B --> C[Functional Simulation Behavioral]
    C --> D{Functionally Correct}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Logic Synthesis RTL to Gate Level Netlist]
    E --> F[Technology Mapping To FPGA Primitives LUT FF BRAM DSP]
    F --> G[Place and Route Physical Implementation]
    G --> H[Static Timing Analysis Setup and Hold Checks]
    H --> I{Timing Met}
    I -- No --> J[Constraints Editing or RTL Optimization]
    J --> E
    I -- Yes --> K[Bitstream Generation]
    K --> L[FPGA Programming via JTAG]
    L --> M[On Board Verification]
    M --> N{Meets Spec}
    N -- No --> A
    N -- Yes --> O[Production]
```

## 4.2 Programmable Logic Device Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Programmable Logic Devices PLD] --> B[SPLD PAL PLA GAL]
    A --> C[CPLD]
    A --> D[FPGA]
    B --> E[Simple PAL with AND OR arrays]
    C --> F[Multiple SPLD blocks with central interconnect]
    D --> G[Array of CLBs LUTs FFs connected by programmable interconnect]
    D --> H[Modern FPGAs with hard IP BRAM DSP PCIe]
```

## 4.3 ASIC vs FPGA — Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Product Concept] --> Q1{Volume greater than 500K units}
    Q1 -- No --> FPGA[Choose FPGA Fast NRE Low Risk]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Power and Performance Critical}
    Q2 -- No --> FPGA
    Q2 -- Yes --> ASIC[Choose ASIC High NRE Optimized Silicon]
    FPGA --> Bitstream[Generate Bitstream Program Device]
    ASIC --> Fab[Tapeout Fabrication Testing]
```

## 4.4 Generic FPGA Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IO[IOB IO Bank with LVCMOS LVDS etc] --> SW[Switch Matrix Programmable Interconnect]
    CLB[CLB Slice with LUT Flip Flop] --> SW
    BRAM[BRAM Block RAM 18Kb or 36Kb] --> SW
    DSP[DSP48 Slice MAC Accumulator] --> SW
    CMT[Clock Management Tile PLL MMCM] --> SW
    PCIE[Hard IP PCIe Gen3 Gen4] --> SW
    SW --> CFG[Configuration Logic SRAM or Flash]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exam:** Always label diagrams with **all major blocks**. A diagram with 3 boxes scores 2 marks; one with 7+ labeled blocks scores full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the major steps in the FPGA design methodology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per significant point):**

The FPGA design methodology consists of the following major steps:

1. **Specification:** Define functional and non-functional requirements (I/O, speed, area, power). (1 mark)
2. **Design Entry:** Capture the design at RTL using VHDL or Verilog HDL. (1 mark)
3. **Synthesis, Place & Route, and Verification:** Translate HDL into a netlist, map to FPGA primitives, place-and-route, run static timing analysis, and generate the bitstream for programming. (1 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Compare ASIC and FPGA technologies on the basis of (i) NRE cost, (ii) time-to-market, and (iii) flexibility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| (i) NRE cost | **Very high** (mask + fab, often > \$1 M) | **Negligible / zero** |
| (ii) Time-to-market | **12–18 months** (fabrication cycle) | **2–8 weeks** (bitstream only) |
| (iii) Flexibility | **Fixed post-fabrication**; no field updates | **Field-programmable**; can be reconfigured unlimited times |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the top-down design methodology for FPGA-based system design with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of HDL in the design flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between SPLD, CPLD, and FPGA architectures. Which is most suitable for high-density, high-performance applications and why? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Top-Down Design Methodology (5 marks):**
The top-down design methodology decomposes a complex system into manageable hierarchical sub-modules. The steps are:

- **Step 1 — System Specification:** Define I/O, clock domain, throughput, and power budget.
- **Step 2 — Architectural Partitioning:** Divide the system into data path, control path, memory, and I/O blocks.
- **Step 3 — RTL Design:** Each block is described at the Register-Transfer Level using HDL (VHDL/Verilog). This is technology-independent.
- **Step 4 — Functional Verification:** Pre-synthesis simulation checks algorithmic correctness.
- **Step 5 — Synthesis & Implementation:** Tools map RTL to LUTs, FFs, BRAM, and DSP on the FPGA.
- **Step 6 — Timing Verification & Bitstream:** STA confirms setup/hold, and a bitstream is generated.

**Role of HDL (2 marks):**
- Provides a **technology-independent** abstraction.
- Enables **simulation** before fabrication.
- Supports **hierarchical design** and **re-use** through IP libraries.
- Acts as the **universal input** to vendor synthesis tools (Vivado, Quartus).

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

| Feature | SPLD | CPLD | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density** | Low (a few hundred gates) | Medium (~10 K gates) | Very High (millions of gates) |
| **Architecture** | AND-OR plane + FF | Multiple SPLDs + central interconnect | Array of CLBs/LUTs/FFs + routing |
| **Logic Granularity** | Coarse (sum-of-products) | Medium | Fine (LUT-based) |
| **Non-Volatility** | Often fuse/UV-EPROM | Non-volatile (Flash/EEPROM) | Mostly volatile (SRAM) |
| **Speed** | Fast for combinational | Predictable, low-skew | High but routing-dependent |
| **Typical Use** | Glue logic, address decoding | State machines, control logic | DSP, SoC, parallel processing |

**Most suitable for high-density, high-performance: FPGA (1 mark)**
Reason: Modern FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx Versal, Intel Stratix 10) integrate **millions of LUTs, hardened DSP, BRAM, transceivers (up to 28 Gbps), and even ARM Cortex cores**, enabling **massive parallelism, high $f_{max}$, and heterogeneous computing** that SPLDs and CPLDs cannot match.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the internal architecture of a typical FPGA. Explain the function of (i) CLB/Slice, (ii) Programmable Interconnect, and (iii) BRAM. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flip-flop in an FPGA has $T_{co} = 0.12$ ns, $T_{su} = 0.18$ ns, and the combinational + routing delay is $T_{comb} = 3.5$ ns. Compute (i) the minimum clock period, and (ii) the maximum operating frequency. If the routing delay increases by 20%, what happens to the maximum frequency? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

A typical FPGA consists of the following key blocks (refer to architecture diagram in SECTION 4.4):

- **CLB / Slice (3 marks):** The **Configurable Logic Block** is the fundamental logic element. Each CLB contains one or more **slices**, and each slice houses multiple **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)** (typically 4-, 5-, or 6-input) and **flip-flops**. The LUT implements any Boolean function of $n$ inputs; the FF provides sequential storage.
- **Programmable Interconnect (2 marks):** A **hierarchical routing network** of switch matrices, horizontal/vertical routing channels, and PIPs (Programmable Interconnect Points). It connects CLBs, BRAM, and DSPs. **Routing delay** typically dominates $T_{comb}$.
- **BRAM (2 marks):** **Block RAM** is dedicated dual-port SRAM (typically 18 Kb or 36 Kb per block). It supports **single-clock, dual-clock, true dual-port, simple dual-port** modes, with optional **FIFO** controllers. Used for data buffers, register files, and memory-mapped interfaces.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $T_{co} = 0.12$ ns, $T_{su} = 0.18$ ns, $T_{comb} = 3.5$ ns.

**(i) Minimum clock period (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{clk,\,min} &= T_{su} + T_{co} + T_{comb} \\
&= 0.18 + 0.12 + 3.5 \\
&= 3.80 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum operating frequency (2 marks):**
$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{3.80 \times 10^{-9}} = 263.16 \text{ MHz}
$$

**(iii) Routing delay increases by 20% (2 marks):**
$$
T_{comb,\,new} = 3.5 \times 1.20 = 4.20 \text{ ns}
$$
$$
T_{clk,\,min,\,new} = 0.18 + 0.12 + 4.20 = 4.50 \text{ ns}
$$
$$
f_{max,\,new} = \frac{1}{4.50 \times 10^{-9}} = 222.22 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 20% increase in routing delay reduces $f_{max}$ by approximately **15.6%** (from 263 MHz to 222 MHz). This demonstrates the **dominance of routing** in FPGA timing closure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Confusing LUTs with logic gates:** A 6-input LUT implements **any** 6-variable Boolean function — students often wrongly equate 1 LUT = 1 gate.
> - **Ignoring the capture-edge offset:** Setup slack must be evaluated **one full clock period** after launch — many students compute the slack at the *same* edge, losing 2–3 marks.
> - **Writing "FPGA is faster than ASIC":** The opposite is generally true. FPGA is *faster to deploy*, not *faster in $f_{max}$*.
> - **Skipping units in timing answers:** Always write ns, MHz explicitly. KTU penalizes unit-less numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Design Methodology** = structured process: Specification → RTL → Synthesis → P&R → STA → Bitstream.
- **Top-down** is the industry-standard FPGA approach; **bottom-up** is used for IP/cell design.
- **HDLs (VHDL/Verilog)** are *technology-independent* descriptions; synthesis maps them onto FPGA primitives.
- **Three families of PLDs:** **SPLD** (low density), **CPLD** (medium, non-volatile), **FPGA** (very high, volatile SRAM-based, but re-programmable).
- **Modern FPGA = LUTs + FFs + BRAM + DSP + Transceivers + Hard IP cores** (e.g., ARM Cortex-A53 in Xilinx Zynq/ Versal).
- **Setup slack formula:** $\text{Slack} = T_{clk} - (T_{su} + T_{co} + T_{comb})$ — must be $\geq 0$.
- **Hold slack formula:** $\text{Slack} = (T_{co} + T_{comb}) - T_{hold}$ — must be $\geq 0$.
- **Dynamic power:** $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$ — proportional to $V_{dd}^2$, hence the industry shift to lower node voltages.
- **ASIC** = high NRE, low per-unit cost, high performance, low power, fixed.
- **FPGA** = zero NRE, moderate per-unit cost, moderate performance, higher power, **field-reprogrammable**.
- **Volume break-even** for ASIC vs FPGA is typically around **$10^5$–$10^6$ units**.
- **Time-to-market advantage** of FPGA is its single biggest commercial asset (weeks vs. months).
- **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** is performed **post-P&R**, not pre-synthesis.
- **Bitstream** is the final configuration data loaded into SRAM cells of the FPGA.
- **Configuration interfaces:** JTAG, SelectMap, SPI, BPI — recall at least **two** for viva.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Level System Architecture and Specification: Behavioral Modelling and Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Level System Architecture and Specification: Behavioral Modelling and Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 High-Level System Architecture

**High-Level System Architecture (HLSA)** is the abstract, technology-independent representation of a digital system's functionality, data flow, control flow, and modular composition before any commitment is made to a particular hardware description, logic family, or physical implementation. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, HLSA is the *first executable specification* of the system — the contract between the system engineer, the verification engineer, and the synthesis toolchain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Module-1):** "High-Level System Architecture is the unambiguous, formal specification of a digital system expressed in terms of its behavioural, structural, and physical characteristics across multiple domains (algorithmic, register-transfer, gate, transistor, and layout)."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing a **house** before construction begins.

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | FPGA Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Owner describes "4 BHK, garden-facing, south-light" | Verbal requirement gathering | **System Specification** |
| Architect draws 2D floor plan | Behaviour & structure of rooms | **Behavioral Model** |
| Structural engineer validates load paths | Modules & interconnections | **Structural Model** |
| Civil engineer pours concrete | Actual layout on silicon | **Physical Model** |

Just as the homeowner cannot start construction with just a verbal wish-list, an FPGA designer cannot synthesize a bitstream from a verbal idea. **Behavioral modelling** is the architectural drawing, and **simulation** is the architect's *walk-through* of the unbuilt house.

---

## 1.2 Behavioral Modelling

**Behavioral modelling** describes a digital system as a *set of algorithms, processes, and data transformations* — **without** specifying how the system is built from gates, flip-flops, or wires. It answers the question:

> "**What does the system DO?**" — not "**How is it built?**"

In Hardware Description Languages (HDLs) such as **VHDL** and **Verilog**, the behavioural view is expressed using:
- High-level procedural constructs (`process`, `always`, `initial`)
- Abstract data types (`integer`, `real`, `bit_vector`)
- Algorithmic statements (`if`, `case`, `for`, `while`)
- Timing controls (`#delay`, `wait for`, `@posedge`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Idea:** A behavioural model is a **simulation artefact** first, and a *potential* synthesis input second. Not every behavioural construct is synthesizable.

### Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Order Ticket

Imagine a waiter in a restaurant who takes an order:
- **Order (Input)**: "Paneer Butter Masala, 1 Naan, 1 Lassi"
- **Behaviour (Process)**: Kitchen follows the recipe (algorithm)
- **Order (Output)**: Food served on the table

The waiter does **not** know (or care) which chef cooked the dish, which stove was used, or how the gas pipeline runs. He only knows the *behaviour* (deliver food given an order). Behavioral modelling in HDL works the same way — it describes the I/O relationship algorithmically.

---

## 1.3 Simulation

**Simulation** is the **dynamic execution of a model** (behavioural, structural, or mixed) under a test-bench stimulus to verify functional correctness *before* any hardware is fabricated or programmed onto an FPGA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Simulation is the process of applying input vectors (stimulus) to a digital model over simulated time, observing the response, and comparing it against the expected golden output.

### Why Simulate?
1. **Cost** — A logic bug caught in simulation costs ₹0; a bug on a fabricated ASIC costs lakhs.
2. **Speed** — HDL simulators run at MHz to GHz effective rates vs. real-time MHz hardware.
3. **Visibility** — Every internal signal, register, and memory can be probed — something impossible in physical silicon without dedicated debug pins.
4. **Reusability** — The same test-bench can be re-run on gate-level netlist after synthesis for regression.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Flight Simulator

A **flight simulator** in aviation training is a perfect analogy:
- The **pilot's reaction** is the *stimulus* (test vectors)
- The **aircraft's response** on-screen is the *model under test (MUT)*
- The **instructor's expected checklist** is the *golden reference*
- The **simulator engine** is the *HDL simulator kernel*

A pilot does *not* need a real plane to learn emergency landing. A designer does *not* need real silicon to learn whether the ALU produces a correct sum.

---

## 1.4 High-Yield Vocabulary for KTU 2024

| Term | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Model** | A formal (HDL) representation of a digital system. |
| **Specification** | The English + formal contract of *what* the system must do. |
| **Behaviour** | The algorithmic / functional view (input → output mapping). |
| **Structure** | The interconnection of components (netlist view). |
| **Test-bench** | HDL module that instantiates the DUT, drives stimulus, and checks output. |
| **DUT** | Device Under Test — the design being verified. |
| **Stimulus** | The sequence of input values applied to the DUT. |
| **Event** | A change in a signal's value at a specific simulated time. |
| **Delta-cycle** | An infinitesimally small time-step used by the simulator to settle combinational glitches. |
| **Race Condition** | A behavioural ambiguity caused by *concurrent* updates to a signal from multiple processes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **delta-cycle** and **event-driven** concepts in 3-mark questions. Memorize the definitions above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart — The Heart of System Specification

The **Y-chart** (popularized by Gajski and Kuhn, 1983) is the single most important diagram in any digital-design or FPGA course. It organizes design into **three domains of abstraction** and **five levels of design detail**.

### The Three Domains (the arms of the Y)

1. **Behavioural Domain** — Algorithms, FSMs, data-flow graphs.
2. **Structural Domain** — Block diagrams, netlists, gate interconnections.
3. **Physical / Geometric Domain** — Floorplan, placement, routing, layout (GDS-II for ASIC; bitstream for FPGA).

### The Five Levels of Abstraction (the rings)

| Level | Behavioural | Structural | Physical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. System / Algorithmic** | C / MATLAB / untimed algorithms | Block diagram of CPUs, memories, buses | Floorplan of PCBs, boards |
| **2. Chip / RT (Register-Transfer)** | FSMs, datapath, micro-operations | ALU, Reg, Mux, Decoder blocks | Chip floorplan, partition |
| **3. Logic / Gate** | Boolean equations, K-maps | Standard-cell netlist | Cell placement, routing |
| **4. Circuit / Transistor** | Differential equations, switch logic | Transistor-level schematic | Transistor layout |
| **5. Layout / Mask** | Polygon-level simulation | Standard cells placed | Full GDS-II layout |

> [!NOTE]
> The Y-chart teaches the student: *as you walk from outer ring (system) toward the centre (layout), the level of detail increases and the abstraction decreases.*

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Behavioural Modelling

### Why Model Behaviourally?
- **Early validation** of architecture before commitment to gates.
- **Faster simulation** than gate-level (no gate delays, fewer signals).
- **Design exploration** — swap a pipelined vs. non-pipelined multiplier in hours.
- **Platform portability** — the same behavioural model can be retargeted to a new FPGA family.

### How is Behavioural Modelling Done?
The HDL provides three primary abstractions:
1. **Dataflow** — concurrent `assign` statements (continuous assignment).
2. **Behavioural / Procedural** — `process` (VHDL) or `always`/`initial` (Verilog) blocks.
3. **Structural** — instantiation of pre-designed components (the lower layers).

A typical behavioural model answers: *"Given input X at time t, what is output Y at time t+Δ?"* — without describing any hardware.

---

## 2.3 The Simulation Algorithm (Event-Driven)

Modern HDL simulators are **event-driven**. The core algorithm is:

1. **Elaborate** the design — expand all hierarchy, resolve parameters, build a *simulation model* in memory.
2. **Initialize** all signals to default values (`'0'`, `'1'`, `'X'`, `'Z'`).
3. **Schedule events** — when a process assigns a new value, an event is queued at the *current simulation time + delta-cycle*.
4. **Advance time** to the next scheduled event.
5. **Update signals** — all processes sensitive to changed signals are re-evaluated.
6. **Iterate** until no more events exist at the current time, then move to next time.
7. **Terminate** when the run-time ends or all processes suspend indefinitely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cycle of *evaluate → update → re-evaluate* within a single simulation timestamp is called a **delta-cycle**. It has **zero physical time** but allows the simulator to settle combinational logic.

### The Two-Process Model of a Test-Bench

A KTU-favourite test-bench follows a universal structure:

| Process / Block | Role |
|---|---|
| **Stimulus Generator** | Drives inputs to the DUT over time. |
| **DUT Instantiation** | The Device Under Test. |
| **Monitor / Checker** | Compares actual outputs against expected values and reports PASS/FAIL. |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Concept | Formal Expression / Statement | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Y-Chart Domain** | Behaviour ∩ Structure ∩ Physical = Design | Unified view of digital design abstraction. |
| 2 | **Delta-Cycle** | $\Delta t \to 0$, but $n_{\Delta} \geq 1$ | Resolves combinational races in zero physical time. |
| 3 | **Event Time** | $t_{\text{event}} = t_{\text{current}} + n_{\Delta} \cdot \Delta t$ | Scheduling formula for simulator. |
| 4 | **Simulation Speed** | $S \propto \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{signals}} \cdot f_{\text{events}}}$ | More events → slower simulation. |
| 5 | **Gate Count Estimate** | $G \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} 4 \cdot \text{inputs}(L_i)$ | Rough FPGA resource utilization (LUTs ≈ 4-input gates). |
| 6 | **Critical-Path Delay (Behavioral)** | $T_{cp} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} t_{pd,k}$ | Sum of combinational delays on the longest path. |
| 7 | **Test-bench Coverage** | $C = \dfrac{N_{\text{vectors\_tested}}}{N_{\text{vectors\_total}}} \times 100\%$ | % of input space covered. |
| 8 | **Test-bench Quality (Toggle)** | $T_{\text{coverage}} = \dfrac{\text{Signals\_toggled}}{\text{Total\_signals}} \times 100\%$ | Used in code-coverage tools. |
| 9 | **Race Condition Criterion** | Two processes assign same signal in same $\Delta$ | Undefined final value (`'X'` in VHDL, `'x'` in Verilog). |
| 10 | **Behavioural Hierarchy Rule** | High level → fewer components, more abstract | Lower level → more components, more detail. |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is a behavioural model not always synthesizable?"* — The answer: because some constructs (e.g., `wait for 10 ns`, file I/O, real-time delays) have no hardware equivalent. Memorize this.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Behavioural Modelling & Simulation |
|---|---|
| **ASIC / FPGA Design** | Front-end verification, regression testing, IP validation. |
| **Embedded Systems** | Pre-silicon firmware co-simulation (CPU + peripheral model). |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | ASIL-D certified functional safety verification. |
| **Aerospace (DO-254)** | Compliance with safety-critical design assurance. |
| **5G / DSP** | Algorithm exploration of FFT, FEC, beamforming before RTL commit. |
| **AI Accelerators** | Evaluating dataflow vs. systolic architectures. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Y-Chart Design-Flow Mapping

The Y-chart establishes a bijection between any design point in the *behavioural* domain and a corresponding point in the *structural* and *physical* domains at the same abstraction level. The mapping is a **step-wise refinement** (also called *lowering* or *synthesis*).

### Step 1 — Start at the System / Algorithmic Level
At the outermost ring, the designer writes an **untimed, functional** specification. Example: a 1024-point FFT for OFDM demodulation.

$$
\text{Behaviour}_{S} = \text{FFT}_{1024}\big(x[n]\big) = \sum_{k=0}^{1023} x[n] \cdot W_N^{nk}
$$

where $W_N^{nk} = e^{-j2\pi nk/N}$ is the twiddle factor.

### Step 2 — Lower to the RT (Register-Transfer) Level
The algorithmic behaviour is mapped to a **datapath + control FSM** pair.

$$
\text{Behaviour}_{RT} = \text{FSM}(S_0, S_1, S_2, \ldots) \;\wedge\; \text{Datapath}(\text{ALU}, \text{Reg}, \text{Mem})
$$

Concretely, the FFT is decomposed into radix-2 butterfly operations, each mapped to a multiply-accumulate (MAC) unit.

### Step 3 — Lower to the Logic Level
Each RT block becomes a **netlist of standard cells** (gates + flip-flops).

$$
\text{Structure}_{L} = \big\{ \text{AND}, \text{OR}, \text{NOT}, \text{DFF} \big\}
$$

### Step 4 — Lower to the Circuit Level
Each gate is mapped to a **transistor-level CMOS implementation** (pull-up / pull-down networks).

$$
\text{Structure}_{C} = \{ \text{PMOS}, \text{NMOS} \}, \quad V_{OH}, V_{OL} \text{ defined}
$$

### Step 5 — Lower to the Physical / Layout Level
Transistors are placed and routed on the silicon die (or, for FPGAs, mapped to **CLBs, LUTs, and routing fabric**).

$$
\text{Physical}_{P} = \text{GDS-II (ASIC)} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \text{Bitstream (FPGA)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each "lowering step" must be **verifiable** by simulation at the new level. This is the core of KTU Module-1: *behavioural modelling enables verification BEFORE the next lowering step is taken.*

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Event-Driven Simulation Cycle

Let the simulated time be $t$, and let the active event queue be $Q(t)$. The simulator performs the following steps (shown as a sequential algorithm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step A:} \quad t \leftarrow 0, \quad \text{initialize all signals to } 'X' \\
&\textbf{Step B:} \quad \text{Elaborate hierarchy, schedule initial events in } Q(0) \\
&\textbf{Step C:} \quad \textbf{while } Q(t) \neq \emptyset \;\textbf{ do} \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{Step C.1:} \quad \text{Pop all events at time } t \text{ from } Q(t) \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{Step C.2:} \quad \text{Update signal values (causes new events)} \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{Step C.3:} \quad \text{Activate all } \text{process sensitive to changed signals} \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{Step C.4:} \quad \text{Evaluate active processes, schedule their assignments at } t + n_{\Delta} \cdot \Delta t \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{Step C.5:} \quad \text{Repeat C.1 - C.4 until } Q(t) \text{ is empty} \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{Step C.6:} \quad t \leftarrow t_{\text{next}}, \text{ where } t_{\text{next}} = \min\{t' \vert Q(t') \neq \emptyset\} \\
&\textbf{Step D:} \quad \text{Print waveforms, finish}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example — A 2-Input AND Gate in Zero Delta-Cycles

Suppose we have `Y = A AND B` with initial values `A = '0'`, `B = '1'`, `Y = 'X'`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0: \quad & A \leftarrow 0, \; B \leftarrow 1, \; Y \text{ (sensitive to } A, B) \\
\Delta\text{-cycle 0:} \quad & Y = 0 \text{ AND } 1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; Y \leftarrow 0 \text{ scheduled at } t + 1\Delta \\
\Delta\text{-cycle 1:} \quad & \text{No further events, } Q(t) = \emptyset \\
\therefore \quad & t_{\text{next}} = \infty \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Simulation halts.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The output `Y` stabilizes after **one delta-cycle** at the same physical time $t=0$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Modelling Styles

| Parameter | Behavioural | Dataflow | Structural | Physical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction** | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| **Synthesizable?** | Often no | Yes | Yes | Yes (to bitstream) |
| **Simulation Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Slow | Slowest |
| **Detail Captured** | Function | Function + connectivity | Gates / Cells | Layout / Geometry |
| **Tool Input** | Spec / Algorithm | RTL | Netlist | Floorplan / Bitstream |
| **Bug Localization** | Easy | Easy | Moderate | Hard |
| **HDL Example** | `always @(*)` with `if/case` | `assign Y = A & B` | `and_gate U1(Y, A, B)` | LUT map + routing |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Test-Bench Coverage Bound

For a digital system with $n$ primary inputs, the **exhaustive** input space has cardinality:

$$
N_{\text{exhaustive}} = 2^n
$$

A practical test-bench with $k$ directed test vectors achieves coverage:

$$
C_{\text{test}} = \dfrac{k}{2^n} \times 100\%
$$

For example, a 32-bit ALU ($n=32$) has $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ possible input combinations. A test-bench of $k = 10{,}000$ directed vectors achieves:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{test}} &= \dfrac{10{,}000}{4{,}294{,}967{,}296} \times 100\% \\
&= 2.328 \times 10^{-4}\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
