# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis from First Principles

### Problem
Given a Schmitt trigger inverter with reference voltage $V_{ref} = 1.65$ V and hysteresis window $\Delta V_H = 0.9$ V, compute the upper and lower switching thresholds for a 3.3 V LVTTL input.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Recall the standard definitions for a non-inverting Schmitt trigger:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{T+} &= V_{ref} + \frac{\Delta V_H}{2} \\[6pt]
V_{T-} &= V_{ref} - \frac{\Delta V_H}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values $V_{ref} = 1.65$ V and $\Delta V_H = 0.9$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{T+} &= 1.65 + \frac{0.9}{2} = 1.65 + 0.45 = 2.10 \text{ V} \\[6pt]
V_{T-} &= 1.65 - \frac{0.9}{2} = 1.65 - 0.45 = 1.20 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation:
- A rising input transitions from logic 0 to logic 1 only after crossing **2.10 V**.
- A falling input transitions from logic 1 to logic 0 only after crossing **1.20 V**.
- Between **1.20 V and 2.10 V**, the output **retains its previous state** — this is the hysteresis band that rejects noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The hysteresis band **must** exceed the worst-case PCB noise voltage. A $900$ mV hysteresis window can absorb typical ground-bounce and reflection noise on 3.3 V LVTTL lines.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Programmable Output Rise Time from Slew Rate

### Problem
A 3.3 V LVCMOS output is configured with slew rate $SR = 2.0$ V/ns. Calculate the 20%–80% rise time.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** The 20%–80% voltage excursion is:

$$
\Delta V = 0.8 \cdot V_{CCO} - 0.2 \cdot V_{CCO} = 0.6 \cdot V_{CCO}
$$

**Step 2:** With $V_{CCO} = 3.3$ V:

$$
\Delta V = 0.6 \times 3.3 = 1.98 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3:** Using the slew rate definition $SR = \Delta V / \Delta t$:

$$
\Delta t = t_r = \frac{\Delta V}{SR} = \frac{1.98}{2.0} = 0.99 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification by $0.8 \cdot V_{CCO}$ shortcut:

$$
t_r = \frac{0.8 \cdot V_{CCO}}{SR} = \frac{0.8 \times 3.3}{2.0} = \frac{2.64}{2.0} = 1.32 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two formulas give slightly different values because the $0.8 \cdot V_{CCO}$ approximation assumes a 0%–100% base and the $\Delta V$ form is exact for 20%–80%. **In KTU board answers, use the exact $0.6 V_{CCO}$ form** unless the question specifies 0%–100%.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drive Strength Constraint on Load Capacitance

### Problem
An LVCMOS33 output buffer is configured for drive strength $I_{OL} = 8$ mA. The output is loaded with a capacitive load $C_L = 25$ pF. Calculate the maximum toggle frequency such that the output settles within 1/10 of the period.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Charging time of an RC load:

$$
t_{charge} = R_{DS,on} \cdot C_L \cdot \ln\left( \frac{V_{CCO}}{V_{CCO} - 0.9 V_{CCO}} \right) = R_{DS,on} \cdot C_L \cdot \ln(10)
$$

**Step 2:** Estimate $R_{DS,on}$ from drive strength:

$$
R_{DS,on} \approx \frac{V_{CCO} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}} = \frac{3.3 - 0.4}{8 \text{ mA}} = \frac{2.9}{0.008} = 362.5 \text{ }\Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Compute charge time:

$$
t_{charge} = 362.5 \times 25 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.303 = 2.087 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 20.87 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum frequency for full settling in one quarter period (rise + fall = 2 transitions per cycle):

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot t_{charge}} = \frac{1}{4 \cdot 20.87 \text{ ns}} \approx 11.98 \text{ MHz}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic Power per I/O Pin

### Problem
A bidirectional I/O pin swings between 0 V and 3.3 V at a frequency of 100 MHz into a 15 pF load (trace + pin capacitance of adjacent device). Calculate the dynamic switching power.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** The standard CMOS dynamic power equation:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{CCO}^2 \cdot f
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ — activity factor (0 to 1, often **0.5** for random data)
- $C_L$ — total load capacitance
- $V_{CCO}$ — I/O supply voltage
- $f$ — toggle frequency

**Step 2:** Plug in values $\alpha = 0.5$, $C_L = 15$ pF, $V_{CCO} = 3.3$ V, $f = 100$ MHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn} &= 0.5 \times 15 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.3)^2 \times 100 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 15 \times 10^{-12} \times 10.89 \times 10^8 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 15 \times 10.89 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 163.35 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&= 81.675 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} \\[4pt]
&= 8.17 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a 100-pin bus, total I/O dynamic power = $0.817$ W.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **activity factor** $\alpha$ and report $16.3$ mW instead of $8.17$ mW. For **clock signals**, $\alpha = 2$ (every edge charges and discharges) and the equation doubles to $P = C_L V_{CCO}^2 f$.

---

| **Standard** | $V_{CCO}$ (V) | $V_{ref}$ (V) | $V_{IL,max}$ (V) | $V_{IH,min}$ (V) | $V_{OL,max}$ (V) | $V_{OH,min}$ (V) | Application |
|-------------|----------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|-------------|

| **LVCMOS12** | 1.2 | — | 0.35·$V_{CCO}$ | 0.65·$V_{CCO}$ | 0.45·$V_{CCO}$ | 0.75·$V_{CCO}$ | Sub-1V SoC |
| **SSTL2** | 2.5 | 1.25 | $V_{ref}-0.18$ | $V_{ref}+0.18$ | 0.61 | 1.79 | DDR1 |

| **SSTL15** | 1.5 | 0.75 | $V_{ref}-0.1$ | $V_{ref}+0.1$ | 0.4·$V_{CCO}$ | 0.6·$V_{CCO}$ | DDR3 |

| **HSTL_II** | 1.5 | 0.75 | $V_{ref}-0.1$ | $V_{ref}+0.1$ | 0.4 | $V_{CCO}-0.4$ | QDR SRAM (high drive) |
| **LVDS** | 2.5 | — | — | — | 1.0 (typ) | 1.4 (typ) | High-speed serial |
| **PCI** | 3.3 | — | 0.5·$V_{CCO}$ | 0.5·$V_{CCO}$ | 0.1·$V_{CCO}$ | 0.9·$V_{CCO}$ | 33/66 MHz bus |

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition for Board Exams:**
> - If you see a question on **DDR memory interfacing**, the answer is **SSTL18** or **SSTL15**.
> - If you see **flat-panel display or high-speed ADC**, the answer is **LVDS**.
> - If you see **5 V tolerance with 3.3 V supply**, the answer is **LVTTL** with a 5 V tolerant I/O bank.

---

## 3.6 Stub Series Termination Length (SSTL Constraint)

For SSTL/HSTL, the PCB stub from the die pad to the first device must be shorter than the critical length to avoid reflections:

$$
L_{stub,max} = \frac{t_r}{2 \cdot t_{pd,PCB}}
$$

With $t_r = 1$ ns and $t_{pd,PCB} = 150$ ps/inch:

$$
L_{stub,max} = \frac{1 \text{ ns}}{2 \times 0.15 \text{ ns/inch}} = 3.33 \text{ inches}
$$

For faster edges $t_r = 0.2$ ns:

$$
L_{stub,max} = \frac{0.2}{0.3} = 0.667 \text{ inches}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **SSTL stubs beyond 0.5 inches cause severe ringing.** This is why FPGA memory interfaces require precise board layout, controlled impedance (50 Ω), and matched length routing.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic FPGA I/O Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Package Pin] --> B[ESD Protection Diodes to VCCO and GND]
    B --> C[Programmable Pull-up or Pull-down Resistor]
    C --> D[Input Buffer with Schmitt Trigger]
    D --> E[Programmable Input Delay IDELAY]
    E --> F1[Input Flip-Flop SDR Capture]
    E --> F2[Input Serializer ISERDES DDR 1 colon 4]
    F1 --> G1[Routing to FPGA Fabric]
    F2 --> G1

    H[FPGA Fabric Logic] --> I[Output Flip-Flop ODDR SDR or DDR]
    I --> J[Programmable Output Delay ODELAY]
    J --> K[Slew Rate and Drive Strength Selector]
    K --> L[Tri-state Control and Output Enable]
    L --> M[Output Buffer CMOS Push-Pull]
    M --> N[External Package Pin]
    M --> O[Open-Drain Mode Optional]

    P[Dedicated VREF Pin] -.-> D
    Q[Dedicated VCCO Bank Rail] -.-> M
    R[Differential Pair Comparator Mode] -.-> D
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Input Cell Detailed Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP[Input Cell Sub-system]
        P[PAD Pin] --> ESD[ESD Clamp Diodes]
        ESD --> PU[Weak Pull-up 40 to 60 kΩ]
        ESD --> PD[Weak Pull-down 40 to 60 kΩ]
        ESD --> KEEPER[Bus Keeper Weak Latch]
        ESD --> DIFF[Diff Comparator for LVDS]
        ESD --> SE[Single Ended Buffer for LVCMOS]
        DIFF --> SCH[Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis]
        SE --> SCH
        SCH --> IDE[IDELAY 32-tap ~78 ps each]
        IDE --> ISE[ISERDES Optional 1:4 or 1:8 DDR]
        IDE --> IFF[Input Flip-Flop SDR]
        ISE --> FAB[FPGA Fabric]
        IFF --> FAB
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Output Cell Detailed Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUT[Output Cell Sub-system]
        F1[Fabric Data] --> OFF[Output FF SDR or ODDR]
        F2[Fabric Tri-state T] --> TFF[Tri-state FF]
        OFF --> ODE[ODELAY Programmable]
        ODE --> SR[Slew Rate Control Slow or Fast]
        SR --> DRV[Drive Strength Selector 2 to 24 mA]
        DRV --> PCHG[PMOS Pull-up Transistor]
        DRV --> NCHG[NMOS Pull-down Transistor]
        TFF --> PCHG
        TFF --> NCHG
        PCHG --> PAD[Package Pin]
        NCHG --> PAD
        OD[Open-Drain Mode] -.-> PCHG
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bidirectional I/O (Tristate Sharing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fabric Input Path] --> B[IFD Input FF]
    B --> C[Fabric Logic]
    C --> D[OFD Output FF]
    D --> E[Tri-state Buffer with OE]
    E --> F[Pad]
    F --> G[Off-chip Trace to Other Device]
    B -.-> F
    OE[OE Control from Fabric] -.-> E
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — I/O Bank Voltage Reference (SSTL/HSTL)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BANK[I/O Bank 8 to 40 Pins per Bank] --> VCCO[Shared VCCO Rail]
    BANK --> VREF[Shared VREF Rail for SSTL or HSTL]
    PIN1[Pin 1 SSTL Driver] --> VCCO
    PIN1 --> VREF
    PIN2[Pin 2 SSTL Driver] --> VCCO
    PIN2 --> VREF
    PIN3[Pin 3 SSTL Driver] --> VCCO
    PIN3 --> VREF
    NOTE[All pins in a bank must share the same VCCO and VREF. Mixing LVTTL and SSTL in the same bank is not allowed.] -.-> BANK
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Critical Fact:** *All pins within a single I/O bank must operate on the same $V_{CCO}$ voltage.* Mixing standards like LVTTL (3.3 V) and SSTL18 (1.8 V) in the same bank is **not** supported. The bank architecture is one of the most common exam topics.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*Define Schmitt trigger input hysteresis in FPGA I/O cells. Why is it important for noise immunity?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Schmitt trigger input buffer has **two distinct switching thresholds**: an upper threshold $V_{T+}$ for the rising edge and a lower threshold $V_{T-}$ for the falling edge. The difference $V_{T+} - V_{T-} = \Delta V_H$ is the **hysteresis window**. Any noise voltage whose amplitude is less than $\Delta V_H/2$ on either side of the reference cannot flip the output state. For a 3.3 V LVTTL input, the hysteresis window is typically **0.9 V**, which is large enough to reject ground bounce, crosstalk, and reflection noise on PCB traces, ensuring the FPGA registers clean logic levels at multi-hundred MHz operation.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Distinguish between slow and fast slew rate control in FPGA output cells. Mention one application where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Slew rate control adjusts the **rise and fall time** of the output edge:
- **Slow slew rate** → longer edge time ($t_r \approx 3$–4 ns), reduced EMI, lower simultaneous switching output (SSO) noise, lower ground bounce. Preferred for **PCI bus** and EMI-sensitive consumer applications.
- **Fast slew rate** → shorter edge time ($t_r \approx 0.5$–1 ns), higher toggle frequency, sharper edges. Preferred for **DDR memory buses** and high-speed synchronous interfaces.

The trade-off is **signal integrity vs. speed**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a typical FPGA I/O cell. Explain the function of the input buffer, output buffer, tri-state control, and pull-up/pull-down resistor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to SSTL18 I/O standard, explain the role of $V_{CCO}$ and $V_{REF}$ in FPGA memory interfacing. List any three differences between SSTL18 and LVCMOS18. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

A **typical FPGA I/O cell** consists of the following functional blocks (block diagram as in SECTION 4.1):

1. **Input Buffer (Schmitt Trigger)**: Accepts external pin voltage, applies hysteresis $V_{T+}$ and $V_{T-}$, and produces a clean internal logic level. Protects against noise. Optionally supports differential comparison for LVDS.
2. **Output Buffer (Push-Pull CMOS)**: Drives the external pin with selectable drive strength (2–24 mA) and slew rate (slow/fast). Comprises a PMOS pull-up and NMOS pull-down totem pole.
3. **Tri-state Control (T)**: Disables both transistors in the output buffer when the pin must enter high-impedance (Hi-Z) state, typically during bus arbitration or when configured as a pure input.
4. **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistor**: Weak internal resistor (~**40–60 kΩ**) that defaults the pin to a known logic level (logic 1 for pull-up to $V_{CCO}$, logic 0 for pull-down to GND) when no external driver is active. Prevents floating input.

**Additional elements (advanced):**
5. **IDELAY/ODELAY**: Programmable delay elements (e.g., 32-tap, 78 ps/tap) for source-synchronous timing alignment.
6. **ISERDES/OSERDES**: Serializers/deserializers for high-speed serial-to-parallel conversion.
7. **ESD Diodes**: Clamp voltage transients to $V_{CCO}$ and GND for protection.

#### Model Solution (b)

**$V_{CCO}$** in SSTL18 is **1.8 V** — the supply rail that powers the **output driver** and sets the output high voltage.

**$V_{REF}$** in SSTL18 is **0.9 V** — a reference voltage that biases the **input buffer's differential comparator**. Inputs are interpreted as logic 0 if $V_{in} < V_{REF} - 0.125$ V and logic 1 if $V_{in} > V_{REF} + 0.125$ V.

**Role in memory interfacing:** $V_{REF}$-based differential sensing rejects common-mode noise from $V_{CCO}$ fluctuations and PCB switching noise, which is critical for **DDR2/DDR3** data and address buses operating at hundreds of MHz.

**Three differences between SSTL18 and LVCMOS18:**

| **Parameter** | **SSTL18** | **LVCMOS18** |
|---------------|------------|--------------|
| Input reference | Uses $V_{REF} = 0.9$ V reference comparator | Single-ended, no $V_{REF}$ |
| Output drive | Symmetric push-pull, current-based | Push-pull CMOS |
| Application | DDR2/DDR3 memory buses | General-purpose, GPIO, low-speed buses |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A(a):**
> Students often **forget to label the direction arrows** on the block diagram and lose 1–2 marks. Always label **input path** (left to right) and **output path** (right to left or fabric to pad) clearly. Also, in part (b), do **NOT** confuse $V_{REF}$ with $V_{CCO}$. $V_{REF}$ is an input reference, not a supply.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the four most common programmable features of an FPGA output cell: drive strength, slew rate, open-drain, and tri-state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3.3 V LVCMOS output drives a 30 pF load at 50 MHz with activity factor $\alpha = 0.5$. Calculate the dynamic switching power per pin. If the FPGA has 64 such I/O pins, compute the total I/O switching power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

1. **Drive Strength ($I_{drive}$)**: The maximum current the output buffer can source/sink while maintaining $V_{OL}/V_{OH}$. Selectable from **2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24 mA** (Xilinx 7-Series). Higher drive strength enables faster edges into large capacitive loads but increases ground bounce and SSO noise.

2. **Slew Rate**: Controls the rise/fall time of the output edge. **SLOW** slew (3–4 ns) reduces EMI and reflections; **FAST** slew (0.5–1 ns) enables high-speed buses. Configured by a global bit in the I/O control register.

3. **Open-Drain**: Disables the PMOS pull-up transistor, leaving only the NMOS pull-down. The output can only actively pull low; an external pull-up is required to reach logic high. Used for **wired-OR buses** (I²C, CAN, SMBus).

4. **Tri-state (High-Z)**: Disables both PMOS and NMOS, presenting a high impedance to the bus. The pin enters Hi-Z state when the Output Enable (OE) control bit is deasserted. Used for **bidirectional buses** and **bus arbitration** in multi-master systems.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:**
- $V_{CCO} = 3.3$ V
- $C_L = 30$ pF
- $f = 50$ MHz
- Activity factor $\alpha = 0.5$

**Step 1 — Dynamic power per pin:**

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{CCO}^2 \cdot f
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.5 \times 30 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.3)^2 \times 50 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 3 — Square $V_{CCO}$:**

$$
(3.3)^2 = 10.89 \text{ V}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.5 \times 30 \times 10^{-12} \times 10.89 \times 50 \times 10^6
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.5 \times 30 \times 10.89 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{dyn} = 0.5 \times 16335 \times 10^{-6} = 8167.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 8.17 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 5 — Total for 64 pins:**

$$
P_{total} = 64 \times 8.17 \text{ mW} = 522.88 \text{ mW} \approx 0.523 \text{ W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B(b):**
> The activity factor $\alpha = 0.5$ is for **random data**. For **clock signals** (every edge toggles the load), $\alpha = 1$ and the power doubles. Many students forget the activity factor entirely and report **16.34 mW** per pin, losing 2 marks. Also, ensure unit conversion: **pF × MHz = μA × V** scale factor, not mA.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 3 Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **I/O Block = IOB** is a **hardened peripheral block**, NOT part of programmable fabric. It sits at the silicon periphery.
- **Bank Rule**: All pins in one bank must share **one $V_{CCO}$** and **one $V_{REF}$**. Mixing incompatible standards is forbidden.
- **Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis** $\Delta V_H = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$ is the noise rejection window. Typical LVTTL: 0.9 V at 3.3 V.
- **Pull-up / Pull-down** resistors are **weak** (40–60 kΩ) and only used to default floating pins — not for driving.
- **Drive strength** is the DC current limit (mA). Higher drive → faster edge → more SSO noise.
- **Slew rate** is the edge rate (V/ns). Slow = EMI friendly, Fast = high speed.
- **Tri-state** = Hi-Z = $Z_{out} \to \infty$. Used for bidirectional buses and multi-master arbitration.
- **Open-drain** requires **external pull-up**. Used for I²C, CAN, wired-OR buses.
- **Differential I/O (LVDS)** uses two pins, 350 mV typical swing, common-mode around 1.2 V.
- **SSTL/HSTL** use $V_{REF}$-based input comparators. Mandatory for **DDR memory interfaces**.
- **IDELAY/ODELAY** provide fine-grained timing alignment (~78 ps per tap) for source-synchronous buses.
- **ISERDES/OSERDES** perform serial-to-parallel and parallel-to-serial conversion (1:4, 1:8, 1:10).
- **Dynamic power per pin**: $P = \alpha C_L V_{CCO}^2 f$. Activity factor $\alpha = 0.5$ for random data, $\alpha = 1$ for clocks.
- **ESD Diodes** clamp pin voltage to $V_{CCO} + 0.5$ V and $-0.5$ V. Critical for hot-plug (PCI).
- **PCI compliance** requires 5 V tolerance with 3.3 V $V_{CCO}$ — only specific I/O banks support it.
- **Stub length** for SSTL: $L_{stub,max} = t_r / (2 t_{pd,PCB})$. Faster edges = shorter allowable stubs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Exam Pearl:**
> The I/O block architecture sits at the intersection of **digital design, analog signal integrity, and PCB layout**. A strong answer always references the **standard name** (e.g., LVCMOS33, SSTL18), the **exact voltage level** ($V_{CCO}$, $V_{REF}$), the **threshold values** ($V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_{OH}$), and the **application context** (memory, display, bus, GPIO). Toppers tie these four anchors into a single paragraph.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clock Input
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clock Input

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Input (Definition per KTU 2024 FPGA Syllabus):**
> A **Clock Input** in an FPGA is a dedicated, high-fanout physical pin (or a routed internal network) that accepts an external periodic square-wave signal used as the **global timing reference** for sequential logic elements such as flip-flops, latches, Block RAMs (BRAMs), DSP slices, and I/O registers. It synchronizes data propagation, defines setup/hold windows, and orchestrates state transitions across the entire device.

In every FPGA (Xilinx Spartan/Artix, Intel/Altera Cyclone, Lattice ECP5, etc.), the clock input pin is typically fed into a dedicated **Clock Buffer** (e.g., IBUFG → BUFG → global clock network) before being distributed to the entire chip fabric.

### Key Technical Parameters of a Clock Input Signal

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | 10 – 500 | MHz |
| Period | $T_{clk}$ | $\frac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | ns |
| Duty Cycle | $D$ | 45 – 55 | % |
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | 0.5 – 2 | ns |
| Fall Time | $t_f$ | 0.5 – 2 | ns |
| Jitter (cycle-to-cycle) | $T_{jit}$ | 50 – 200 | ps |
| Skew (across chip) | $T_{skew}$ | 50 – 500 | ps |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Heartbeat of a City:**
> Imagine a busy railway station with thousands of passengers boarding trains simultaneously. Without a master **clock** (like a station-master's whistle), trains would leave at random, causing chaos and collisions.
>
> The **FPGA Clock Input** is exactly that master whistle. Every flip-flop "hears" the whistle and only then updates its stored value. Without the clock, the FPGA is just a pile of static logic gates — the clock brings it to **life**, making it a synchronous digital system.

Think of the clock as the **heartbeat** of the FPGA:
- A **heart rate of 100 MHz** means the heart beats every **10 ns**.
- Each beat allows every flip-flop in the design to **sample** its input data and **propagate** the result.
- If the heartbeat is irregular (high jitter), the system becomes unreliable — just as an irregular heartbeat causes medical issues.

---

## 1.3 Why Clock Inputs Are Special (Not Ordinary I/O Pins)

In an FPGA, clock pins are **physically and electrically distinct** from regular user I/O pins:

1. **Dedicated Routing Channels** — Clock inputs connect to global clock trees (low-skew networks) rather than generic interconnect.
2. **Differential Signaling Support** — Most FPGAs support LVDS, LVPECL, or HSTL differential clock inputs for noise immunity.
3. **Direct Connection to Clock Management Tiles (CMT/PLL/MMCM)** — Clock inputs can be routed to Phase-Locked Loops for frequency synthesis.
4. **Higher Drive Strength** — Clock buffers drive thousands of loads simultaneously.
5. **Input Pin Standards** — Only specific I/O standards (LVCMOS, LVDS, LVPECL, SSTL) are supported on clock pins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Clock inputs are the entry point to the FPGA's **clock distribution network**, which is a hierarchical structure consisting of:
> - **Global Clock Buffers** (BUFG in Xilinx / ALTCLKCTRL in Intel)
> - **Regional Clock Networks** (BUFR)
> - **I/O Clock Networks** (BUFIO)
> - **Clock Management Tiles** (MMCM/PLL)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Clock Input Path

A clock signal entering the FPGA traverses a well-defined hierarchy:

```
External Crystal/Oscillator
        │
        ▼
   ┌────────────┐
   │  Clock Pin │   ← Dedicated clock pin (e.g., Xilinx: MRCC/SRCC pins)
   └────────────┘
        │
        ▼
   ┌────────────┐
   │   IBUFG    │   ← Input Global Buffer (converts pin standard → internal logic)
   └────────────┘
        │
        ▼
   ┌────────────┐
   │ BUFG / BUF │   ← Global Clock Buffer (drives the entire chip)
   └────────────┘
        │
        ├────► MMCM/PLL (frequency multiplication, phase shift)
        │
        ▼
  Global Clock Spine (low-skew H-tree distribution)
        │
        ├──► Region 1 flip-flops
        ├──► Region 2 flip-flops
        ├──► Region 3 flip-flops
        └──► Region 4 flip-flops
```

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Functional Breakdown

**Step 1: External Source Generation**
- A **crystal oscillator** generates a precise sine/square wave (e.g., 50 MHz).
- This signal is fed to a dedicated **clock input pin** of the FPGA via a PCB trace.

**Step 2: Input Buffering (IBUFG)**
- The clock pin is connected to an **IBUFG** (Input Global Buffer).
- This buffer converts the external voltage standard (e.g., LVCMOS33, LVDS) into the FPGA's internal core logic level (typically 1.0 V – 1.8 V).

**Step 3: Global Buffering (BUFG)**
- The buffered clock drives a **BUFG**, which is a high-fanout buffer capable of driving thousands of flip-flops across the entire die with minimal skew.
- The BUFG connects to a **global clock spine** (H-tree topology).

**Step 4: Optional Frequency Synthesis (MMCM/PLL)**
- If a different frequency or phase is required, the global clock can be routed to an **MMCM (Mixed-Mode Clock Manager)** or **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)**.
- These blocks can:
  - **Multiply** the frequency (e.g., 50 MHz → 200 MHz)
  - **Divide** the frequency (e.g., 50 MHz → 25 MHz)
  - **Shift phase** (e.g., 90° for DDR interfaces)
  - **Deskew** the clock to compensate for PCB delays

**Step 5: Distribution to Sequential Elements**
- The clock reaches every flip-flop in the FPGA simultaneously (with minimal skew, typically < 100 ps).

---

## 2.2 Clock Skew, Jitter, and Timing Margins

### 2.2.1 Clock Skew ($T_{skew}$)
The difference in arrival time of the clock signal at two different flip-flops due to routing delays.

### 2.2.2 Clock Jitter ($T_{jit}$)
Short-term variation of the clock edge from its ideal position.

### 2.2.3 Setup Time ($T_{su}$) and Hold Time ($T_{h}$)
- **Setup Time**: Minimum time data must be stable *before* the clock edge.
- **Hold Time**: Minimum time data must be stable *after* the clock edge.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR CLOCK INPUT (KTU Board Exam Essentials)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Clock period from frequency | ns |
| 2 | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Frequency from period | MHz |
| 3 | $T_{su}^{margin} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{skew} - T_{su}$ | Setup time slack (positive ⇒ design works) | ns |
| 4 | $T_{h}^{margin} = T_{cq} + T_{skew} - T_{h}$ | Hold time slack (must be $> 0$) | ns |
| 5 | $f_{out} = f_{in} \cdot \dfrac{M}{D}$ | PLL/MMCM output frequency (M = multiplier, D = divider) | MHz |
| 6 | $f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot \dfrac{M}{D}$ | VCO operating frequency (must be within PLL range, e.g., 600–1600 MHz) | MHz |
| 7 | $\phi_{shift} = \dfrac{T_{clk}}{360°} \cdot \theta$ | Phase shift to time conversion | ns |
| 8 | $T_{skew}^{max} = \sqrt{T_{skew,A}^{2} + T_{skew,B}^{2}}$ | RSS-based skew composition | ps |
| 9 | $T_{jit}^{total} = \sqrt{T_{jit,src}^{2} + T_{jit,pll}^{2} + T_{jit,int}^{2}}$ | Total jitter (root-sum-square) | ps |
| 10 | $Duty = \dfrac{T_{high}}{T_{clk}} \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle definition | % |
| 11 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{signal}$ | Nyquist criterion for sampled signals | MHz |
| 12 | $Power_{clk} = C_{load} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Dynamic clock-tree power dissipation | mW |

> **Notation Note:**
> - $T_{cq}$ = Clock-to-Q delay (flip-flop propagation delay)
> - $T_{comb}$ = Combinational logic delay between flip-flops
> - $T_{su}, T_{h}$ = Setup and hold time requirements of the flip-flop

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Clock inputs are foundational to virtually every digital engineering application:

1. **Microprocessor & SoC Design** — Clock inputs feed CPU pipelines, caches, and bus interfaces.
2. **Communication Systems** — DDR memory controllers, PCIe, SERDES, and Ethernet MACs require precise multi-frequency clocks.
3. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** — FIR filters, FFT engines sample data on clock edges.
4. **High-Speed ADC/DAC Interfacing** — Sample clocks determine analog bandwidth.
5. **Aerospace & Defense** — Radiation-tolerant FPGAs (e.g., Xilinx Virtex-5QV) use redundant clock inputs for fault tolerance.
6. **Automotive ADAS** — Sensor fusion systems need deterministic clock distribution.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Setup Time Margin (KTU High-Yield Derivation)

### Problem Setup
A data signal travels from Flip-Flop **FF1** → combinational logic → Flip-Flop **FF2**. Both FFs are triggered by the same clock edge. Derive the **setup time slack** equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify all timing elements on the data path**

The data path consists of:
- Clock-to-Q delay of FF1: $T_{cq}$
- Combinational logic delay: $T_{comb}$
- Routing delay: $T_{route}$

$$
T_{data\_arrival} = T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{route}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the clock arrival time at FF2**

$$
T_{clk\_arrival} = T_{clk\_period} + T_{skew}
$$

Where $T_{skew}$ is the difference in clock arrival between FF2 and FF1. If the clock arrives *later* at FF2, $T_{skew} > 0$ (helping setup).

**Step 3: Apply the setup time requirement**

For correct operation, data must arrive at FF2 at least $T_{su}$ before the next clock edge:

$$
T_{data\_arrival} \leq T_{clk\_arrival} - T_{su}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and solve for the slack**

$$
T_{cq} + T_{comb} + T_{route} \leq T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
T_{slack}^{setup} = T_{clk} + T_{skew} - T_{su} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{route}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{slack}^{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{route} - T_{su} + T_{skew}}
$$

> **Key Insight:** For setup to be satisfied, $T_{slack}^{setup} > 0$. This is the **most-tested** formula in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PLL Output Frequency (MMCM/PLL Configuration)

### Problem Setup
An FPGA receives a 50 MHz reference clock on its clock input pin. Design a PLL configuration to generate a 200 MHz output clock with a 90° phase shift.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify input frequency**

$$
f_{in} = 50 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2: Determine desired output frequency**

$$
f_{out} = 200 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3: Apply PLL frequency synthesis equation**

$$
f_{out} = f_{in} \cdot \frac{M}{D}
$$

Where $M$ is the multiplier and $D$ is the divider.

**Step 4: Solve for $M/D$**

$$
\frac{M}{D} = \frac{f_{out}}{f_{in}} = \frac{200}{50} = 4
$$

Choose $M = 4$, $D = 1$.

**Step 5: Verify VCO frequency is within acceptable range**

$$
f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot \frac{M}{D} = 50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ MHz}
$$

For typical Xilinx MMCMs, $f_{VCO}$ must be in [600 MHz, 1600 MHz]. Therefore, we must adjust:
- Choose $M = 16$, $D = 1$ → $f_{VCO} = 800$ MHz (within range).
- Use output divider $O = 4$ to get $f_{out} = 800 / 4 = 200$ MHz.

**Step 6: Compute phase shift in time**

$$
\phi_{shift} = \frac{T_{clk}}{360°} \cdot \theta = \frac{5 \text{ ns}}{360°} \cdot 90° = 1.25 \text{ ns}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Clock Power Dissipation

### Problem Setup
A global clock buffer drives a load capacitance of $C_{load} = 100$ pF at $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V and $f_{clk} = 250$ MHz. Compute the dynamic power dissipated by the clock tree.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the dynamic power formula for CMOS**

$$
P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{load} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

For a clock signal, switching activity $\alpha = 1$ (toggles every cycle).

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
P_{dynamic} = 1 \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (1.0)^{2} \cdot (250 \times 10^{6})
$$

**Step 3: Calculate**

$$
P_{dynamic} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 250 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
P_{dynamic} = 25{,}000 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{dynamic} = 25 \text{ mW}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Single Clock Input | Multiple Clock Inputs | Differential Clock Input |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pin Count | 1 pin | N pins | 2 pins (P + N) |
| Noise Immunity | Moderate | Moderate | High (common-mode rejection) |
| Skew | Low (global net) | Higher (domain crossing issues) | Very low |
| Use Case | Simple synchronous designs | Multi-clock domain designs | High-speed SERDES, DDR |
| Typical Standard | LVCMOS33 | LVCMOS33, LVTTL | LVDS, LVPECL, HSTL |
| Frequency Range | up to ~200 MHz | up to ~200 MHz each | up to >1 GHz |
| Power Consumption | Lower | Higher | Moderate |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

### Problem
A flip-flop has $T_{su} = 0.5$ ns and $T_{h} = 0.3$ ns. The combinational logic between two FFs has $T_{comb} = 3$ ns, $T_{cq} = 0.4$ ns, and routing delay $T_{route} = 0.2$ ns. Clock skew is $T_{skew} = 0.1$ ns. What is the minimum clock frequency at which the design functions correctly?

### Solution

**Step 1: Use the setup slack formula**

$$
T_{slack}^{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{route} - T_{su} + T_{skew}
$$

**Step 2: Set slack = 0 for minimum frequency operation**

$$
0 = T_{clk} - 0.4 - 3 - 0.2 - 0.5 + 0.1
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_{clk}$**

$$
T_{clk} = 0.4 + 3 + 0.2 + 0.5 - 0.1 = 4.0 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to frequency**

$$
f_{clk} = \frac{1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{4.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 250 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{clk,min} = 250 \text{ MHz}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Clock Input Distribution Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Crystal Oscillator 50 MHz] --> B[FPGA Clock Input Pin MRCC]
    B --> C[IBUFG Input Global Buffer]
    C --> D{BUFG Global Buffer}
    D --> E[MMCM PLL Block]
    D --> F[Global Clock Spine H Tree]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Region 1 FFs]
    F --> H[Region 2 FFs]
    F --> I[Region 3 FFs]
    F --> J[Region 4 FFs]
    G --> K[Sequential Logic Output]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Clock Input Flow — Detailed Architectural View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Source]
        XTAL[Crystal Oscillator]
    end

    subgraph FPGA[FPGA Chip]
        direction TB
        PIN[Clock Pin]
        IBUF[IBUFG Buffer]
        BUFR[Regional Buffer BUFR]
        BUFG[Global Buffer BUFG]
        MMCM[MMCM PLL Tile]
        SPINE[Global Clock Spine]
        F1[FF Region A]
        F2[FF Region B]
        F3[FF Region C]
        F4[FF Region D]
    end

    XTAL -->|PCB trace| PIN
    PIN --> IBUF
    IBUF --> BUFG
    IBUF --> BUFR
    BUFG --> MMCM
    MMCM --> SPINE
    BUFG --> SPINE
    SPINE --> F1
    SPINE --> F2
    SPINE --> F3
    SPINE --> F4
```

## 4.3 Clock Signal Waveform (Visual Timing Reference)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Clock Period Tclk] --> B[High Pulse Th]
    A --> C[Low Pulse Tl]
    B --> D[Rise Time tr]
    C --> E[Fall Time tf]
    D --> F[Setup Window Tsu]
    E --> G[Hold Window Th]
    F --> H[Data Valid]
    G --> H
```

## 4.4 Clock Skew vs Clock Jitter Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SKEW[Clock Skew Spatial Variation]
        S1[FF1 receives clock at t=0 ns]
        S2[FF2 receives clock at t=0.2 ns]
        S1 -.->|skew 200 ps| S2
    end

    subgraph JIT[Clock Jitter Temporal Variation]
        J1[Edge 1 at ideal t]
        J2[Edge 2 at ideal t + jitter]
        J3[Edge 3 at ideal t - jitter]
        J1 --> J2
        J2 --> J3
    end
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a Clock Input pin in an FPGA. List any two key electrical characteristics that distinguish a clock input pin from a regular I/O pin.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Clock Input pin** in an FPGA is a dedicated physical pin used to receive an external periodic timing signal that serves as the global reference for all sequential operations within the device.

Two key distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Connection to Global Clock Buffers** — Clock pins connect directly to high-fanout buffers (BUFG/IBUFG) and the global clock spine, unlike regular I/Os which route through generic interconnect.
2. **Support for Differential Signaling** — Clock pins typically support LVDS/LVPECL differential standards for noise immunity, whereas regular I/Os may not.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: What is the role of an MMCM/PLL block in an FPGA clock distribution network?**

**Model Answer:**

An **MMCM (Mixed-Mode Clock Manager)** or **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** in an FPGA is used for **clock conditioning and synthesis**. Its primary roles are:
1. **Frequency Multiplication/Division** — Generating internal clocks at multiples or fractions of the input frequency.
2. **Phase Shifting** — Producing phase-offset clocks (e.g., 90°, 180°, 270°) for DDR interfaces.
3. **Clock Deskewing** — Compensating for PCB and internal routing delays.
4. **Jitter Filtering** — Cleaning noisy input clocks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts** worth **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a clock input path in an FPGA, starting from the external oscillator to the global clock distribution network. Discuss the role of IBUFG and BUFG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sequential circuit operates at 100 MHz. The flip-flops used have $T_{su} = 0.6$ ns, $T_{h} = 0.4$ ns, and $T_{cq} = 0.5$ ns. The combinational path delay is $T_{comb} = 4$ ns, and the clock skew between source and destination FFs is 0.15 ns. Determine:
- (i) The setup time slack
- (ii) The maximum combinational delay the design can tolerate without setup violation **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Architecture Explanation (7 Marks)

The clock input path in an FPGA consists of the following stages:

**1. External Oscillator (1 Mark)**
A crystal oscillator generates a stable periodic waveform (e.g., 50 MHz). This signal is routed via a controlled-impedance PCB trace to a dedicated clock pin of the FPGA (e.g., Xilinx MRCC pin).

**2. Input Global Buffer — IBUFG (2 Marks)**
The clock pin is connected to an **IBUFG** which converts the external I/O standard (LVCMOS33, LVDS, etc.) to the FPGA's internal core voltage level. It also provides impedance matching.

**3. Global Buffer — BUFG (2 Marks)**
The output of the IBUFG drives a **BUFG**, a high-fanout buffer that distributes the clock to the entire device via a low-skew H-tree topology. The BUFG ensures that all flip-flops see the clock edge within ~100 ps of each other.

**4. Optional MMCM/PLL (1 Mark)**
For frequency synthesis or phase shifting, the clock is routed to an MMCM/PLL block.

**5. Distribution to FFs (1 Mark)**
The clock reaches every sequential element with minimal skew and jitter.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Oscillator → Clock Pin → IBUFG → BUFG → MMCM/PLL → Global Spine → All FFs
```

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $f_{clk} = 100$ MHz ⇒ $T_{clk} = 10$ ns
- $T_{su} = 0.6$ ns, $T_{h} = 0.4$ ns, $T_{cq} = 0.5$ ns
- $T_{comb} = 4$ ns, $T_{skew} = 0.15$ ns

**Part (i): Setup Slack Calculation (3 Marks)**

Using the setup slack formula:

$$
T_{slack}^{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{su} + T_{skew}
$$

$$
T_{slack}^{setup} = 10 - 0.5 - 4 - 0.6 + 0.15
$$

$$
T_{slack}^{setup} = 5.05 \text{ ns}
$$

Since $T_{slack}^{setup} > 0$, the design has **positive setup margin** and works correctly.

**Part (ii): Maximum Combinational Delay (4 Marks)**

For setup slack = 0:

$$
0 = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb}^{max} - T_{su} + T_{skew}
$$

$$
T_{comb}^{max} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{su} + T_{skew}
$$

$$
T_{comb}^{max} = 10 - 0.5 - 0.6 + 0.15
$$

$$
T_{comb}^{max} = 9.05 \text{ ns}
$$

The maximum combinational delay the design can tolerate without setup violation is **9.05 ns**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concepts of clock skew and clock jitter. How do they impact FPGA design timing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An FPGA receives a 100 MHz clock on its clock input pin. The PLL is configured with $M = 8$, $D = 2$, and an output divider $O = 4$. Calculate:
- (i) The VCO frequency
- (ii) The output frequency
- (iii) The time equivalent of a 45° phase shift at the output **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Clock Skew vs Clock Jitter (7 Marks)

**Clock Skew (3 Marks):**
Clock skew is the **spatial difference** in clock arrival times at two different flip-flops on the same chip. It arises due to:
- Differences in routing wire lengths
- Buffer delays in the clock tree
- Load mismatches

**Impact on design:**
- Positive skew (destination clock arrives later) **helps setup** but **harms hold**.
- Negative skew **harms setup** but **helps hold**.

**Clock Jitter (3 Marks):**
Clock jitter is the **temporal variation** of the clock edge from its ideal position. It is caused by:
- PLL/MMCM phase noise
- Power supply noise
- Thermal effects
- Crosstalk from adjacent signals

**Impact on design:**
- Reduces the effective timing margin.
- Limits the maximum achievable frequency.
- Must be budgeted in timing closure.

**Comparison (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Skew | Jitter |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Spatial | Temporal |
| Cause | Routing | Noise/PLL |
| Mitigation | Careful clock tree design | Proper decoupling, PLL filtering |

---

#### Part (b) — PLL Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f_{in} = 100$ MHz, $M = 8$, $D = 2$, $O = 4$

**Part (i): VCO Frequency (2 Marks)**

$$
f_{VCO} = f_{in} \cdot \frac{M}{D} = 100 \cdot \frac{8}{2} = 400 \text{ MHz}
$$

> **Note:** In Xilinx MMCMs, $f_{VCO}$ must typically be ≥ 600 MHz. Therefore, in a real design, this configuration would be invalid — but the calculation methodology is the exam point.

**Part (ii): Output Frequency (2 Marks)**

$$
f_{out} = \frac{f_{VCO}}{O} = \frac{400}{4} = 100 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Part (iii): 45° Phase Shift (3 Marks)**

The output clock period is:

$$
T_{out} = \frac{1}{f_{out}} = \frac{1}{100 \text{ MHz}} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

The time equivalent of 45°:

$$
t_{phase} = \frac{T_{out}}{360°} \cdot 45° = \frac{10}{360°} \cdot 45° = 1.25 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{phase} = 1.25 \text{ ns}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Skew with Jitter** — Skew is *spatial* (between FFs); Jitter is *temporal* (edge variation). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Skew Sign in Setup Slack** — Many students write the formula as $T_{slack} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{su}$ without the skew term. Always include $T_{skew}$!
>
> 3. **Wrong Units** — Using MHz where ns is required (or vice versa). Always show unit conversions explicitly.
>
> 4. **Mis-identifying Clock Pins** — Not all FPGA pins can accept clocks. Only dedicated **MRCC/SRCC** pins (Xilinx) or **CLK** pins (Intel) are valid clock inputs. Routing a clock to a regular I/O pin causes timing failure.
>
> 5. **Skipping Block Diagrams** — In 7-mark architecture questions, KTU examiners expect a **labeled block diagram**. Omitting it loses at least 2 marks.
>
> 6. **VCO Range Violation** — When solving PLL problems, students often forget to check if $f_{VCO}$ lies in the valid range of the device. Always verify.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Clock Input**

### Core Concepts
- A **Clock Input** is a dedicated FPGA pin receiving an external periodic signal used as the global timing reference.
- Clock inputs are routed through **IBUFG → BUFG → Global Spine → FFs**.
- **MMCM/PLL** blocks allow frequency synthesis, phase shifting, and jitter filtering.
- Clock pins support differential standards (LVDS, LVPECL) for noise immunity.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$
- $T_{slack}^{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{comb} - T_{su} + T_{skew}$
- $f_{out} = f_{in} \cdot \dfrac{M}{D \cdot O}$
- $P_{clk} = C_{load} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$

### Distinctions
- **Skew** = spatial; **Jitter** = temporal
- **Setup slack > 0** ⇒ design works; **Hold slack > 0** ⇒ no hold violation
- Differential clocks → better noise immunity, higher frequencies
- Single-ended clocks → simpler PCB, lower cost

### Design Best Practices
- Use **dedicated clock pins** (MRCC/SRCC) — never route clocks through regular I/O.
- Connect unused clock pins to ground or leave them properly terminated.
- Use **clock buffers** (BUFG) for high-fanout clock nets.
- Verify **PLL/MMCM VCO frequency** is within the device's valid range.
- Apply **proper clock constraints** in the SDC/XDC timing file.

### KTU High-Yield Keywords
Clock Input Pin • IBUFG • BUFG • MMCM • PLL • Clock Skew • Clock Jitter • Setup Time • Hold Time • Global Clock Spine • H-Tree Distribution • Duty Cycle • Phase Shift • Frequency Synthesis

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Timing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **FPGA-Based System Design**, **Timing** refers to the rigorous analysis and verification of the temporal (chronological) behavior of digital signals as they propagate through combinational and sequential logic primitives, interconnect resources (routing), and clock distribution networks within a programmable logic device. It encompasses the evaluation of **propagation delays**, **setup and hold time constraints**, **clock skew**, **clock jitter**, and **slack** to guarantee that the design meets its operational frequency requirements under all specified Process, Voltage, and Temperature (PVT) corners. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus** > Timing is the *gatekeeper* of functional correctness in synchronous digital systems. A design that simulates correctly but violates a timing constraint will *fail in hardware* — this is the fundamental motivation for Static Timing Analysis (STA) over dynamic simulation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **relay race** where runners (data signals) must pass a baton (data bit) within a precisely timed handoff zone, synchronized to a starting pistol (clock edge). - The **Setup Time** is like the minimum distance the runner must be *near* the next runner *before* the pistol fires. - The **Hold Time** is the minimum distance the runner must *stay* near the receiver *after* the pistol fires. - The **Propagation Delay** is how long the runner takes to traverse their leg of the track. - The **Clock Skew** is the unfair situation where runners in lane 1 hear the pistol slightly earlier or later than runners in lane 8. - The **Slack** is the *cushion time* — how much spare time remains before a violation occurs (positive slack = safe, negative slack = failure). If the runner arrives too early or too late, the baton is dropped — and in an FPGA, this means **setup or hold violation → metastability → system failure**. ## 1.3 Why Timing Matters in FPGAs - **Pre-fabrication verification**: FPGA vendors (Xilinx, Intel/Altera, Lattice, Microchip/Microsemi) provide timing models that allow accurate delay prediction *before* silicon exists for the user. - **Speed grading**: Each FPGA device has a speed grade (e.g., **-1, -2, -3** for Xilinx 7-series), representing worst-case delay characteristics. - **Compile-time closure**: Unlike ASICs, FPGAs use *place-and-route* tools that iteratively adjust layout to meet timing — this iterative process is called **timing closure**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics** in FPGA timing: > - **ns (nanosecond, 10⁻⁹ s)** — typical unit of delay > - **MHz (megahertz, 10⁶ Hz)** — unit of operating frequency > - **°C (degrees Celsius)** — temperature corner (commercial: 0–85°C, industrial: -40–100°C) > - **V (Volt)** — voltage corner (nominal V_ccint, e.g., **1.0 V** for modern FPGAs) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Timing Analysis Timing analysis in FPGAs is decomposed into the following logical layers: ### 2.1.1 Component-Level Delays - **Intrinsic Gate Delay (t_gate)**: Delay of a single LUT, flip-flop, or hard block (BRAM, DSP). - **Interconnect Delay (t_wire)**: Delay of programmable routing switches and metal segments. - **Net Delay (t_net)**: Sum of routing + parasitic capacitance loading. ### 2.1.2 Path-Level Delay Accumulation A timing path is a directed chain from a *launch* element (typically a flip-flop clocked at edge *n*) through combinational logic to a *capture* element (flip-flop clocked at edge *n+1* or later). The total path delay is: $$T_{path} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{gate,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} t_{wire,j}$$ ### 2.1.3 Clock Distribution Concepts - **Clock Skew (t_skew)**: The maximum difference in arrival time of a clock edge at two different flip-flops driven by the same clock source. - **Clock Jitter (t_jitter)**: The cycle-to-cycle variation in clock period from the ideal nominal period. - **Clock Uncertainty (t_uncertainty)**: A STA term combining skew + jitter + margin. ### 2.1.4 Constraint Types - **Setup Check (max-delay analysis)**: Verifies the data arrives *before* the next active clock edge. - **Hold Check (min-delay analysis)**: Verifies the data remains stable *after* the current active clock edge. - **Recovery/Removal Checks**: For asynchronous reset signals. - **IO Timing Constraints**: For external device interfacing. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | Maximum Operating Frequency | $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk(min)}}$ | MHz | | Setup Slack | $Slack_{setup}$ | $T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{logic} - T_{net} - T_{setup} + t_{skew}$ | ns | | Hold Slack | $Slack_{hold}$ | $T_{cq} + T_{logic} + T_{net} - T_{hold} - t_{skew}$ | ns | | Critical Path Delay | $T_{critical}$ | Maximum of all $T_{path}$ values | ns | | Data Arrival Time | $T_{arrival}$ | $T_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{data}$ | ns | | Data Required Time | $T_{required}$ | $T_{capture} + T_{clk} - T_{setup}$ | ns | | Clock Period | $T_{clk}$ | $1 \div f_{clk}$ | ns | | Clock-to-Q Delay | $T_{cq}$ | Vendor-specified (typ. 0.1–0.5 ns for FPGAs) | ns | | Setup Time | $T_{setup}$ | Vendor-specified (typ. 0.05–0.2 ns) | ns | | Hold Time | $T_{hold}$ | Vendor-specified (typ. 0.05–0.2 ns) | ns | | Power Dissipation (Dynamic) | $P_{dyn}$ | $\alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | W | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility**: These formulas are used in **Synopsys Design Constraints (SDC)** files to instruct the FPGA synthesis tool (Vivado, Quartus, Diamond) on the desired performance targets. A designer writing `create_clock -period 10 [get_ports clk]` is essentially specifying $T_{clk} = 10$ ns (i.e., **100 MHz**). ## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Timing - **Why**: Even with correct logic, a signal that arrives too late causes the next flip-flop to sample stale or transitioning data — known as **metastability**. This creates unpredictable outputs, data corruption, or system crashes. - **How**: Static Timing Analysis (STA) walks *every* logical path in the design (without test vectors), computes the worst-case arrival and required times, and reports a slack value. The tool then guides the place-and-route engine to optimize failing paths through techniques like **logic replication, retiming, pipelining, and floorplanning**. ## 2.4 FPGA-Specific Timing Resources Modern FPGAs include dedicated hardware to *help* timing closure: - **Carry Chains (CARRY4/CARRY8)**: Dedicated fast paths for arithmetic (1-bit delay per stage). - **DSP Slices**: Pipelined multiply-accumulate with predictable timing. - **Block RAM (BRAM)**: Synchronous memory with registered outputs. - **Clock Management Tiles (CMT / MMCM / PLL)**: Generate low-skew, low-jitter derived clocks. - **Global Clock Buffers (BUFG)**: Distribute clocks with minimum skew across the die. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Setup Slack Equation We start from first principles, considering a simple **register-to-register** path. ### Step 1: Define the Launch Event The launching flip-flop (FF1) receives the active clock edge at time $T_{launch}$. After an inherent delay called the **clock-to-Q delay** ($T_{cq}$), the new data value appears at the Q output of FF1. $$T_{data\_out\_FF1} = T_{launch} + T_{cq}$$ ### Step 2: Traverse the Combinational Logic and Routing The data then passes through a chain of $N$ logic levels and $M$ wire segments. Each level adds its own intrinsic delay. $$T_{arrival} = T_{launch} + T_{cq} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{logic,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} t_{wire,j}$$ We can compact this by letting $T_{data} = \sum t_{logic,i} + \sum t_{wire,j}$. $$T_{arrival} = T_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{data}$$ ### Step 3: Define the Capture Event The capturing flip-flop (FF2) needs the data to be stable *before* its next active clock edge. The next edge occurs at $T_{capture}$, which is exactly one clock period after the launch edge (in single-clock-domain analysis, ignoring skew for the moment). $$T_{required} = T_{launch} + T_{clk} - T_{setup}$$ This is because data must arrive $T_{setup}$ *before* the next clock edge at the capture register. ### Step 4: Account for Clock Skew If the clock arrives at FF2 *earlier* than at FF1, FF2's window is shifted. This is a **negative skew** from the data's perspective, *helping* setup. Conversely, late arrival of clock at FF2 hurts setup. Define $t_{skew} = T_{capture} - T_{launch}$ (one period in ideal case, but adjusted by skew in real systems). $$T_{required} = T_{launch} + T_{clk} + t_{skew} - T_{setup}$$ ### Step 5: Compute Setup Slack Slack is defined as the *difference* between the time data is needed and the time it actually arrives. Positive slack = safe. $$Slack_{setup} = T_{required} - T_{arrival}$$ Substituting: $$Slack_{setup} = \left(T_{launch} + T_{clk} + t_{skew} - T_{setup}\right) - \left(T_{launch} + T_{cq} + T_{data}\right)$$ The $T_{launch}$ terms cancel: $$Slack_{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{data} - T_{setup} + t_{skew}$$ This is the canonical KTU setup slack equation. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Hold Slack Equation The hold check ensures data is not changed *too soon* after the active clock edge of the capturing register. ### Step 1: Data Arrival at Capture Register (Hold Perspective) In hold analysis, we are checking the *same* clock edge — the data launched by this edge must not propagate fast enough to violate the hold requirement of the *same* edge at the destination. $$T_{arrival,hold} = T_{cq} + T_{data}$$ (Here the launch and capture occur on the same edge, so launch time cancels.) ### Step 2: Required Time for Hold The data must remain stable until at least $T_{hold}$ after the capture edge. $$T_{required,hold} = t_{skew} + T_{hold}$$ ### Step 3: Hold Slack $$Slack_{hold} = T_{arrival,hold} - T_{required,hold}$$ $$Slack_{hold} = T_{cq} + T_{data} - T_{hold} - t_{skew}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice the Key Difference**: Setup slack is improved by *larger* clock periods and is independent of clock frequency scaling. Hold slack is *independent* of clock period — it is a pure delay-comparison check. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem**: A register-to-register path in a Xilinx Artix-7 FPGA has the following characteristics: - Clock period $T_{clk} = 10$ ns (i.e., **100 MHz**) - $T_{cq} = 0.15$ ns - $T_{data}$ (logic + routing) = 7.85 ns - $T_{setup} = 0.10$ ns - $T_{hold} = 0.10$ ns - $t_{skew} = +0.20$ ns (clock arrives at capture FF *later* — hurts setup) **Compute Setup and Hold Slack**. ### Setup Slack Calculation: $$Slack_{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{data} - T_{setup} + t_{skew}$$ $$Slack_{setup} = 10 - 0.15 - 7.85 - 0.10 + 0.20$$ $$Slack_{setup} = 10.20 - 8.10 = 2.10 \text{ ns}$$ Positive → **PASS** ✓ ### Hold Slack Calculation: $$Slack_{hold} = T_{cq} + T_{data} - T_{hold} - t_{skew}$$ $$Slack_{hold} = 0.15 + 7.85 - 0.10 - 0.20$$ $$Slack_{hold} = 8.00 - 0.30 = 7.70 \text{ ns}$$ Positive → **PASS** ✓ ## 3.4 Maximum Frequency Derivation For setup slack to be $\geq 0$: $$T_{clk(min)} = T_{cq} + T_{data} + T_{setup} - t_{skew}$$ $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk(min)}}$$ For our example: $$T_{clk(min)} = 0.15 + 7.85 + 0.10 - 0.20 = 7.90 \text{ ns}$$ $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{7.90 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 126.6 \text{ MHz}$$ The design can comfortably run at **100 MHz** with **2.10 ns of timing margin**. ## 3.5 Pipelining for Timing Improvement Adding a pipeline register halves the combinational depth: $$T_{data,new} = \frac{T_{data,old}}{2}$$ This approximately *doubles* the $f_{max}$, at the cost of **one clock cycle of latency** — a fundamental speed-area-latency tradeoff in FPGA design. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level FPGA Timing Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB clk_src[CLOCK SOURCE
External Crystal or Oscillator] --> clk_pin[FPGA CLOCK INPUT PIN] clk_pin --> bufg[BUFG GLOBAL BUFFER
Low Skew Distribution] bufg --> ff1[LAUNCH FLIP FLOP FF1
Has Tcq delay] bufg --> ff2[CAPTURE FLIP FLOP FF2
Has Tsetup and Thold windows] ff1 --> logic[COMBINATIONAL LOGIC
LUTs DSPs BRAMs] logic --> route[INTERCONNECT ROUTING
Programmable Switches] route --> ff2 bufg -.Clock Skew.-> ff2 logic -.Critical Path.-> route ff1 -.Setup Check.-> ff2 ff1 -.Hold Check.-> ff2 sdc[SDC CONSTRAINTS FILE
create_clock and set_input_delay] --> sta[STATIC TIMING ANALYZER] sta --> slack[SLACK REPORT
Positive Means Pass] slack --> closure[TIMING CLOSURE
Iterative P and R Optimization] ``` ## 4.2 Timing Path Classification Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ALL TIMING PATHS IN FPGA] --> B[REGISTER TO REGISTER PATH] A --> C[INPUT TO REGISTER PATH] A --> D[REGISTER TO OUTPUT PATH] A --> E[INPUT TO OUTPUT PATH] A --> F[CLOCK PATH] B --> B1[Tcq plus Tdata plus Tsetup check] C --> C1[Tinput_delay plus Tdata plus Tsetup check] D --> D1[Tcq plus Tdata plus Toutput_delay check] E --> E1[Combinational only Pure delay check] F --> F1[Skew Jitter Uncertainty analysis] ``` ## 4.3 Setup vs Hold Timing Window Diagram ```mermaid gantt title Setup and Hold Time Windows Relative to Clock Edge dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Clock Edge at Capture FF Clock Edge Event :a1, 0, 1 section Setup Window Setup Time Required Before Edge :crit, 1, 3 section Hold Window Hold Time Required After Edge :crit, 4, 3 section Data Validity Data Must Be Stable Here :active, 1, 6 ``` ## 4.4 SDC Constraint Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD user[DESIGNER WRITES RTL
VHDL or Verilog] --> synth[SYNTHESIS TOOL
Generates Netlist] sdcfile[SDC FILE
Timing Constraints] --> synth synth --> pnr[PLACE AND ROUTE TOOL
Vivado or Quartus] pnr --> sta[STATIC TIMING ANALYSIS] sta --> report[TIMING REPORT
WNS WHS TPS] report --> fail{Qualify All Slack Positive} fail -- No --> pnr fail -- Yes --> bitstream[BITSTREAM GENERATION
Programming File Output] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define setup time and hold time with respect to a flip-flop. Why are both checks necessary in synchronous digital design?** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: **Setup Time (1 Mark)**: Setup time is the minimum time interval *before* the active clock edge during which the data input must remain stable. If the data changes within this window, the flip-flop may enter a metastable state. **Hold Time (1 Mark)**: Hold time is the minimum time interval *after* the active clock edge during which the data input must remain stable. Violating hold time causes the flip-flop to capture incorrect or oscillating data. **Why Both Checks Are Necessary (1 Mark)**: Setup time ensures the *next* clock cycle correctly samples the intended data (long-path/max-delay check). Hold time ensures the *current* cycle's data is not corrupted by early transitions of the *next* cycle's data (short-path/min-delay check). Both checks together guarantee the flip-flop operates deterministically under all PVT conditions. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Differentiate between clock skew and clock jitter. How does each affect timing closure in FPGAs?** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: **Clock Skew (1 Mark)**: Clock skew is the *spatial* (deterministic) difference in clock arrival time between two flip-flops driven by the same clock source. It arises from unequal path lengths in the clock distribution network. **Clock Jitter (1 Mark)**: Clock jitter is the *temporal* (random) variation in clock edge timing from cycle to cycle relative to the ideal period. It arises from PLL/MMCM noise, supply noise, and thermal effects. **Effect on Timing (1 Mark)**: Skew directly *subtracts* from setup margin and *adds* to hold margin — a systematic, layout-dependent effect. Jitter reduces the *effective* clock period available for data path delay, requiring the design to budget for the worst-case cycle variation. Both must be modeled in the `set_clock_uncertainty` SDC command. ## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the fundamental concepts of Static Timing Analysis (STA) in FPGAs. Describe the four major timing path classifications and the corresponding checks performed on each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a register-to-register path in a Xilinx Kintex-7 FPGA, the following parameters are reported by the synthesis tool: - $T_{clk} = 8$ ns - $T_{cq} = 0.18$ ns - Combinational logic delay $T_{logic} = 4.20$ ns - Routing delay $T_{net} = 2.95$ ns - $T_{setup} = 0.12$ ns - $T_{hold} = 0.08$ ns - Clock skew = +0.15 ns (capture clock arrives later than launch) Calculate: (i) Setup Slack, (ii) Hold Slack, (iii) Maximum achievable operating frequency $f_{max}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**: **Static Timing Analysis Overview (2 Marks)**: STA is a *vectorless* verification methodology that exhaustively analyzes *all* logical paths in a digital design to verify that timing constraints are met under worst-case PVT conditions. Unlike dynamic simulation, STA does not require testbenches and achieves near-100% coverage of structural paths. In FPGAs, STA is performed by vendor tools (Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus) using the pre-characterized delay models of the silicon primitives. **The Four Timing Path Classifications (5 Marks)**: 1. **Register-to-Register Path (1.5 Marks)**: The most common path. Data travels from the Q output of one flip-flop through combinational logic and routing to the D input of another flip-flop. Both *setup* (max-delay) and *hold* (min-delay) checks are performed across one (or more) clock cycles. This is the path the designer optimizes most aggressively. 2. **Input-to-Register Path (1.5 Marks)**: Data enters the FPGA through an input pin, passes through the input buffer (IBUF), and propagates to the D input of an internal flip-flop. The *setup* check uses the `set_input_delay` constraint to define the external device's timing characteristics. 3. **Register-to-Output Path (1.5 Marks)**: Data leaves a flip-flop's Q output, propagates through output logic, and exits through the output buffer (OBUF) to an external pin. The *setup* check uses the `set_output_delay` constraint to model the downstream device's timing requirements. 4. **Input-to-Output Path (0.5 Marks)**: A purely combinational path traversing the FPGA from an input pin to an output pin. It is checked against `set_max_delay` constraints since there is no clock relationship. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**: Given data: - $T_{clk} = 8$ ns - $T_{cq} = 0.18$ ns - $T_{logic} = 4.20$ ns - $T_{net} = 2.95$ ns - $T_{setup} = 0.12$ ns - $T_{hold} = 0.08$ ns - $t_{skew} = +0.15$ ns **Step 1: Compute total data path delay (1 Mark)**: $$T_{data} = T_{logic} + T_{net} = 4.20 + 2.95 = 7.15 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 2: Compute Setup Slack (2 Marks)**: $$Slack_{setup} = T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{data} - T_{setup} + t_{skew}$$ $$Slack_{setup} = 8 - 0.18 - 7.15 - 0.12 + 0.15$$ $$Slack_{setup} = 8.15 - 7.45 = 0.70 \text{ ns}$$ Positive → **Setup constraint met** ✓ **Step 3: Compute Hold Slack (2 Marks)**: $$Slack_{hold} = T_{cq} + T_{data} - T_{hold} - t_{skew}$$ $$Slack_{hold} = 0.18 + 7.15 - 0.08 - 0.15$$ $$Slack_{hold} = 7.33 - 0.23 = 7.10 \text{ ns}$$ Positive → **Hold constraint met** ✓ **Step 4: Compute $f_{max}$ (2 Marks)**: For $Slack_{setup} = 0$: $$T_{clk(min)} = T_{cq} + T_{data} + T_{setup} - t_{skew}$$ $$T_{clk(min)} = 0.18 + 7.15 + 0.12 - 0.15 = 7.30 \text{ ns}$$ $$f_{max} = \frac{1}{7.30 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 136.99 \text{ MHz}$$ The design operates reliably at the target **125 MHz** ($T_{clk} = 8$ ns) with a 0.70 ns setup margin. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the concept of clock skew and clock jitter in FPGA-based systems. Discuss the techniques used in modern FPGAs to minimize these effects and achieve timing closure. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete FPGA timing analysis workflow from RTL design to bitstream generation. List the major SDC constraints used in this workflow. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**: **Clock Skew (2 Marks)**: Clock skew is the *deterministic* difference in arrival time of a clock edge at two or more sequential elements. It is caused by: - Unequal clock routing path lengths - Different loading conditions on clock branches - Process variations across the die Skew has a *systematic* effect: it directly reduces the setup margin if capture FF receives the clock later than launch FF. Conversely, large positive skew can cause hold violations because it reduces the data path's effective guard time. **Clock Jitter (2 Marks)**: Clock jitter is the *random* cycle-to-cycle variation in clock period. Sources include: - PLL/MMCM phase-locked loop noise - Power supply noise (V_ccint ripple) - Thermal noise - Crosstalk from adjacent signals Jitter reduces the *effective* clock period by $\pm t_{jitter}$, shrinking the available setup time budget. **Techniques to Minimize Skew and Jitter (3 Marks)**: 1. **Dedicated Global Clock Networks (BUFG)**: FPGAs provide pre-routed, low-skew H-tree or spine clock distribution networks. Designers should route all high-fanout clocks through `BUFG` rather than general routing. 2. **Clock Management Tiles (MMCM/PLL)**: Mixed-Mode Clock Managers filter input jitter, multiply/divide frequencies, and provide phase-shifted outputs with sub-100 ps jitter. 3. **Clock Region Partitioning**: Modern FPGAs (UltraScale, Versal) are divided into clock regions; placing FFs in the same region minimizes skew. 4. **Balanced Clock Tree Synthesis**: The place-and-route tool balances clock tree buffers to equalize delays. 5. **Clock Buffer Constraints**: SDC commands like `set_clock_skew` and `set_clock_uncertainty` instruct the tool on acceptable skew budgets. 6. **Avoid Asynchronous Clock Domain Crossing**: Use FIFOs or synchronizers when crossing clock domains. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**: **Block Diagram (4 Marks)** — Refer to the **SDC Constraint Flow Architecture** in SECTION_4.4 of these notes, which depicts: RTL Design → Synthesis → Place and Route → STA → Timing Report → (Loop if fails) → Bitstream Generation. **Step-by-Step Workflow (2 Marks)**: 1. **Design Entry**: RTL is written in VHDL/Verilog. 2. **Synthesis**: RTL is converted to a gate-level netlist of FPGA primitives (LUTs, FFs, DSPs, BRAMs). 3. **SDC Constraint Application**: A separate `*.sdc` file specifies timing intent. 4. **Place and Route**: Logical primitives are mapped to physical sites on the die, and routing resources are assigned. 5. **Static Timing Analysis**: The tool calculates delays and slack for every path. 6. **Timing Closure Loop**: If slack is negative, the designer or tool modifies placement, constraints, or RTL and iterates. 7. **Bitstream Generation**: Once timing is clean, the configuration bitstream is generated for programming the FPGA. **Key SDC Constraints (1 Mark)**: - `create_clock`: Defines clock frequency and port - `create_generated_clock`: Defines derived clocks from PLLs - `set_input_delay` / `set_output_delay`: External device timing - `set_clock_uncertainty`: Models skew + jitter - `set_false_path`: Excludes non-functional paths - `set_multicycle_path`: Allows N-cycle paths > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: > - **Forgetting to define the clock constraint**: If you write RTL with a `clk` input but no `create_clock` directive, the synthesis tool will assume *no* timing requirement, and your design will silently fail in hardware. > - **Confusing setup and hold slack signs**: Setup slack = $T_{required} - T_{arrival}$. Hold slack = $T_{arrival} - T_{required}$. Do *not* use the same formula for both. > - **Ignoring clock skew direction**: A positive skew value *adds* to setup slack but *subtracts* from hold slack. Sign errors are the #1 cause of timing calculation mistakes in KTU exams. > - **Skipping the $T_{cq}$ term**: A common mistake is to omit $T_{cq}$ from the setup equation, leading to over-optimistic frequency estimates. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Setup Time** = data stable *before* clock edge; protects the *next* cycle. - **Hold Time** = data stable *after* clock edge; protects the *current* cycle. - **Setup Slack** = $T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{data} - T_{setup} + t_{skew}$ — must be **≥ 0**. - **Hold Slack** = $T_{cq} + T_{data} - T_{hold} - t_{skew}$ — must be **≥ 0**. - **Clock Skew** is deterministic and spatial; **Clock Jitter** is random and temporal. - **BUFG** provides low-skew global clock distribution in FPGAs. - **MMCM/PLL** provides low-jitter clock synthesis with phase alignment. - **STA** is vectorless and covers all paths; complement (not replace) dynamic simulation. - **Four Path Types**: Reg→Reg, Input→Reg, Reg→Output, Input→Output. - **Speed grades** (-1, -2, -3) reflect worst-case silicon delay — higher number = faster. - **Pipelining** adds registers to break long combinational paths, trading latency for higher $f_{max}$. - **SDC** (Synopsys Design Constraints) is the universal language of timing specification across FPGA vendors. - **Timing Closure** is an *iterative* loop between STA reports and place-and-route adjustments. - The relationship $T_{clk(min)} = T_{cq} + T_{data} + T_{setup} - t_{skew}$ defines the absolute speed limit of a path. - **Metastability** is the physical consequence of timing violations — undefined logic levels lasting for an unbounded time.

topics.title: Power Dissipation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Dissipation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Dissipation in an FPGA** is the total electrical energy converted into heat per unit time by all active and passive elements of the device, including the programmable logic blocks, configurable interconnect, clock distribution network, block RAMs (BRAMs), DSP slices, hard IP cores, and I/O buffers, while it operates at a given supply voltage, clock frequency, and switching activity.

Mathematically, the **Total Power Dissipation (P_total)** in a CMOS-based FPGA is universally expressed as:

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$$

Where:

- **P_static** = Leakage power dissipated even when the circuit is idle (no switching).
- **P_dynamic** = Switching power plus short-circuit power consumed during logic transitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern nanoscale FPGAs (e.g., 28 nm, 20 nm, 16 nm, 7 nm process nodes), static power accounts for **30% to 50%** of the total power budget, which is a major shift from older 90 nm / 65 nm nodes where dynamic power dominated (>90%).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "FPGA City" Analogy**
> Imagine an FPGA as a busy, modern smart city:
>
> - **Static Power** is the cost of keeping every streetlight, traffic camera, and water pump running 24×7, even at 3 a.m. when no cars are moving. You pay this *baseline electricity bill* no matter what.
> - **Dynamic Power** is the extra electricity used when cars actually drive through intersections and traffic lights actually switch. The faster the cars move (clock frequency) and the more lanes that are used (switching activity), the higher the fuel cost.
> - **I/O Power** is the electricity used at the city gates to load and unload goods from trucks entering or leaving.
> - **Short-Circuit Power** is the brief "sparks" of wasted current when both pull-up and pull-down transistors momentarily conduct during a logic transition — like a short glitch at a relay switch.

A smart city engineer (the FPGA designer) must minimize *all three* bills. Ignoring the baseline (static) power in modern processes is like ignoring your water bill — it eventually becomes the largest expense.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

The following **standard metrics** are universally used in KTU-evaluated FPGA power problems:

- **V_DD (or V_CCINT)** : Core supply voltage, typically **0.85 V – 1.2 V** in modern FPGAs.
- **V_CCO** : I/O supply voltage, typically **1.2 V, 1.5 V, 1.8 V, 2.5 V, or 3.3 V**.
- **f_clk** : Clock frequency, typically **50 MHz – 500 MHz** for high-performance FPGAs.
- **α (alpha)** : Switching activity factor, ranging from **0 to 1** (0 = never toggles, 1 = toggles every cycle).
- **C_L** : Load capacitance, typically **fF to pF** per node.
- **I_leak** : Leakage current per transistor, typically **nA to µA** at 25 °C.
- **Unit of Power** : **Watts (W)** or **milliwatts (mW)**.
- **Thermal Resistance (θ_JA)** : **°C/W** — junction-to-ambient thermal resistance of the package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, always assume **room temperature (T = 25 °C)** unless the question explicitly states otherwise. Leakage current roughly **doubles for every 10 °C rise** in temperature.

---

## 1.4 Why Power Dissipation Matters in FPGAs

- **Thermal Limits**: Silicon junctions must remain below **125 °C – 150 °C** (military grade) or **100 °C** (commercial grade). Exceeding this destroys the device.
- **Battery Life**: In portable / aerospace / IoT applications, every mW matters.
- **Reliability**: Higher temperature → higher electromigration → shorter device lifetime (Arrhenius equation).
- **Packaging Cost**: A 2× power increase may force a more expensive BGA package with a heatsink.
- **Signal Integrity**: Power supply droop (IR drop) caused by high current can cause logic errors.
- **Cooling System**: Fans, heat sinks, and PCB copper pours all add BOM cost and size.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Power Dissipation in an FPGA

An FPGA consumes power from **four primary sources**, listed in hierarchical order of importance for modern processes:

### 2.1.1 Static (Leakage) Power — P_static

- Caused by sub-threshold conduction and gate-oxide tunneling currents in **idle CMOS transistors**.
- Exists as long as the FPGA is **powered ON**, regardless of whether logic is switching.
- **Dominant in**: always-on blocks, un-used logic, configuration memory, SRAM-based configuration cells.
- Strongly **process-dependent** and **temperature-dependent**.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic (Switching) Power — P_switch

- Caused by charging and discharging of load capacitances when logic nodes toggle.
- Proportional to **V_DD²** and to **f_clk** — hence the famous "V_DD squared law".
- **Dominant in**: high-speed data paths, clock trees, BRAM read/write operations.

### 2.1.3 Short-Circuit Power — P_sc

- Caused by momentary current flow directly from V_DD to GND through both NMOS and PMOS transistors during a logic transition.
- Occurs only for the **rise/fall time (τ)** of the input signal.
- Usually **5% – 15%** of dynamic power; can be ignored in back-of-envelope calculations.

### 2.1.4 I/O Power — P_IO

- Power consumed in output drivers driving off-chip capacitive loads (PCB traces, connector pins, downstream IC inputs).
- PCB trace capacitance is typically **5 – 15 pF**, which is **orders of magnitude larger** than on-chip capacitance (~fF).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Power Flow in an FPGA

1. **Power enters** the FPGA through V_CCINT and V_CCO pins.
2. **Distribution network** (metal layers, decoupling capacitors) delivers current to all logic and routing.
3. **Combinational logic (LUTs, muxes, FAs)** toggles at rate α·f, charging internal capacitances.
4. **Sequential elements (FFs, latches)** toggle on clock edges, consuming clock-tree power.
5. **Interconnect (programmable routing)** carries signals — its capacitance is charged/discharged per toggle.
6. **Block RAMs, DSP blocks, transceivers** consume power proportional to their activity.
7. **Leakage** drains a constant small current through every "off" transistor.
8. **All power is converted to heat** at the silicon die, which must be removed via the package and PCB.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master cheat sheet** for solving any KTU numerical on power dissipation. Memorize these six equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Static Power** | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage, $I_{leak}$ = total leakage current | W |
| 2 | **Switching (Dynamic) Power** | $P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = activity factor, $C_L$ = load capacitance, $f$ = toggle frequency | W |
| 3 | **Short-Circuit Power** | $P_{sc} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^{3} \cdot \tau \cdot f$ | $\beta$ = transistor gain, $V_{th}$ = threshold voltage, $\tau$ = rise/fall time | W |
| 4 | **Total Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = P_{switch} + P_{sc}$ | — | W |
| 5 | **Total FPGA Power** | $P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dyn} + P_{IO}$ | $P_{IO}$ = I/O power | W |
| 6 | **Thermal Power Density** | $P_{diss} = \dfrac{T_J - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$ | $T_J$ = junction temp, $T_A$ = ambient, $\theta_{JA}$ = thermal resistance | W |
| 7 | **Leakage Temp Scaling** | $I_{leak}(T) \approx I_{leak}(T_0) \cdot 2^{\,(T - T_0)/10\,°C}$ | Empirical doubling per 10 °C | A |

> [!NOTE]
> For **KTU 3-mark and 7-mark derivation questions**, Equation 2 (the $\alpha C V^2 f$ equation) is the **single most important formula** in the entire chapter. Master its derivation completely.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Xilinx Power Estimator (XPE)** and **Intel/Altera PowerPlay** are tools that implement these exact equations to give pre-silicon power budgets.
- **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** uses $P \propto V^2$ to drastically cut power in low-performance modes.
