- **Clock Gating** exploits the fact that $P_{switch} = 0$ when $\alpha = 0$.
- **Power-aware placement & routing** (PAR) tools in Vivado / Quartus minimize wire length, reducing $C_L$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Dynamic Switching Power Equation

**Goal:** Derive $P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ from first principles.

**Starting Point:** Consider a single CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. The output node toggles between logic 0 (0 V) and logic 1 ($V_{DD}$).

### Step 1 — Energy Required to Charge the Capacitor (0 → 1 transition)

When the output switches from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the PMOS transistor connects the load capacitance to $V_{DD}$. The energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{charge} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \cdot i \cdot dt = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \cdot dV$$

This evaluates to:

$$E_{charge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Why?** Because the voltage across the capacitor rises linearly from 0 to $V_{DD}$ as charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ is delivered. Half of the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ is stored on the capacitor; the other half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS channel resistance.

### Step 2 — Energy Stored on the Capacitor

The energy actually stored on $C_L$ at the end of the charge cycle is:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Dissipated During Discharge (1 → 0 transition)

When the output falls from $V_{DD}$ to 0, the NMOS transistor shorts the capacitor to ground. The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is **completely dissipated as heat** in the NMOS channel. The supply delivers **no additional energy** during this transition.

$$E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Total Energy Dissipated Per Full Cycle (0 → 1 → 0)

Adding the two halves:

$$E_{cycle} = E_{charge} + E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

### Step 5 — Energy Per Second = Power (for a clock of frequency f)

If the node toggles on **every** clock cycle, the number of full cycles per second is $f$. Therefore:

$$P_{switch} = E_{cycle} \cdot f = C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$$

### Step 6 — Incorporating the Switching Activity Factor α

In a **real circuit**, not every node toggles every clock cycle. We define the **switching activity factor** $\alpha$ as the fraction of clock cycles in which the node actually toggles. Multiplying:

$$\boxed{P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f}$$

**End of derivation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "1/2" cancellation trick:** Many students mistakenly leave a $\frac{1}{2}$ factor. Remember — the energy stored on $C$ is dissipated on the *next* transition, so the average power per full clock cycle is $C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$, not $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A FPGA design has 10,000 internal nodes. Each node has an average load capacitance of 50 fF. The supply voltage is 1.2 V, the clock frequency is 100 MHz, and the average switching activity is 0.15. Calculate the dynamic switching power.

**Solution:**

- Number of nodes: $N = 10{,}000$
- $C_L = 50 \text{ fF} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$
- $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$
- $f = 100 \text{ MHz} = 100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- $\alpha = 0.15$

Total load capacitance:

$$C_{total} = N \cdot C_L = 10{,}000 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F}$$

Substituting into the switching power equation:

$$P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

$$P_{switch} = 0.15 \times (5 \times 10^{-10}) \times (1.2)^{2} \times (100 \times 10^{6})$$

Evaluating $V_{DD}^{2} = 1.44 \text{ V}^{2}$:

$$P_{switch} = 0.15 \times (5 \times 10^{-10}) \times 1.44 \times (10^{8})$$

$$P_{switch} = 0.15 \times 5 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$P_{switch} = 1.08 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W} = 10.8 \text{ mW}$$

> **Answer:** $P_{switch} = 10.8 \text{ mW}$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Static Power with Temperature Scaling

**Problem:** An FPGA at 25 °C has a static leakage current of 8 µA at $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V. Calculate the static power at 25 °C and at 65 °C. Assume leakage doubles every 10 °C.

**Solution at 25 °C:**

$$P_{static}(25°C) = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = 1.0 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \text{ µW}$$

**Solution at 65 °C:**

Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 65 - 25 = 40°C$, which equals 4 doubling intervals.

$$I_{leak}(65°C) = I_{leak}(25°C) \times 2^{40/10} = 8 \text{ µA} \times 2^{4} = 8 \times 16 = 128 \text{ µA}$$

$$P_{static}(65°C) = 1.0 \times 128 \times 10^{-6} = 128 \text{ µW}$$

> **Answer:** $P_{static}$ increases from **8 µW to 128 µW** — a **16× rise** due to temperature alone.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Voltage & Frequency Scaling

| Design Choice | Effect on $P_{switch}$ | Practical Trade-off |
|---------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Reduce $V_{DD}$ from 1.2 V → 0.9 V** | $P$ drops to $(0.9/1.2)^2 = 0.5625$ → **44% reduction** | Slower transistors; must reduce $f$ |
| **Reduce $f$ from 200 MHz → 100 MHz** | $P$ drops by **50%** | Lower throughput |
| **Clock-gate an idle block (α: 0.1 → 0.01)** | $P$ drops by **90%** | Adds gating logic latency |
| **Reduce $C_L$ via shorter routes** | Linear reduction | Routing congestion |
| **Use sleep transistor (power gating)** | $P_{static} \to 0$ for that block | Wake-up latency (~µs) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Dissipation Sources — Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the **four major sources of power** in an FPGA, showing their inputs, contributions, and convergence into a single thermal output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[VDD Supply Pin] --> B1[Static Leakage Current]
    A1 --> B2[Dynamic Charging Path]
    A1 --> B3[Short Circuit Path]
    A1 --> B4[I/O Driver Path]

    B1 --> C1[Idle CMOS Transistors]
    B1 --> C2[Configuration SRAM Cells]
    B1 --> C3[Unused Logic and Routing]

    B2 --> D1[Combinational LUTs]
    B2 --> D2[Sequential Flip Flops]
    B2 --> D3[Clock Distribution Tree]
    B2 --> D4[BRAM and DSP Blocks]
    B2 --> D5[Programmable Interconnect]

    B3 --> E1[PMOS NMOS Simultaneous Conduction]
    B3 --> E2[Glitches and Hazards]

    B4 --> F1[Off Chip PCB Traces]
    B4 --> F2[Connector Pin Capacitance]
    B4 --> F3[Downstream IC Input Loads]

    C1 --> G1[Total Static Power Pstatic]
    C2 --> G1
    C3 --> G1

    D1 --> G2[Total Dynamic Power Pdynamic]
    D2 --> G2
    D3 --> G2
    D4 --> G2
    D5 --> G2

    E1 --> G2
    E2 --> G2

    F1 --> G3[Total I O Power PIO]
    F2 --> G3
    F3 --> G3

    G1 --> H[Total Ptotal in Silicon Die]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H

    H --> I1[Heat Sink]
    H --> I2[PCB Copper Pour]
    H --> I3[Fan or Forced Air]
    I1 --> J[Ambient Temperature]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

---

## 4.2 Power Reduction Techniques — Decision Flow

This diagram shows the **hierarchy of low-power design techniques** that an FPGA designer can apply, ordered by impact and ease of implementation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High FPGA Power Detected] --> B{Type of Power Dominant}
    B -->|Dynamic| C[Apply Dynamic Power Reduction]
    B -->|Static| D[Apply Static Power Reduction]
    B -->|I O| E[Apply I O Power Reduction]

    C --> C1[Clock Gating]
    C --> C2[Reduce Switching Activity Alpha]
    C --> C3[Operate at Lower VDD]
    C --> C4[Operate at Lower Frequency f]
    C --> C5[Use Pipelining to Reduce Glitches]
    C1 --> F[Power Reduced]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    C5 --> F

    D --> D1[Power Gating with Sleep Transistors]
    D --> D2[Use High Vth Transistors in Non Critical Paths]
    D --> D3[Disable Unused Blocks]
    D --> D4[Use Lower Process Node e g 28nm to 20nm]
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F

    E --> E1[Use Low Voltage I O Standards e g LVCMOS18]
    E --> E2[Reduce Number of Switching Outputs]
    E --> E3[Use Series Termination to Reduce Reflections]
    E --> E4[Use Differential Signaling for High Speed]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
```

---

## 4.3 Power Estimation Flow — Pre-Silicon vs Post-Silicon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HDL RTL Code] --> B[Synthesis Report]
    B --> C[Utilization and Toggle Rate]
    C --> D[Power Estimator Tool XPE or PowerPlay]
    D --> E[Pre Silicon Power Estimate]
    E --> F{Meets Power Budget}
    F -->|No| G[Optimization Pass]
    G --> A
    F -->|Yes| H[Place and Route]
    H --> I[Post Layout Simulation]
    I --> J[Accurate Power and Thermal Report]
    J --> K[Final Sign Off]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define static power dissipation in an FPGA. Why is it becoming more significant in modern FPGAs?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Static power dissipation is the power consumed by an FPGA due to leakage currents (sub-threshold conduction and gate-oxide tunneling) that flow even when the logic is not switching. It is given by $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$.
- **(1 mark)** It exists as long as the device is powered, independent of clock frequency or activity.
- **(1 mark)** In modern nanoscale FPGAs (28 nm, 20 nm, 16 nm and below), thinner gate oxides and shorter channel lengths dramatically increase leakage current. Hence static power now accounts for 30%–50% of total power, whereas in older 90 nm / 65 nm nodes dynamic power was >90% of the total.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. What is the switching activity factor (α) in dynamic power calculation? State the dynamic power equation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The switching activity factor α is the probability (or fraction) that a logic node toggles in a given clock cycle. Its value ranges from 0 (node never switches) to 1 (node switches every cycle). Typical average value in real designs is 0.1 to 0.3.
- **(1 mark)** The dynamic switching power equation is $P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$, where $C_L$ is the load capacitance and $f$ is the clock frequency.
- **(1 mark)** This shows that dynamic power is **quadratically proportional** to supply voltage, which is why voltage scaling is the most effective dynamic power reduction technique.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Derive the dynamic switching power equation $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$ for a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance. Explain each step clearly. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An FPGA design operates at 1.0 V, 200 MHz, with 20,000 internal nodes. The average load capacitance per node is 80 fF and the average switching activity is 0.20. Calculate: (i) the dynamic switching power, (ii) the dynamic power if voltage is reduced to 0.8 V, and (iii) the percentage reduction achieved. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$ **(7 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's expected answer mapping: **Step 1 (1 M)**, **Step 2 (1 M)**, **Step 3 (1 M)**, **Step 4 (1 M)**, **Step 5 (1 M)**, **Step 6 (1 M)**, **Final boxed equation (1 M)**.

**Step 1 — Consider a CMOS inverter with load $C_L$:** Assume the output node has a total load capacitance $C_L$ to ground, and the inverter is driven by a clock of frequency $f$.

**Step 2 — Energy to charge $C_L$ from 0 V to $V_{DD}$ (0→1 transition):**

$$E_{charge} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \cdot dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

The PMOS transistor delivers charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ from the supply. Half of the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ is stored on the capacitor, the other half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS on-resistance.

**Step 3 — Energy stored on the capacitor at the end of charge:**

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated during discharge (1→0 transition):**

The stored energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is **completely dissipated as heat** in the NMOS transistor. The supply delivers no additional energy during this transition.

$$E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Total energy dissipated per full cycle (0→1→0):**

$$E_{cycle} = E_{charge} + E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to average power (energy per second):**

If the node toggles on every cycle, the number of full cycles per second is $f$. Therefore:

$$P = E_{cycle} \cdot f = C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$$

**Final Step — Incorporate the activity factor α:**

In real designs, not every node toggles every cycle. Multiplying by the switching activity factor α:

$$\boxed{P = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The famous "1/2 cancellation":** The two halves $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ (one from charging, one from discharging) **add up to a full $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ per cycle** — there is no leftover $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in the final equation.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation **(7 Marks)**

**Given data:**

- $V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, $f = 200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- $N = 20{,}000$ nodes, $C_L = 80 \text{ fF} = 80 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$
- $\alpha = 0.20$

**(i) Dynamic power at 1.0 V:**

Total load capacitance:

$$C_{total} = N \cdot C_L = 20{,}000 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} = 1.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$$

Substitute into the switching power equation:

$$P_1 = \alpha \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

$$P_1 = 0.20 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-9}) \times (1.0)^{2} \times (200 \times 10^{6})$$

$$P_1 = 0.20 \times 1.6 \times 200 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_1 = 64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 64 \text{ mW}$$

**(2 marks for part i)**

**(ii) Dynamic power at 0.8 V (frequency unchanged):**

$$P_2 = 0.20 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-9}) \times (0.8)^{2} \times (200 \times 10^{6})$$

$$P_2 = 0.20 \times 1.6 \times 0.64 \times 200 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_2 = 40.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \approx 40.96 \text{ mW}$$

**(2 marks for part ii)**

**(iii) Percentage reduction:**

$$\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1} \times 100 = \frac{64 - 40.96}{64} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{23.04}{64} \times 100 = 36\%$$

**(2 marks for part iii)** (plus 1 mark for final boxed answer with units)

> **Final Answer:** (i) $P_1 = 64 \text{ mW}$, (ii) $P_2 = 40.96 \text{ mW}$, (iii) **36% reduction** in dynamic power by lowering $V_{DD}$ from 1.0 V to 0.8 V.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
>
> **(a)** Explain the various sources of power dissipation in an FPGA. List and describe the four main categories with their physical origin. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A modern 28 nm FPGA operates at 0.9 V core voltage with a total leakage current of 250 µA at 25 °C. The dynamic switching power at 25 °C is 80 mW. The device is deployed in a field application where the ambient temperature is 75 °C. Assuming leakage doubles every 10 °C and the dynamic power remains constant, calculate: (i) the static power at 25 °C and 75 °C, and (ii) the total power at 75 °C. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Sources of Power Dissipation **(7 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's expected answer mapping: **Static (2 M)**, **Dynamic switching (2 M)**, **Short circuit (1.5 M)**, **I/O power (1.5 M)**.

An FPGA has **four major sources** of power dissipation:

**1. Static (Leakage) Power — $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$:**
Caused by two physical mechanisms: (i) **sub-threshold conduction** — small current flowing from drain to source even when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, exponentially dependent on temperature; (ii) **gate-oxide tunneling** — electrons tunneling through the thin gate oxide. In modern nanoscale FPGAs, this dominates when the device is idle or in always-on blocks like configuration memory.

**2. Dynamic Switching Power — $P_{switch} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$:**
Caused by charging and discharging the load capacitances of internal nodes (LUTs, FFs, interconnect) every time they toggle. This is the **dominant** source in active high-speed designs. Quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$ makes voltage scaling the most effective reduction technique.

**3. Short-Circuit Power — $P_{sc}$:**
Caused by the brief period during a logic transition when both the PMOS pull-up and NMOS pull-down transistors conduct simultaneously, creating a direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND. Lasts for the rise/fall time $\tau$ of the input. Typically 5%–15% of dynamic power — can be reduced by ensuring sharp input edges.

**4. I/O Power — $P_{IO} = \alpha \cdot C_{L,off} \cdot V_{CCO}^{2} \cdot f$:**
Caused by output drivers charging **off-chip** capacitances such as PCB traces (5–15 pF), connector pins, and downstream IC inputs. The off-chip capacitance is **orders of magnitude** larger than on-chip capacitance, so even a few switching outputs can consume significant power. I/O voltage standards (LVCMOS18, LVDS, etc.) directly affect this term.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation with Temperature Scaling **(7 Marks)**

**Given data:**

- $V_{DD} = 0.9 \text{ V}$, $I_{leak}(25°C) = 250 \text{ µA} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$
- $P_{switch}(25°C) = 80 \text{ mW}$ (constant with temperature for this problem)
- $T_0 = 25°C$, $T_{new} = 75°C$, doubling every 10 °C

**(i) Static power at 25 °C:**

$$P_{static}(25°C) = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}(25°C) = 0.9 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{static}(25°C) = 225 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 225 \text{ µW} = 0.225 \text{ mW}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Static power at 75 °C:**

Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 75 - 25 = 50°C$ → 5 doubling intervals.

$$I_{leak}(75°C) = 250 \text{ µA} \times 2^{50/10} = 250 \text{ µA} \times 2^{5} = 250 \times 32 = 8000 \text{ µA} = 8 \text{ mA}$$

$$P_{static}(75°C) = 0.9 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 7.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 7.2 \text{ mW}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Total power at 75 °C:**

$$P_{total}(75°C) = P_{static}(75°C) + P_{switch}$$

$$P_{total}(75°C) = 7.2 \text{ mW} + 80 \text{ mW} = 87.2 \text{ mW}$$

**(2 marks for final calculation + 2 marks for statement showing static + dynamic = total)**

> **Final Answer:** (i) $P_{static}(25°C) = 0.225 \text{ mW}$, $P_{static}(75°C) = 7.2 \text{ mW}$, (ii) $P_{total}(75°C) = 87.2 \text{ mW}$. Notice the **32× rise** in leakage current due to temperature, increasing total power by ~9%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the activity factor α** — Many students write $P = C V^2 f$ and lose 1 mark. Always state "average dynamic power" with α included.
> 2. **Leaving a $\frac{1}{2}$ in the final dynamic power equation** — The $\frac{1}{2}$ factors from charge and discharge **add up to 1**. Do not write $\frac{1}{2} \alpha C V^2 f$ unless the question explicitly asks for energy per half-cycle.
> 3. **Confusing $V_{DD}$ with $V_{CCO}$** — $V_{DD}$ (or $V_{CCINT}$) is the **core** voltage, $V_{CCO}$ is the **I/O** voltage. They are different and used in different equations.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion of capacitance** — 1 fF = $10^{-15}$ F, 1 pF = $10^{-12}$ F. Slipping this up gives a power answer off by 1000×.
> 5. **Temperature scaling: forgetting exponent** — Leakage doubles *per 10 °C*. For 50 °C rise, multiply by $2^5 = 32$, not by 5.
> 6. **Not stating the assumption that $P_{switch}$ is constant with temperature** — In a real device it is also temperature-dependent (mobility changes), but KTU problems usually hold it constant unless told otherwise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision for KTU ESE — read this list 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Total Power = Static + Dynamic + I/O.** Always state this decomposition first.
- ☐ **Dynamic power equation** (the single most important formula): $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$.
- ☐ **Static power equation**: $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$.
- ☐ **Leakage current doubles every 10 °C rise** — for an $N$ × 10 °C rise, multiply by $2^N$.
- ☐ **Quadratic V_DD dependence** is the key insight — halving $V_{DD}$ cuts dynamic power to **one-fourth** (not half).
- ☐ **Four sources**: static leakage, dynamic switching, short-circuit, I/O — be ready to name all four with equations.
- ☐ **Activity factor α** ranges from 0 to 1; typical average is 0.1 – 0.3 in real designs.
- ☐ **Clock tree is the biggest dynamic power consumer** in synchronous FPGAs (~30%–40% of dynamic power).
- ☐ **Short-circuit power is usually negligible** (5%–15%) — can be ignored for back-of-envelope calculations.
- ☐ **Power reduction techniques** (in priority order): (1) Clock gating, (2) Voltage scaling, (3) Frequency scaling, (4) Power gating / sleep modes, (5) Reduce switching activity, (6) Reduce capacitive load via better routing, (7) Use low-power I/O standards.
- ☐ **Thermal equation**: $P = (T_J - T_A) / \theta_{JA}$ — needed if a heat-sink / thermal question appears.
- ☐ **Remember the "1/2 cancellation"** — full energy per cycle is $C_L V_{DD}^{2}$, not $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$.
- ☐ **Units check**: power in W, capacitance in F, frequency in Hz, voltage in V. Always convert fF, pF, MHz to SI units before calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmable Interconnect: Partitioning and Placement, Routing Resources, Delays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmable Interconnect: Partitioning and Placement, Routing Resources, Delays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Programmable Interconnect — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Programmable Interconnect** in an FPGA is the configurable network of **pre-fabricated metal wire segments**, **programmable switches (PIPs)**, and **multiplexers (Muxes)** that electrically connect the inputs and outputs of Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs), I/O blocks, and embedded hard cores (Block RAM, DSP, etc.) in a user-defined manner after fabrication.

The interconnect resources occupy **more than 70%** of the die area and contribute **more than 50%** of the total path delay in modern FPGAs, making them the single most critical determinant of system performance, area, and power.

The three sequential CAD-flow stages that govern the use of these resources are:

1. **Partitioning** — Decomposing a large circuit into smaller, technology-mapped sub-circuits.
2. **Placement** — Assigning each sub-circuit to a specific physical location on the FPGA fabric.
3. **Routing** — Selecting physical wire segments and switches to realize the required electrical connections.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Road Network" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a brand-new city built on a **grid of empty plots** with a complete network of pre-paved roads and **traffic-signal controllers at every intersection**, but the controllers are powered OFF.
> - The **plots** are the **CLBs / Logic Cells** (fixed locations, ready to host a function).
> - The **roads** are the **wire segments** (already laid out in metal).
> - The **traffic signals** are the **PIPs / Pass Transistors / Muxes** (programmable to either allow or block traffic).
> - **Partitioning** = deciding which "company" goes into which zone of the city.
> - **Placement** = pinning each company onto a specific plot.
> - **Routing** = turning on the right traffic signals to draw the shortest/fastest route between companies.
> - **Delay** = the actual travel time between two companies, depending on road length, number of red lights, and traffic load.

## 1.3 The Three CAD-Flow Sub-Problems (Why They Matter)

| Sub-Problem | Primary Goal | Direct Effect on FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| **Partitioning** | Balance logic across regions | Reduces inter-region wire congestion |
| **Placement** | Minimize total wire-length | Improves timing closure, lowers delay |
| **Routing** | Realize 100% connectivity with minimum delay | Determines final Fmax of the design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on understanding **why the interconnect dominates timing** — students are expected to draw and explain the Elmore delay model for a distributed RC wire.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Partitioning — Theory and Algorithms

**Partitioning** is the process of dividing a circuit netlist into $k$ balanced sub-sets such that the number of nets crossing between partitions (the **cut-size**) is minimized. For a 2-way partition of $n$ logic cells:

$$ \text{Cut-size} = \sum_{e \in E_{\text{cut}}} w(e) $$

where $w(e)$ is the weight (typically 1) of net $e$, and $E_{\text{cut}}$ is the set of edges (nets) having at least one terminal in each partition.

### 2.1.1 Kernighan–Lin (K-L) Algorithm

A **heuristic, iterative, bi-partitioning** algorithm based on a "swap-and-evaluate" policy:

- **Step 1:** Start with a random balanced initial partition of $n$ cells into groups A and B.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **gain** $g(v)$ of each unlocked cell $v$ as the difference between the number of nets leaving $v$'s partition and the number of nets becoming internal if $v$ is moved.
- **Step 3:** Iteratively swap the *unlocked* pair of cells $(a \in A,\, b \in B)$ that gives the **maximum positive or least negative gain**.
- **Step 4:** Lock the swapped cells, record the cumulative gain $G_k$ after the $k$-th swap, and continue until all cells are locked.
- **Step 5:** Find the swap step $k^*$ that maximizes $\sum_{i=1}^{k^*} G_i$, retain that partition, and unlock all cells for the next pass.
- **Step 6:** Repeat passes until the gain is zero or negative.

**Complexity:** $O(n^{2 \log n})$ per pass, worst-case $O(n^{3})$.

### 2.1.2 Fiduccia–Mattheyses (F-M) Improvement

The F-M algorithm is a **linear-time, single-cell move** refinement of K-L:

- Cells are stored in a **bucket list** indexed by gain value (range $\pm P_{\max}$).
- Only the **highest-gain movable cell** is moved at each step.
- After every move, gains of affected neighbours are updated incrementally.
- **Complexity:** $O(P)$ per pass, where $P$ is the number of pins, giving near-linear runtime — the de-facto industrial standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why partition?** A flat placement of millions of cells is computationally infeasible. Hierarchical partitioning reduces the problem to manageable sub-regions, mirroring the FPGA's **quadrant / clock-region** architecture.

---

## 2.2 Placement — Theory and Algorithms

**Placement** assigns each logic cell to a specific $(x,y)$ coordinate on the FPGA grid so as to **minimize the total estimated wire-length** while obeying physical constraints.

The **bounding-box wire-length** for a net $N$ with terminals at $(x_i, y_i)$ is:

$$ L_{\text{bb}}(N) = \max_i x_i - \min_i x_i \;+\; \max_i y_i - \min_i y_i $$

The total cost to minimize is:

$$ \Phi = \sum_{N \in \text{Nets}} L_{\text{bb}}(N) $$

### 2.2.1 Simulated Annealing (TimberWolf / VPR placer)

Modelled on the physical cooling of metals:

- Start with a high "temperature" $T$ — accepts almost all bad moves.
- Each iteration, perturb the placement (move one cell to a new location or swap two cells).
- Accept the move with probability:

$$ P(\Delta \Phi, T) = \begin{cases} 1, & \Delta \Phi \le 0 \\[4pt] \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{T} \right), & \Delta \Phi > 0 \end{cases} $$

- Lower $T$ geometrically: $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, where $\alpha \approx 0.95$.
- Stops when $T$ approaches a "freezing" threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** At high $T$, the placer "jiggles" freely, exploring bad regions to escape local minima. As $T$ cools, it commits to the best solution it found, mirroring crystal formation.

### 2.2.2 Force-Directed Placement

Models nets as **springs** and cells as **masses**. The attractive force on a cell $i$ from a net $N$ is:

$$ \vec{F}_{i,N}^{\text{att}} = k_N \cdot \bigl( \vec{r}_i - \vec{c}_N \bigr) $$

where $\vec{c}_N$ is the centroid of net $N$ and $k_N$ is a spring constant inversely proportional to the number of terminals. Equilibrium is reached when net forces are zero — the **minimum-energy** configuration.

---

## 2.3 Routing Resources — Physical Architecture

A modern FPGA routing fabric consists of:

1. **Wire Segments** of varying lengths:
   - **Single-length (length-1):** Connect adjacent switch matrices.
   - **Double-length (length-2):** Skip one switch matrix.
   - **Long lines (length-N, where $N$ spans the chip):** For clocks, global resets, and high-fanout nets.
2. **Connection Boxes (CB):** Connect CLB pins to wire segments.
3. **Switch Boxes (SB) / Switch Matrices:** Route signals between intersecting wires. Configured by **SRAM cells**.
4. **Programmable Interconnect Points (PIPs):** The atomic switching element — a **pass transistor** or **tri-state buffer** controlled by an SRAM bit.
5. **Multiplexers:** Mux trees used in newer architectures to drive CLB inputs, offering higher speed at the cost of area.

### 2.3.1 Routing Algorithm — PathFinder (Negotiated Congestion)

The **PathFinder** algorithm (used in VPR) performs **routing and rip-up iteratively**:

- Allow over-congestion in early iterations.
- After each routing pass, identify over-used nodes and **increase their cost**:

$$ \text{Cost}(n) = b_n + h_n \cdot p(n) $$

where $b_n$ is the base cost, $h_n$ is the historical congestion term, and $p(n)$ is the present congestion penalty.

- **Rip-up and re-route** using Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm with the new costs.
- Iterate until the present congestion $p(n) = 0$ for all $n$ — the **monotonic convergence** property.

---

## 2.4 Interconnect Delay — The Elmore RC Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The Elmore delay for a distributed RC line is **the most-asked formula** in Module 4 exams.

A wire of length $L$, width $w$, driven by a driver of resistance $R_d$ and loaded by a lumped capacitance $C_L$, is modelled as a distributed $\pi$- or T-network with **per-unit-length** parameters:

- $r$ = sheet resistance $\cdot$ (1 / width) $[\Omega/\mu\text{m}]$
- $c$ = oxide capacitance per unit length $[\text{F}/\mu\text{m}]$

For a wire uniformly divided into $N$ identical RC segments, the **Elmore delay** from input to the output of the $k$-th segment is:

$$ t_{pd,k} = R_d \cdot c \cdot L + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left[ \frac{r \cdot L}{N} \cdot c \cdot (L - \tfrac{iL}{N}) \right] $$

For a continuous distributed line ($N \to \infty$), the **Elmore delay to the far end** of an open-circuited, source-terminated line is:

$$ \boxed{\, t_{pd} = R_d \cdot c \cdot L + 0.5 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot L^{2} \,} $$

This shows the celebrated **quadratic dependence of delay on wire length** — the reason FPGAs use **segmented routing** and **repeaters (hex buffers)** to break long wires into smaller, faster pieces.

### 2.4.1 Optimal Buffer / Repeater Insertion

Inserting buffers of size $s$ every length $L_{\text{opt}}$ minimizes total delay. Classical result (Bakoglu):

$$ L_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 R_0}{r \cdot R_d}} \quad ; \quad s_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_d \cdot c_0}{r \cdot R_0}} $$

where $R_0$ is the buffer output resistance and $c_0$ its input capacitance. The minimum achievable delay becomes **linear in $L$**, not quadratic.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Variable | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{\text{bb}}(N) = (\max x - \min x) + (\max y - \min y)$ | Half-perimeter bounding-box wire-length of net $N$ | $\mu$m |
| 2 | $P(\Delta \Phi, T) = \exp(-\Delta \Phi / T)$ | Simulated-annealing acceptance probability | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, $\alpha \approx 0.95$ | Annealing cooling schedule | K (abstract) |
| 4 | $\text{Cost}(n) = b_n + h_n \cdot p(n)$ | PathFinder edge-cost function | dimensionless |
| 5 | $t_{pd} = R_d \cdot c \cdot L + 0.5 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot L^{2}$ | Elmore delay of a distributed RC line | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $L_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{2 R_0 / (r R_d)}$ | Optimal repeater spacing | m |
| 7 | $s_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{R_d c_0 / (r R_0)}$ | Optimal repeater size (drive strength) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Fanout} = C_L / C_{\text{in}}$ | Effective fanout of a net | unitless |
| 9 | $t_{pd} \approx \tau \cdot \text{Fanout} \cdot (1 + 0.7 \text{Fanout})$ | Logical-effort approximation for a chain | seconds |
| 10 | $\text{Wire Resistance} = R_{\square} \cdot L / w$ | Sheet-resistance-based wire R | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Elmore model is the workhorse for **static timing analysis (STA)** in every commercial FPGA tool (Vivado, Quartus, VPR). Without a fast delay estimator, iterative placement and routing of millions of nets would be impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Elmore Delay of a Distributed RC Wire

We model a wire of total length $L$ as **$N$ cascaded RC $\pi$-sections**, each of length $L/N$. The $i$-th section contributes a series resistance $r L / N$ and a shunt capacitance $c L / N$ to ground, plus the final load capacitance $C_L$.

The Elmore delay to the output of the $k$-th node is the **first moment of the impulse response**, which equals the sum over all capacitors of (resistance seen by that capacitor from the source) × (capacitor value).

**Step 1 — Resistances seen by each capacitor.**

Each shunt capacitor $C_i = c L / N$ at node $i$ is seen through:
- The driver resistance $R_d$,
- The series resistance of the wire from the driver to node $i$: $R_{\text{wire}}(i) = r \cdot i \cdot L / N$.

Hence the resistance seen by $C_i$ is:
$$ R_{\text{seen}}(i) = R_d + \frac{r \cdot i \cdot L}{N} $$

**Step 2 — Final load capacitor.**

The load $C_L$ is at the far end (node $N$), so:
$$ R_{\text{seen}}(N) = R_d + \frac{r \cdot L \cdot N}{N} = R_d + r L $$

**Step 3 — Elmore delay to the far end (node $N$).**

$$ t_{pd} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{\text{seen}}(i) \cdot C_i + R_{\text{seen}}(N) \cdot C_L $$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{pd} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( R_d + \frac{r \, i \, L}{N} \right) \left( \frac{c \, L}{N} \right) + \left( R_d + r L \right) C_L \\[6pt]
&= \frac{R_d \, c \, L}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 1 \;+\; \frac{r \, c \, L^{2}}{N^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} i \;+\; R_d \, C_L \;+\; r L \, C_L \\[6pt]
&= R_d \, c \, L \;+\; \frac{r \, c \, L^{2}}{N^{2}} \cdot \frac{N(N+1)}{2} \;+\; R_d \, C_L \;+\; r L \, C_L \\[6pt]
&= R_d \bigl( c L + C_L \bigr) + r \, C_L \, L + \frac{r \, c \, L^{2}}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Continuous limit $N \to \infty$ (with no lumped load $C_L = 0$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{pd} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \left[ R_d \, c \, L + \frac{r \, c \, L^{2}}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{N} \right) \right] \\[6pt]
&= R_d \, c \, L + 0.5 \, r \, c \, L^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the two terms:**
> - $R_d \cdot c \cdot L$ → **linear term** dominated by the **driver resistance** charging the **total wire capacitance**.
> - $0.5 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot L^{2}$ → **quadratic term** caused by the **distributed RC nature of the wire** — the *Why* behind long-wire timing closure failures in dense FPGAs.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Elmore Delay Calculation

**Problem:** A 5 mm wire in a 0.18 $\mu$m CMOS FPGA has the following parameters:
- $r = 0.08\ \Omega/\mu\text{m}$
- $c = 0.20\ \text{fF}/\mu\text{m}$
- $R_d = 200\ \Omega$ (driver output resistance)
- $C_L = 50\ \text{fF}$ (load at far end)

Compute the Elmore delay with and without $C_L$.

**Step 1 — Compute line totals.**

$$
L = 5\,\text{mm} = 5000\,\mu\text{m}, \quad r L = 0.08 \times 5000 = 400\,\Omega, \quad c L = 0.20 \times 5000 = 1000\,\text{fF}
$$

**Step 2 — Without load $C_L = 0$ (open-circuited).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{pd}^{(a)} &= R_d \cdot c L + 0.5 \cdot r \cdot c L \cdot L \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times 1000 \times 10^{-15} + 0.5 \times 400 \times 1000 \times 10^{-15} \\[4pt]
&= 200\,\text{ps} + 200\,\text{ps} = \mathbf{400\,ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — With load $C_L = 50\ \text{fF}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{pd}^{(b)} &= R_d \bigl( c L + C_L \bigr) + r L \, C_L + 0.5 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot L^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 200 \times (1000 + 50) \times 10^{-15} \;+\; 400 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \;+\; 200\,\text{ps} \\[4pt]
&= 210\,\text{ps} + 20\,\text{ps} + 200\,\text{ps} = \mathbf{430\,ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Analytical insight.**

The driver's linear term contributes **48.8 %** of the delay, the distributed RC quadratic term **46.5 %**, and the load $C_L$ **4.7 %** — confirming that **doubling $L$ quadruples the quadratic term**, which is the dominant reason that clock networks in FPGAs use **H-trees** (balanced, length-minimized) rather than straight lines.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Table — Routing-Resource Choices

| Routing Resource | Per-Seg Delay | Area Cost | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-length segment + PIP | Low (≈ 100 ps) | High (PIP per junction) | Local intra-CLB routing |
| Double-length segment | Medium | Lower | Inter-CLB within a quadrant |
| Hex / Long-line (length 4–24) | Lower per-unit-length | Lowest per-bit | Clocks, high-fanout control |
| Mux-based (e.g., Xilinx ULTRA) | Very low (50–80 ps) | Highest | Critical paths in UltraScale+ |
| Hard wire (e.g., DSP→BRAM) | Near-zero | Zero (pre-routed) | DSP cascades, memory data ports |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FPGA Interconnect Architecture — Block-Level Overview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CLB Logic Cell Array] --> B[Connection Box CB]
    C[Block RAM] --> B
    D[DSP Slice] --> B
    E[IOB / IO Tile] --> B
    B --> F[Switch Matrix SM]
    F --> G[Length 1 Segments]
    F --> H[Length 2 Segments]
    F --> I[Length N Long Lines]
    F --> J[Hex Lines Clock Nets]
    G --> F
    H --> F
    I --> F
    J --> F
    F --> K[Programmable Interconnect Points PIPs]
    K --> L[Configuration SRAM Cells]
    L -.-> F
    L -.-> B
    L -.-> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the **bidirectional data-flow** (solid arrows) versus the **configuration bitstream** (dotted arrows) that programs every PIP and Mux at FPGA power-up.

## 4.2 Switch Matrix Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SM[Switch Matrix SM]
        n1[Wire Segment Horizontal H1] --> p1[PIP 1]
        n2[Wire Segment Horizontal H2] --> p2[PIP 2]
        n3[Wire Segment Horizontal H3] --> p3[PIP 3]
        m1[Wire Segment Vertical V1] --> p1
        m2[Wire Segment Vertical V2] --> p2
        m3[Wire Segment Vertical V3] --> p3
        p1 --> o1[Output Pin to next SM]
        p2 --> o1
        p3 --> o1
    end
    sram[Config SRAM Bitstream] -.-> p1
    sram -.-> p2
    sram -.-> p3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each **PIP is a 6T-SRAM-controlled pass transistor**; closing it connects the two intersecting wires. The three PIPs here represent a **Wilton switch-box topology** — one of the most area-efficient SB architectures.

## 4.3 CAD Flow — Partitioning → Placement → Routing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HDL Netlist RTL] --> B[Synthesis + Technology Mapping]
    B --> C[Partitioning Kernighan-Lin or F-M]
    C --> D[Global Placement Simulated Annealing]
    D --> E[Detailed Placement]
    E --> F[Routing PathFinder Iterative]
    F --> G{Convergence No Congestion}
    G -->|Yes| H[Bitstream Generation]
    G -->|No| I[Rip-up and Re-route Increase Cost]
    I --> F
    H --> J[FPGA Configuration Download]
```

## 4.4 Distributed RC Wire Model — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver R_d] --> B[R1 rL/N]
    B --> C[R2 rL/N]
    C --> D[R3 rL/N]
    D --> E[R_N rL/N]
    B -.-> n1[C1 cL/N to GND]
    C -.-> n2[C2 cL/N to GND]
    D -.-> n3[C3 cL/N to GND]
    E -.-> n4[C_N cL/N to GND]
    E --> F[Load C_L]
    A --> IN[Input]
    F --> OUT[Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **lumped-$\pi$ RC model** used to derive the Elmore delay in Section 3. Each shunt capacitor $C_i$ is charged through the cumulative resistance to its left, explaining the **quadratic delay growth** with $L$.

## 4.5 Routing-Resource Graph (RRG) Abstraction

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Node 0 Source CLB Pin] --- B[Node 1 PIP]
    B --- C[Node 2 Length-1 Segment]
    C --- D[Node 3 Switch Matrix]
    D --- E[Node 4 Length-2 Segment]
    D --- F[Node 5 Length-1 Segment]
    F --- G[Node 6 PIP Sink CLB Pin]
    E --- H[Node 7 Long Line]
    H --- G
    classDef crit fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222
    class B,D,G crit
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PathFinder treats the FPGA as a directed Routing-Resource Graph (RRG).** Each node is a wire or pin; each edge is a PIP. The shortest-path search (Dijkstra / A*) operates on this graph with congestion-aware edge costs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between single-length, double-length, and long-line routing segments in an FPGA. Why is the interconnect area typically larger than the logic area in modern FPGAs?**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**
- **Single-length segments** connect adjacent switch matrices, offering maximum flexibility but higher delay and area due to the large number of PIPs.
- **Double-length segments** span two switch matrices, halving the PIP count for medium-range connections.
- **Long lines** span an entire row/column or chip, with very few (or no) PIPs, and are reserved for clocks, enables, and high-fanout nets.
- **Why interconnect dominates area:** A single logic cell has only a few I/O pins (≈ 4–8), but the routing fabric must allow **any** input to connect to **any** logic cell. With $n$ cells, the interconnect complexity grows as $O(n^{2})$ while the logic grows as $O(n)$ — so the interconnect area **scales super-linearly** and routinely exceeds 70–80% of the die.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Elmore delay. State the expression for the Elmore delay of a uniformly distributed RC line driven by a source resistance $R_d$.**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
The **Elmore delay** of a linear RC network is the **first moment of its impulse response**, computed as the sum over every capacitor of (resistance seen from the source) × (capacitor value). It is the **industry-standard first-order delay estimator** used by FPGA timing analyzers because it is computationally cheap yet sufficiently accurate for interconnect-dominated paths.

For a uniform RC line of length $L$ driven by a source resistance $R_d$, with per-unit-length resistance $r$ and capacitance $c$:

$$ t_{pd} = R_d \, c L + \tfrac{1}{2}\, r \, c \, L^{2} $$

The first term is **linear in $L$** (driver-limited); the second is **quadratic in $L$** (wire-limited).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** write just the final Elmore formula in the derivation part — **show the substitution of $R_{\text{seen}}(i)$ and the summation** (marks are awarded for the discrete-to-continuous limit, the standard error).
> - For PathFinder questions, students forget to mention the **monotonic-convergence** property of the present-congestion term $p(n) \to 0$. This is a 2-mark deduction trap.
> - In placement questions, **simulated annealing uses a geometric cooling schedule**, not a linear one. Confusing the two costs 1 mark.
> - Drawings of switch matrices: students often draw the **PIPs as fixed connections** instead of **pass-transistor symbols controlled by SRAM cells** — 1 mark lost for not showing the programming mechanism.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **architecture of the programmable interconnect** in an FPGA, clearly identifying CLBs, IOBs, switch matrices, connection boxes, and routing segments. Discuss how **Configuration SRAM cells** control the Programmable Interconnect Points (PIPs). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Elmore delay** for a uniformly distributed RC line of length $L$ modelled as $N$ cascaded RC $\pi$-sections, driven by a source resistance $R_d$, per-unit-length $r$ and $c$, and terminated in a load $C_L$. Show the limit as $N \to \infty$ and discuss the engineering implications. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **CLB Array** — Replicated array of configurable logic blocks, each containing LUTs, FFs, and a local crossbar.
2. **IOB Ring** — Surrounds the array, providing pad drivers and input synchronizers.
3. **Connection Box (CB)** — Provides the **fan-out** from each CLB pin into a small set of wire segments.
4. **Switch Matrix (SM)** — Located at every grid intersection; routes horizontal and vertical segments via **PIPs**.
5. **Wire Segments** — Length-1, length-2, length-4, and long lines.
6. **Configuration SRAM** — A large dedicated SRAM array stores one bit per PIP. At configuration time, bits are loaded and **latch the pass transistors ON or OFF** for the entire operating session.
7. **Programming Mechanism** — Each PIP is a CMOS pass-transistor (or transmission gate) whose gate is tied to an SRAM cell output. When the bit = 1, the transistor is ON and the wires are connected; when the bit = 0, the wires are isolated.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Full derivation as in **Section 3.1** of these notes:

- Resistance seen by the $i$-th shunt capacitor: $R_{\text{seen}}(i) = R_d + r \cdot i \cdot L / N$.
- Capacitor at node $i$: $C_i = c L / N$.
- Elmore delay to the far end:
$$ t_{pd} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( R_d + \frac{r i L}{N} \right) \frac{c L}{N} + \left( R_d + r L \right) C_L $$
- Evaluate the sums: $\sum 1 = N$, $\sum i = N(N+1)/2$.
- Take the limit $N \to \infty$ with $C_L = 0$ to obtain:
$$ t_{pd} = R_d \, c L + 0.5 \, r \, c \, L^{2} $$

**Engineering implications:**
- The **quadratic term** dominates for long wires → justifies segmented routing and **repeater (buffer) insertion**.
- The **linear term** dominates for short wires and high-impedance drivers → motivates **stronger driver sizing** in synthesis.
- This model underlies the delay estimates used in **VPR** and **Vivado** static timing analysis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Kernighan–Lin (K-L) partitioning algorithm** step-by-step. How does the Fiduccia–Mattheyses (F-M) improvement reduce the complexity, and why is partitioning necessary before placement? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **simulated-annealing-based placement algorithm** with the acceptance probability expression. Explain why **PathFinder routing** uses an iterative rip-up and re-route strategy and how the **cost function** penalizes congestion. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**K-L Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize a balanced 2-way partition of the $n$ cells into groups A and B.
2. Compute the initial **gain** $g(v)$ for each cell: $g(v) = E(v) - I(v)$, where $E(v)$ is the number of nets cut by moving $v$ to the other side, and $I(v)$ is the number of nets that become internal.
3. Repeatedly pick the *unlocked* swap pair $(a, b)$ that **maximizes the gain** $g(a) + g(b) - 2 \cdot c(a, b)$ where $c$ is the connection term; lock $a$ and $b$.
4. After all $n/2$ swaps, compute the cumulative gain curve $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ and find $k^*$ that maximizes it. Lock the configuration at $k^*$ and reset all locks.
5. Repeat until $G_{k^*} \le 0$.

**F-M Improvement:**
- Operates on **single-cell moves** (not swaps), so complexity drops to $O(P)$ per pass where $P$ is the total pin count.
- Uses a **bucket list** of gains, so the highest-gain cell is moved in $O(1)$.
- Updates gains of neighbours incrementally after each move.

**Why partition first?**
- A flat placement of millions of cells is computationally infeasible.
- Hierarchical partitioning reduces the problem into a **divide-and-conquer** tree, allowing the placer to handle each region independently and exploit **locality** of intra-region nets.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Simulated Annealing Placement:**
- Start at a high temperature $T_0$ where almost all moves (cell swaps or relocations) are accepted, including bad ones.
- Acceptance probability for a move that worsens the cost by $\Delta \Phi > 0$:
$$ P(\Delta \Phi, T) = \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{\Delta \Phi}{T} \right) $$
- Cooling schedule: $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, with $\alpha \approx 0.95$.
- Stops when $T$ falls below a freezing threshold.

**PathFinder Routing:**
- Routes all nets using **Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm** on the Routing-Resource Graph.
- Early iterations allow **over-congestion** (multiple nets sharing a node).
- After each pass, every over-used node's **present congestion** $p(n) > 0$ inflates its cost:
$$ \text{Cost}(n) = b_n + h_n \cdot p(n) $$
- Nets routed through congested nodes are **ripped up** and re-routed, finding detours.
- Iteration continues until **monotonic convergence**: $p(n) \to 0$ for all $n$, ensuring 100% routability with minimal detours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (3-Mark Sub-Question):** Always mention the term **monotonic convergence** and that $h_n$ is the **historical** penalty that prevents oscillation between congested and uncongested solutions across iterations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- **Interconnect Dominance:** **More than 70 % of FPGA die area** and **more than 50 % of the critical-path delay** come from programmable interconnect — placement and routing decisions matter more than logic choice.

- **Partitioning Goal:** Minimize **cut-size** while keeping partitions **balanced**. Industrial tools use the **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (F-M)** algorithm with $O(P)$ complexity, an improvement over the $O(n^{2\log n})$ Kernighan–Lin algorithm.

- **Placement Goal:** Minimize **total bounding-box wire-length** $\sum L_{\text{bb}}(N)$. **Simulated annealing** with acceptance probability $\exp(-\Delta \Phi / T)$ is the de-facto standard (VPR); **force-directed** placement is faster but lower quality.

- **Routing Architecture:** Three classes of wire segments — **single-length, double-length, long lines (and hex lines)**. Each is a trade-off between **flexibility, delay, and area**. **Mux-based** routing (e.g., Xilinx UltraScale) replaces pass-transistor PIPs for higher speed.

- **PIPs:** The atomic switch — a **pass transistor or tri-state buffer** controlled by a **Configuration SRAM cell** (6T-SRAM). One bit per PIP, loaded at power-up.

- **PathFinder Algorithm:** Iterative rip-up and re-route. Cost function = **base + history × present-congestion**. **Monotonic convergence** is its key property.

- **Elmore Delay (the must-know formula):**
$$ t_{pd} = R_d \, c L + 0.5 \, r \, c L^{2} $$
- **Linear term** → driver-limited; **quadratic term** → wire-limited.

- **Why Repeaters:** Inserting buffers every $L_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{2R_0 / (rR_d)}$ converts the **quadratic** delay into a **linear** dependence on $L$.

- **Clock Networks:** Use **H-trees or spines** so that the clock arrives at all flip-flops with **balanced** path lengths — minimising clock skew that arises from the **quadratic wire-delay** term.

- **FPGA Tool Mapping:** Synthesis → Partitioning → Global Placement → Detailed Placement → Routing (PathFinder) → Bitstream Generation.

- **Logical Effort Hint:** For a fanout of $F$, the path delay is approximately $t_{pd} \approx \tau \, F (1 + 0.7 F)$, useful for **fanout-driven buffering** decisions.

- **Why wires dominate:** $R \propto L$ and $C \propto L$, but the distributed-RC delay of a line is **quadratic in $L$** — this single fact explains every architectural choice in modern FPGA routing fabrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications: Embedded System Design Using FPGAs, DSP Using FPGAs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications: Embedded System Design Using FPGAs, DSP Using FPGAs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 FPGA-Based Embedded System Design

**Formal Definition:**
An **FPGA-based embedded system** is a self-contained computing platform where a Field Programmable Gate Array acts as the central processing element, integrating hardware (custom digital logic, soft/hard processor cores, memory controllers, peripherals) and software (firmware running on embedded processors) on a single reconfigurable silicon fabric to perform a dedicated function within a larger mechanical or electrical system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term *"embedded system"* in the FPGA context refers to a tightly coupled hardware-software system where the FPGA is *not* a general-purpose computer, but a **custom-tuned engine** optimized for a specific real-time task (e.g., motor control, packet routing, image processing).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Swiss Army Knife Workshop:**
Imagine a carpenter's workshop. A traditional microcontroller is a **fixed toolbox** — it has a hammer, a saw, and a screwdriver bolted to the wall. You must rearrange *your workflow* around these fixed tools. An **FPGA**, on the other hand, is a **3D printer for tools** — given a blueprint (the bitstream), it can *literally fabricate* the exact hammer, saw, or screwdriver you need for the job, and you can re-fabricate new tools tomorrow. The FPGA-based embedded system is the *workshop itself* — it has the printer (FPGA), raw material bins (BRAM/registers), conveyor belts (interconnect/AXI buses), and an operator console (soft-core processor + firmware) all working as one cohesive unit.

**Key Embedded System Metrics Used by KTU Examiners:**

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Determinism** | **Hard real-time** | Response time is guaranteed; no OS jitter |
| **Latency** | **< 1 µs** typical | Time from input stimulus to output reaction |
| **Clock Frequency** | **100 – 500 MHz** | Operating frequency of the FPGA fabric |
| **Power Dissipation** | **0.5 W – 5 W** | Dynamic + static CMOS power |
| **Logic Density** | **10K – 500K LUTs** | Combinational + sequential capacity |

---

### 1.2 DSP Using FPGAs

**Formal Definition:**
**DSP using FPGAs** refers to the implementation of Digital Signal Processing algorithms (filters, transforms, modulators, codecs) directly on the programmable logic fabric of an FPGA, exploiting **parallelism, pipelining, and dedicated hardware multiplier/DSP blocks** to achieve throughput far exceeding sequential DSP microprocessors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** A *DSP processor* (e.g., TMS320) executes instructions sequentially — even if it has a MAC unit, it is still one MAC unit. An *FPGA-DSP* can instantiate **hundreds of parallel MAC units**, multiplying throughput by orders of magnitude.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Supermarket Checkout:**
A DSP processor is a **single checkout counter** with one cashier. Customers (data samples) form a line and are processed one-by-one at, say, 5 customers/minute. An FPGA-based DSP is like **opening 100 checkout counters side by side**, each with a cashier — now 500 customers/minute flow through. Both systems "do the same math," but the FPGA's *spatial parallelism* (multiple physical MAC units) destroys the bottleneck of sequential instruction fetch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Term to Memorize:** *Compute Density* — defined as **GMACs/sec/Watt (Giga MAC operations per second per Watt)**. Modern FPGAs achieve **> 100 GMACs/sec/Watt**, often **10× more efficient** than DSP processors for sustained workloads.

---

### 1.3 Why FPGAs for Embedded & DSP? (The "Why" Behind the Curriculum)

Three engineering reasons that the KTU 2024 syllabus stresses:

1. **Parallelism** — N independent operations per clock cycle.
2. **Determinism** — Worst-case latency is known at synthesis time, critical for **hard real-time** systems.
3. **Reconfigurability** — The same FPGA can be re-bitstreamed in milliseconds to perform a *completely different* algorithm (e.g., a Wi-Fi modem on Monday, an LTE modem on Tuesday).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of an FPGA-Based Embedded System

An FPGA-based embedded system is typically built from **three layered hierarchies** that the KTU syllabus expects students to draw and label.

**Layer 1 — The Compute Layer:**
- **Soft-core processors**: MicroBlaze, RISC-V (e.g., SiFive E21), Nios-II
- **Hard-core processors**: ARM Cortex-A9 / Cortex-A53 (in Zynq / ZynqMP SoCs)
- **Custom Hardware Accelerators**: RTL blocks for compute-heavy tasks

**Layer 2 — The Memory & Interconnect Layer:**
- **BRAM (Block RAM)**: On-chip dual-port memory, typically **18 Kb or 36 Kb** per block
- **DDR Controller**: Interfaces to external DRAM
- **AXI / AHB / Wishbone Buses**: Standard on-chip interconnects
- **DMA Engines**: For high-bandwidth data movement without CPU intervention

**Layer 3 — The I/O & Peripheral Layer:**
- **GPIO, UART, SPI, I²C, Ethernet MAC, PCIe, USB**
- **ADCs / DACs interfaces**: For real-world analog signal digitization
- **Custom LVDS / SerDes links**: For chip-to-chip high-speed signaling

---

### 2.2 FPGA-DSP Design Flow (The 8-Stage Pipeline)

1. **System Specification** — Define algorithm, sampling rate, SNR target.
2. **Algorithm Development** — MATLAB / Python floating-point model.
3. **Fixed-Point Conversion** — Quantize to N-bit integers (typically 16-bit or 18-bit).
4. **Architecture Mapping** — Decide between *fully parallel*, *pipelined*, or *folded* structures.
5. **HDL Coding** — VHDL / Verilog / HLS (C-to-RTL).
6. **Synthesis & Place-and-Route** — Convert to bitstream.
7. **Verification** — Simulation + on-board testing.
8. **Deployment** — Load bitstream into configuration memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Expected Knowledge:** The student must be able to identify *which step* determines throughput (Step 4 — architecture mapping) and *which step* determines clock frequency (Step 6 — P&R).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numericals)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** | Hz |
| 2 | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ | **FIR Filter Difference Equation** | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}$ | **N-point DFT** | complex |
| 4 | $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ | **Twiddle Factor** | unit complex |
| 5 | $\text{SNR} = 6.02B + 1.76$ dB | **Quantization SNR (B-bit ADC)** | dB |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} = f_{clk} \times N_{parallel}$ | **Parallel MAC throughput** | MACs/sec |
| 7 | $\text{Latency} = D_{pipe} \cdot T_{clk} + t_{setup}$ | **Pipeline Latency** | seconds |
| 8 | $\text{Power}_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | **Dynamic CMOS Power** | Watts |
| 9 | $\text{MAC/cycle} = N_{DSP48} \times \text{Op/MAC}$ | **DSP Block Throughput** | ops/cycle |
| 10 | $\text{Goertzel: } \vert X[k] \vert^2 = y_N^2 + y_{N-1}^2 - 2 \cos(2\pi k/N) y_N y_{N-1}$ | **Single-bin DFT via Goertzel** | unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** When a question asks *"compare FPGA-DSP vs DSP processor"*, examiners expect the formula in row 6: an FPGA with **$N_{parallel} = 64$ MAC units** at **$f_{clk} = 250$ MHz** gives **16 GMACs/sec** — a single DSP processor at 1 GHz with 1 MAC gives **1 GMACs/sec**. The FPGA is **16× faster**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** 5G NR base-station channel filtering, OFDM modulation — FPGAs handle **sub-microsecond** symbol timing.
- **Radar / Sonar:** Pulse compression, beamforming, MTI filtering — latency-bound; cannot use cloud computing.
- **Medical Imaging:** MRI k-space reconstruction, ultrasound beamforming — sample rates of **40 MSPS**.
- **Audio / Video Codecs:** MP3, AAC, H.264 encoders — systolic FIR/IIR arrays.
- **Industrial Motor Control:** Field-Oriented Control (FOC) loops at **< 10 µs** execution time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: FIR Filter Throughput on FPGA vs DSP Processor

**Problem Setup:** Implement a **16-tap FIR filter** $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{15} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$. Compare the throughput of a sequential DSP processor vs a fully parallel FPGA implementation.

**Step 1 — DSP Processor Approach (Sequential):**

A DSP processor has **1 MAC unit**. To compute one output sample, it must perform **16 multiply-accumulate operations sequentially**.

$$
T_{sample,DSP} = 16 \times T_{MAC} = 16 \times \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

For a DSP clock of **$f_{clk} = 1$ GHz**:

$$
T_{sample,DSP} = 16 \times 1 \text{ ns} = 16 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
f_{sample,max,DSP} = \frac{1}{T_{sample,DSP}} = \frac{1}{16 \text{ ns}} = 62.5 \text{ MSPS}
$$

**Step 2 — FPGA Approach (Fully Parallel, Unfolded):**

The FPGA instantiates **16 physical MAC units** operating concurrently. The 16 multiplies and 15 additions are done in **one clock cycle** (with a tree-adder).

$$
T_{sample,FPGA} = 1 \times T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}
$$

For a typical FPGA fabric clock of **$f_{clk} = 250$ MHz**:

$$
T_{sample,FPGA} = 1 \times 4 \text{ ns} = 4 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
f_{sample,max,FPGA} = \frac{1}{4 \text{ ns}} = 250 \text{ MSPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup Factor:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{f_{sample,FPGA}}{f_{sample,DSP}} = \frac{250}{62.5} = 4 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The FPGA is **4× faster despite a 4× lower clock**. The speedup comes purely from **spatial parallelism** (16 vs 1 MAC units). KTU examiners love this counter-intuitive result.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipelined FIR Latency

**Problem Setup:** A 16-tap FIR is implemented as a **pipelined systolic array**. Each tap uses a dedicated multiplier, and a shift-register chain of 16 BRAMs feeds it. Find the latency of the pipeline.

**Step 1 — Identify Pipeline Stages:**

A systolic FIR has 3 stage types per tap:
- **Stage A:** Tap delay register ($T_A = 1$ clock)
- **Stage B:** Multiplication ($T_B = 1$ clock, assuming registered multiplier)
- **Stage C:** Adder tree ($T_C = \log_2(16) = 4$ clocks)

**Step 2 — Total Latency Equation:**

$$
L_{total} = L_{tap\_delay} + L_{mult} + L_{adder\_tree}
$$

$$
L_{total} = (16 \times 1) + (16 \times 1) + 4
$$

$$
L_{total} = 16 + 16 + 4 = 36 \text{ clocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Seconds:**

At **$f_{clk} = 200$ MHz**, $T_{clk} = 5$ ns:

$$
L_{total,sec} = 36 \times 5 \text{ ns} = 180 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Latency in a pipelined FIR is *fixed* — it does **not** grow with throughput. Once the pipeline is full, one new output emerges *every clock cycle* (1 / $f_{clk}$). This is the essence of *systolic computation* — high throughput with bounded latency.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: FFT Butterfly Complexity Reduction (Cooley-Tukey Radix-2)

**Problem Setup:** Compute an **8-point FFT** and show how FPGAs exploit the *in-place* butterfly structure.

**Step 1 — DFT Definition:**

$$
X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{7} x[n] \cdot W_8^{kn}, \quad W_8 = e^{-j 2\pi / 8}
$$

Direct DFT requires **$N^2 = 64$** complex multiplies.

**Step 2 — Decimation-In-Frequency (DIF) Decomposition:**

The 8-point FFT splits into two 4-point FFTs, requiring **3 stages of butterflies** (since $\log_2 8 = 3$).

$$
\text{Multiplies per stage} = N / 2 = 4
$$

$$
\text{Total multiplies} = \text{Stages} \times \text{Mults/stage} = 3 \times 4 = 12
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup of FFT over DFT:**

$$
\text{FFT Speedup} = \frac{N^2}{(N/2) \log_2 N} = \frac{64}{12} \approx 5.33 \times
$$

**Step 4 — FPGA Realization:** An 8-point FFT engine uses **4 radix-2 butterfly units per stage**, fully unrolled. The total hardware is **12 butterfly units**, but latency is just **3 clock cycles** (with all stages in parallel) — versus 64 clocks on a DSP processor.

> [!NOTE]
> **General formula (for KTU):** An N-point FFT has $\log_2 N$ stages, each with $N/2$ butterflies. Total butterflies $= (N/2) \log_2 N$. Latency = $\log_2 N$ clocks (fully unrolled).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: ADC SNR for an FPGA-Based DSP Front-End

**Problem Setup:** A medical ultrasound receiver uses a **14-bit ADC** to digitize echoes. Compute the theoretical SNR.

**Step 1 — Apply the Quantization SNR Formula:**

$$
\text{SNR} = 6.02 \cdot B + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute B = 14:**

$$
\text{SNR} = 6.02 \times 14 + 1.76
$$

$$
\text{SNR} = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with 10-bit ADC (typical low-end):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{10} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic Range Improvement:**

$$
\Delta \text{SNR} = 86.04 - 61.96 = 24.08 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each additional bit** of ADC resolution yields **~6 dB** more SNR — a 4-bit upgrade is a **24 dB** (≈ 16×) dynamic-range gain, justifying why high-end DSP-on-FPGA systems prefer 14–16 bit converters.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: FPGA vs DSP Processor vs ASIC

| Parameter | FPGA | DSP Processor | ASIC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Spatial (parallel) | Temporal (sequential) | Spatial (parallel) |
| **Peak Throughput** | **10 – 1000 GMACs/s** | 1 – 5 GMACs/s | 50 – 5000 GMACs/s |
| **Flexibility** | Reconfigurable | Programmable | Fixed (one-time) |
| **NRE Cost** | Zero (off-the-shelf) | Zero | **>$1M** |
| **Time-to-Market** | **Weeks** | Weeks | **Months – Years** |
| **Power Efficiency** | 10–100 GMACs/W | 1–5 GMACs/W | 50–500 GMACs/W |
| **Real-time Determinism** | **High (compile-time known)** | Medium (OS jitter) | High |
| **Best Use Case** | Low-volume + evolving | Low-cost, stable algo | High-volume, mature algo |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: FPGA-Based Embedded System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Real World Input Signal] --> B[ADC]
    B --> C[DMA Engine]
    C --> D[BRAM Buffer]
    D --> E[Hardware Accelerator: FIR FFT etc]
    E --> F[AXI Interconnect]
    F --> G[Soft Core Processor MicroBlaze]
    F --> H[DAC Output Stage]
    H --> I[Actuator or Display]
    G --> J[UART Console Debug]
    G --> K[External DDR Memory]
    subgraph "FPGA Fabric on Single Chip"
      C
      D
      E
      F
      G
      H
    end
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style I fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style G fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style E fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **blue** input block represents the analog physical world (sensor, antenna, microphone).
- The **red** block is the *custom hardware accelerator* — the FPGA's "superpower" (e.g., a 64-tap systolic FIR).
- The **orange** block is the *soft-core processor* running C firmware for control flow and housekeeping.
- All three share the **AXI interconnect** (the FPGA's "data highway").

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Structure of a Single DSP48 Slice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[18-bit Input A] --> M[25 x 18 Multiplier]
    B[18-bit Input B] --> M
    M --> P[48-bit Product]
    P --> AD[Adder or Subtractor]
    C[48-bit Cascade Input] --> AD
    AD --> R[Output Register with Optional Pipeline]
    R --> O1[48-bit Output P]
    R --> O2[30-bit Pattern Detect]
    R --> O3[Carry Out for Accumulation]
    style M fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style AD fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style R fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** The DSP48 block is the FPGA's **hardware multiplier + accumulator**. The 25×18 signed multiply produces a 48-bit product. A cascade path allows multiple DSP48s to chain into **MAC arrays** (Multiply-Accumulate) without leaving the DSP column.

---

### 4.3 Flow Diagram: FPGA-DSP Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: System Specification] --> S2[Step 2: Floating Point Model in MATLAB]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Fixed Point Quantization B bits]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Architecture Choice: Parallel or Pipelined or Folded]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: HDL Coding VHDL or Verilog or HLS]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Synthesis and Place and Route]
    S6 --> S7{Step 7: Timing Met?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Step 8: Iterative Optimization]
    S8 --> S4
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Step 9: Bitstream Generation]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: On Board Validation]
    style S1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style S10 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style S7 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style S8 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** The loop between **S4 → S8** is the **iteration loop** of FPGA design — if the timing closure fails, you re-architect (add pipeline stages, reduce parallelism, lower clock). This is the "design pain" KTU examiners want students to be aware of.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Pipelined Systolic FIR Filter (4-Tap Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[x n] --> D1[Delay z-1] --> X2[x n-1] --> D2[Delay z-1] --> X3[x n-2] --> D3[Delay z-1] --> X4[x n-3]
    X1 --> M1[Multiplier h0]
    X2 --> M2[Multiplier h1]
    X3 --> M3[Multiplier h2]
    X4 --> M4[Multiplier h3]
    M1 --> A1[Adder]
    M2 --> A1
    A1 --> A2[Adder]
    M3 --> A2
    A2 --> A3[Adder]
    M4 --> A3
    A3 --> Y[Output y n]
    style M1 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style M2 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style M3 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style M4 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each tap has its **own physical multiplier** (4 multipliers, 4 registers, 3 adders for an N=4 FIR). This is the *spatial parallel* approach that makes FPGA-DSP 10–100× faster than sequential DSPs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 1]**
>
> **Q1. List any three advantages of implementing DSP algorithms on FPGAs over conventional DSP processors.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Massive Spatial Parallelism** — FPGAs can instantiate N parallel MAC units, multiplying throughput by N×, while a DSP processor has only 1 MAC unit. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **Determinism & Low Latency** — Worst-case latency is known at synthesis time, enabling hard real-time guarantees essential for control and communication systems. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **Reconfigurability** — The same FPGA can be re-bitstreamed in milliseconds to implement a different algorithm, supporting evolving standards (e.g., 4G → 5G). *(1 mark)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2]**
>
> **Q2. Define *systolic array* in the context of FPGA-based DSP and state why it is preferred for high-throughput FIR filtering.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *systolic array* is a network of identical processing elements (multipliers + adders) arranged in a regular, pipelined geometry where data flows rhythmically between neighbors in lockstep with the clock. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> It is preferred for FIR filtering because:
> - **Throughput is one sample per clock cycle** (after pipeline fill), independent of filter order N. *(0.75 marks)*
> - **Latency is bounded and predictable** — equal to (2N + $\log_2 N$) clocks, making it ideal for *hard real-time* applications. *(0.75 marks)*

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Module 4, Sample Question Pattern]**
>
> ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **FPGA-based embedded system**. Discuss the roles of soft-core processors, hardware accelerators, BRAM, and AXI interconnect. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Design a **16-tap FIR low-pass filter** with cutoff 5 kHz at a sampling rate of 44.1 kHz. Show how this filter is mapped onto an FPGA using a systolic array. Calculate the throughput and compare it with a sequential DSP processor implementation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The FPGA-based embedded system is organized in **three layers** (draw the block diagram from Section 4.1).

1. **Compute Layer** *(2 marks)*:
   - *Soft-core processor* (e.g., MicroBlaze, RISC-V): runs sequential C code for control, configuration, and non-critical tasks.
   - *Hardware accelerators*: RTL-coded kernels for compute-intensive DSP (FIR, FFT, encryption). These execute in **parallel**, offloading the processor.

2. **Memory & Interconnect Layer** *(2.5 marks)*:
   - *BRAM (Block RAM)*: on-chip, dual-port, 18/36 Kb blocks used for data/sample buffering. Zero-cycle access for nearby logic.
   - *AXI Interconnect*: ARM's standardized on-chip bus protocol; provides high-bandwidth (e.g., AXI4 = 256-bit data @ 250 MHz = 64 Gbps) data movement between processor, accelerators, and memory.
   - *DMA Engine*: bulk data movement without CPU intervention.

3. **I/O & Peripheral Layer** *(1.5 marks)*:
   - ADC/DAC interfaces, GPIO, UART, SPI, Ethernet MAC.
   - External memory controller (DDR) for large datasets.

4. **Integration in KTU Exam Context** *(1 mark)*: The processor runs a lightweight OS (FreeRTOS / bare-metal), configures the accelerator via memory-mapped registers, and the accelerator produces results that are read back via DMA — completing the embedded loop.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Filter Design:** Using the windowed-sinc method (Hamming window), the 16 coefficients $h[0] \dots h[15]$ are computed to satisfy a 5 kHz cutoff at 44.1 kHz sampling. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Systolic Mapping:** Each coefficient $h[k]$ is hardwired into one of 16 dedicated multipliers. A 16-stage shift register chain (using BRAM in *shift-register* mode) provides $x[n], x[n-1], \dots, x[n-15]$ in lockstep. A pipelined adder tree sums the 16 partial products. *(2 marks — refer to the systolic FIR diagram in Section 4.4)*

**Step 3 — Throughput on FPGA:** With a clock of $f_{clk} = 200$ MHz, after pipeline fill, one new output emerges per clock.

$$
f_{sample,FPGA} = \frac{f_{clk}}{1} = 200 \text{ MSPS}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Throughput on DSP Processor:** Sequential MAC execution needs 16 cycles per output.

$$
f_{sample,DSP} = \frac{f_{clk}}{16} = \frac{1 \text{ GHz}}{16} = 62.5 \text{ MSPS}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Comparison & Conclusion:** The FPGA delivers **~3.2× higher throughput** despite operating at **1/5th the clock speed**, purely from spatial parallelism. Additionally, the FPGA's latency is fixed (≈ 36 clocks = 180 ns) while the DSP processor's latency grows with N. *(2 marks)*

---

> ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
>
> **(a)** What is **fixed-point arithmetic**? Why is it essential in FPGA-based DSP design? Show how a floating-point design is converted to a 16-bit fixed-point format. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **8-point radix-2 DIF FFT algorithm**. Show how it is implemented on an FPGA using butterfly units and twiddle factor ROM. Calculate the number of complex multiplications saved compared to a direct DFT. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fixed-point arithmetic represents real numbers using an integer format with an implied binary point. A Qm.n format (e.g., Q1.14) uses 1 sign/integer bit and 14 fractional bits, giving range $[-1, +1 - 2^{-14}]$ with resolution $2^{-14}$.

**Why Essential in FPGA (2 marks):**
- Floating-point units (FPU) consume **5–10× more LUTs and DSP blocks** than fixed-point.
- Fixed-point enables **single-cycle MAC operations** in DSP48 slices.
- Power consumption is **~3× lower** at the same throughput.
- Determinism — every operation completes in a fixed number of cycles.

**Conversion Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Simulate the floating-point model in MATLAB with realistic inputs.
2. Determine the dynamic range (max absolute value) of each signal.
3. Assign an integer bit-width so that the maximum value uses the full range (with 1 headroom bit for overflow).
4. Assign fractional bits based on the required SNR / quantization noise floor:

$$
\text{SNR} = 6.02 \cdot B_{frac} + 1.76 \text{ dB}
$$

   For 14 fractional bits, SNR ≈ 86 dB.
5. Re-simulate the model in fixed-point and verify SNR; iterate the bit allocation.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — DFT Cost (2 marks):**

$$
\text{DFT Multiplies} = N^2 = 64 \text{ (for N=8)}
$$

**Step 2 — FFT Decomposition (2 marks):** The 8-point radix-2 DIF FFT splits into two 4-point DFTs, then four 2-point DFTs, requiring 3 butterfly stages.

**Step 3 — Butterfly Unit (1.5 marks):** A radix-2 butterfly computes:

$$
A' = A + B \cdot W_N^k, \quad B' = A - B \cdot W_N^k
$$

It uses 1 complex multiplier and 2 complex adders. Twiddle factors $W_N^k$ are precomputed and stored in a ROM (lookup table).

**Step 4 — FPGA Implementation (1 mark):** Each stage uses 4 butterfly units (since N/2 = 4). With full unrolling, all 3 stages run in parallel — total latency = 3 clock cycles. Total butterflies = $(N/2) \log_2 N = 4 \times 3 = 12$.

**Step 5 — Savings Calculation (0.5 mark):**

$$
\text{Multiplies Saved} = 64 - 12 = 52
$$

$$
\text{Speedup Factor} = \frac{64}{12} \approx 5.33 \times
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "parallelism" with "faster clock":** A common error is to say "FPGA is faster because it has a higher clock." WRONG. The FPGA is faster because it has **more functional units**, often at a *lower* clock.
> 2. **Forgetting pipeline-fill latency:** When asked for latency, students compute only the *steady-state* throughput and forget the **N-clock pipeline-fill delay** at startup.
> 3. **Confusing "DSP processor" with "DSP block":** A *DSP processor* is a chip (TMS320, SHARC). A *DSP block / DSP48 / DSP slice* is a *hardware multiplier* inside an FPGA. They are completely different things.
> 4. **Wrong units on throughput:** Throughput of FIR filter is in **MSPS** (Mega Samples Per Second), not in MHz. Clock is MHz; data rate is MSPS.
> 5. **Saying "FPGAs are software-programmed":** No — FPGAs are *hardware-configured* via a bitstream. This is fundamentally different from *software execution* on a CPU.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] An **FPGA-based embedded system** combines a soft/hard processor, hardware accelerators, BRAM, and AXI interconnect on a single reconfigurable chip.
- [x] **DSP on FPGA** exploits *spatial parallelism* and *pipelining* — many MAC units running concurrently.
- [x] The **Nyquist theorem** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is the foundational constraint for all DSP-based FPGA systems.
- [x] **FIR filter** $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] x[n-k]$ maps naturally to a **systolic array** on FPGA.
- [x] **FPGA throughput = $f_{clk}$** (one output per clock after pipeline fill).
- [x] **DSP processor throughput = $f_{clk} / N$** for an N-tap FIR.
- [x] **Quantization SNR** = $6.02B + 1.76$ dB — each extra bit gives ~6 dB.
- [x] **Radix-2 FFT** reduces complex multiplies from $N^2$ to $(N/2)\log_2 N$.
- [x] **Fixed-point** (Q-format) is preferred over floating-point in FPGA-DSP for **speed, area, and power**.
- [x] **DSP48 / DSP slice** is the FPGA's dedicated hardware multiplier — instantiate these rather than building multipliers from LUTs.
