- [x] **Determinism** (compile-time known latency) is the #1 reason FPGAs dominate hard real-time embedded systems.
- [x] **Compute density** (GMACs/sec/Watt) is the key metric for comparing FPGAs to DSP processors and GPUs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial FPGAs: Xilinx, Altera, Actel (Different Series Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT755 - FPGA BASED SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Commercial FPGAs: Xilinx, Altera, Actel (Different Series Description Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Commercial FPGAs** are off-the-shelf, mass-produced programmable logic devices manufactured by leading semiconductor vendors and sold through standard distribution channels. They integrate a sea of configurable logic blocks (CLBs), programmable interconnects, I/O blocks, and dedicated hardware resources (block RAM, DSP slices, transceivers, PLLs, clock managers, and hard processor cores) onto a single die. The KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT755 – Module 4) explicitly restricts this topic to a *descriptive study* of the major commercial families offered by the three pioneer vendors:

1. **Xilinx** (now AMD Xilinx)
2. **Altera** (now Intel FPGA / Intel Programmable Solutions Group)
3. **Actel** (now Microchip Technology, FPGA division)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This is a *descriptive-only* module. No internal architecture derivation of LUTs, switch matrices, or routing graphs is expected. Students must memorize the **series names, technology node, target application, and distinguishing features** of each family.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The FPGA Supermarket"

Imagine an FPGA as a **"LEGO Factory"**. Each commercial vendor is a different LEGO manufacturer. Just as LEGO sells the *City*, *Technic*, *Architecture*, and *Mindstorms* lines to suit different builders, FPGA vendors segment their products into **series** (called *families* or *device lines*). A student doing a low-cost college project will pick the "LEGO Classic" box (Xilinx Spartan / Altera Cyclone / Actel IGLOO), whereas a 5G base-station engineer picks the "LEGO Technic Expert" box (Xilinx Virtex / Altera Stratix / Actel PolarFire). The *technology node* (28 nm, 16 nm, 7 nm) is the size of each LEGO brick — smaller bricks mean more pieces per square centimetre and lower power.

### 1.3 Why Three Vendors? Market Context

| Vendor | Founding Year | Current Parent | Headquarters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Xilinx** | 1984 (inventor of FPGA) | AMD (acquired 2022) | San Jose, California, USA |
| **Altera** | 1983 | Intel (acquired 2015) | San Jose, California, USA |
| **Actel** | 1985 | Microchip Technology (acquired 2010) | Mountain View, California, USA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Note:** Xilinx holds ≈ **50–55%** of the global FPGA market, Intel (Altera) ≈ **30–35%**, and Microchip (Actel) ≈ **5–8%** (largest share in *radiation-hardened / aerospace* FPGAs). Lattice Semiconductor and QuickLogic make up the rest.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Xilinx — AMD Series Lineup

#### 2.1.1 Legacy Cost-Optimized Series
- **Spartan-6**: **45 nm** process, 6-input LUTs, ~150 K logic cells. Used in consumer displays, automotive infotainment.
- **Spartan-7**: **28 nm** process, Artix-7 silicon re-packaged into a low-cost line. Up to ~102 K logic cells.

#### 2.1.2 Mid-Range / General Purpose (7-Series)
- **Artix-7**: **28 nm**, low-power, mid-range. Transceivers up to 6.6 Gbps. Target: portable ultrasound, software-defined radio.
- **Kintex-7**: **28 nm**, balanced performance. Up to **478 K** logic cells, 32 transceivers at 12.5 Gbps. Target: 4K video processing, LTE baseband.
- **Virtex-7**: **28 nm**, top-tier. Up to **1.95 M** logic cells, 96 transceivers at 28 Gbps. Target: 100G optical transport, ASIC prototyping.

#### 2.1.3 UltraScale / UltraScale+ (20 nm & 16 nm)
- **Kintex UltraScale** (20 nm), **Kintex UltraScale+** (16 nm FinFET).
- **Virtex UltraScale / UltraScale+**: up to **8.4 M** logic cells; 32.75 Gbps / 58 Gbps transceivers.
- **Zynq-7000 / Zynq UltraScale+ (ZynqMP)**: SoC FPGAs integrating **ARM Cortex-A9 (dual)** or **Cortex-A53 (quad)** with FPGA fabric — industry-first *Processing System (PS) + Programmable Logic (PL)* architecture.

#### 2.1.4 Versal Adaptive Compute Acceleration Platform (ACAP) — 7 nm
- **Versal AI Edge / AI Core / Prime / Premium / HBM**: Scalar engines (ARM Cortex-A72), Adaptable Engines (FPGA fabric), Intelligent Engines (AI engines / DSP), and Connectivity Engines. 7 nm process. **Largest device: 21.5 M logic cells.**

#### 2.1.5 Newer Cost-Optimized
- **Spartan UltraScale+** (16 nm) — replaces Spartan-7 in newer designs.

### 2.2 Altera — Intel (formerly) Series Lineup

#### 2.2.1 Cost-Optimized
- **Cyclone IV** (60 nm), **Cyclone V** (28 nm), **Cyclone 10 LP** (20 nm), **Cyclone IV GX** (integrated transceivers up to 3.125 Gbps).

#### 2.2.2 Mid-Range
- **Arria V** (28 nm), **Arria 10** (20 nm): up to **560 K** logic elements, 17.4 Gbps transceivers, hardened floating-point DSP blocks. Target: video broadcast, military radar.

#### 2.2.3 High-End
- **Stratix IV** (40 nm), **Stratix V** (28 nm), **Stratix 10** (14 nm): up to **10.2 M** logic elements, 57.8 Gbps transceivers, **HBM2** integrated memory. Target: 400G networking, HPC.

#### 2.2.4 SoC FPGAs
- **Cyclone V SoC**, **Arria 10 SoC**: integrate **ARM Cortex-A9** dual-core HPS (Hard Processor System).
- **Stratix 10 SoC**: ARM Cortex-A53 quad-core.

#### 2.2.5 Newest — Agilex
- **Agilex 5 / 7 / 9** series (10 nm SuperFin / Intel 7): 2nd-gen Hyperflex core, 116 Gbps transceivers, Compute Express Link (CXL) support, hardware AI tensor blocks.

### 2.3 Actel — Microchip Series Lineup

Actel is *historically* famous for **non-volatile, flash-based and antifuse FPGAs**, and for the *only* radiation-hardened (Rad-Hard) FPGAs used in space.

#### 2.3.1 Antifuse (One-Time Programmable) — Oldest
- **Axcelerator**, **RTAX-S**, **RT-ProASIC3**: used in deep space (Mars rovers, satellites). Immune to radiation-induced configuration upsets.

#### 2.3.2 Flash-Based (Non-Volatile, Reprogrammable)
- **ProASIC3 / ProASIC3E / ProASIC3L**: 130 nm / low-power variants. 1 K to 3 M system gates.
- **IGLOO / IGLOO2 / IGLOO Nano**: 130 nm / 65 nm; ultra-low power, used in battery-operated IoT, **standby current as low as 2 µW**.
- **SmartFusion 2**: integrates **ARM Cortex-M3** with FPGA fabric — the "FPGA + MCU + fabric" SoC.

#### 2.3.3 Modern (Newer Microchip) Families
- **PolarFire**: **28 nm**, low-power mid-range. 100 K to 481 K logic elements, 12.7 Gbps transceivers. **Total power: 10× lower** than equivalent 7-series Xilinx at static.
- **PolarFire SoC**: adds **RISC-V (RV64IMAC)** quad-core hard processor + Linux-capable subsystem — industry first RISC-V SoC FPGA.
- **RTG4**: **65 nm**, radiation-tolerant, up to 150 K logic elements. Target: deep-space communication, satellite payloads.
- **Low-Density "Small" Families**: **IGLOO nano** (1 K–250 K gates), **ProASIC3 nano**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Metric Mappings)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although the topic is descriptive, the following *metric-conversion formulas* are high-yield for KTU numericals in Module 4 & 5.

| Quantity | Formula / Conversion | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Logic Cells (Xilinx) | 1 Logic Cell ≈ 1.125 6-input LUTs | — | 7-series and beyond |
| Logic Elements (Altera) | 1 LE = 1 4-input LUT + 1 FF | — | Altera/Intel convention |
| System Gates (Actel) | System Gates ≈ 3 × Logic Modules | — | Marketing number; not silicon area |
| Logic Modules (Actel) | 1 LM = 1 3-input LUT (VersaTile) | — | PolarFire uses 4-input |
| BRAM (Xilinx) | 36 Kb per block = 4 Kb × 9 parity | Kb | 7-series & UltraScale |
| M20K (Altera) | 20 Kb per block | Kb | Stratix 10 / Agilex |
| LSRAM (PolarFire) | 18 Kb per block | Kb | True dual-port |
| DSP48E1 / DSP48E2 | 25 × 18-bit multiply-add | bits | 7-series / UltraScale |
| Variable-Precision DSP | 27 × 27 integer or FP16/FP32 | bits | UltraScale+ / Versal |
| Transceiver Line Rate | $f_{\text{baud}} = \frac{f_{\text{ref}} \times M}{N}$ | Gbps | Where M/N is PLL feedback |
| Total On-Die Power | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{dynamic}} + P_{\text{static}}$ | W | Static dominates at idle |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f$ | W | Standard CMOS equation |
| Static Power (Process-Scaled) | $P_{\text{stat}} \propto V \cdot e^{-V_{t}/(n V_{T})}$ | W | Sub-threshold leakage |
| Energy per Switch | $E = C \cdot V^{2}$ | Joules | Per configuration bit |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Defence:** Actel RTAX/RTG4 (rad-hard) → satellites, Mars rovers, missile guidance.
- **Telecom (5G NR baseband):** Xilinx Versal / Altera Agilex 7 with hardened 100G/400G Ethernet MACs.
- **Automotive ADAS:** Xilinx Spartan UltraScale+ / Altera Cyclone 10 LP — ASIL-C/ASIL-D functional safety.
- **Medical Imaging:** Kintex-7 / PolarFire — portable ultrasound CT scanners.
- **Edge AI Inference:** Versal AI Edge / Agilex 5 — INT8 / FP16 tensor blocks for low-latency CNN.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since this is a **descriptive** KTU module, the "derivation" below is presented as an **analytical comparison matrix** with explicit step-by-step logical reasoning rather than algebraic derivations.

### 3.1 Vendor Positioning Triangle — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Identify the market axis.** Commercial FPGAs compete on three axes: *performance*, *power*, and *cost*.

**Step 2 — Map vendors to axes.**
- **Xilinx** = Performance leader (broadest portfolio, highest logic density).
- **Altera (Intel)** = Performance + integrated HBM (high-bandwidth memory).
- **Actel (Microchip)** = Power + radiation tolerance leader.

**Step 3 — Identify process nodes.** Smaller node → higher density + lower dynamic power + higher static leakage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Density Scaling} &\propto \frac{1}{s^{2}} \\
\text{Dynamic Power} &\propto s \\
\text{Static Power} &\propto \frac{1}{V_{t}} \quad \text{(leakage rises)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $s$ is the minimum feature size and $V_{t}$ is the threshold voltage.

**Step 4 — Apply to current commercial nodes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Xilinx Versal} &: 7 \text{ nm FinFET} \\
\text{Intel Agilex 9} &: 10 \text{ nm SuperFin} \\
\text{Microchip PolarFire} &: 28 \text{ nm planar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> PolarFire deliberately uses 28 nm to keep **static leakage very low** — at the cost of raw logic density.

> This is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 4 — study it thoroughly.

| Xilinx | Virtex US+ | 16 nm | 6-LUT | 8.4 M | 58 Gbps | Highest serial bandwidth | ASIC prototyping |
| Xilinx | Zynq-7000 | 28 nm | 6-LUT | 444 K | 12.5 Gbps | Dual Cortex-A9 + fabric | Embedded vision |

| Actel | RTAX-S | 250 nm | 2-OR | 4 M sys gates | None | Rad-hard antifuse | GEO orbit, missiles |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Decision Flow: "Which FPGA do I pick?"

**Step 1 — Application category.**

$$
\text{Application} = \{\text{Cost-critical}, \text{Performance-critical}, \text{Power-critical}, \text{Rad-tolerant}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Match to vendor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost-critical} &\rightarrow \text{Spartan-7 / Cyclone 10 LP / IGLOO2} \\
\text{Performance-critical} &\rightarrow \text{Versal / Agilex / Virtex / Stratix} \\
\text{Power-critical} &\rightarrow \text{PolarFire / IGLOO2} \\
\text{Rad-tolerant} &\rightarrow \text{RTG4 / RTAX-S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check process node for power budget.**

$$
P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^{2} \cdot f
$$

Lower $V$ (driven by smaller process) reduces $P_{\text{dyn}}$ quadratically.

**Step 4 — Check for embedded CPU need.**

$$
\text{If needs Linux + RTOS} \rightarrow \text{SoC FPGA (ZynqMP / Agilex SoC / PolarFire SoC)}
$$

**Step 5 — Check transceiver line rate requirement.**

$$
R_{\text{needed}} = \text{number of lanes} \times \text{line rate}
$$

Choose family with appropriate GT (Xilinx) / GX (Altera) / XCVR (Microchip) ratings.

### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Pattern]`

**(a) Describe the various series of Xilinx FPGAs with their process nodes and applications.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Xilinx (now AMD) segments its FPGA portfolio into **six major groups**:

**1. Low-Cost / Cost-Optimized:**
- **Spartan-6 (45 nm)** – legacy consumer interface; max 147 K logic cells.
- **Spartan-7 (28 nm)** – up to 102 K logic cells; no transceivers; **target: displays, set-top boxes, IoT edge.**
- **Spartan UltraScale+ (16 nm)** – successor to Spartan-7, ~1.5× density.

**2. Mid-Range 7-Series:**
- **Artix-7 (28 nm)** – low static power; 6.6 Gbps transceivers; **target: portable ultrasound, SDR.**
- **Kintex-7 (28 nm)** – best Mbps/$ ratio; 12.5 Gbps transceivers; **target: 4K video, LTE baseband, avionics.**

**3. High-End 7-Series:**
- **Virtex-7 (28 nm)** – up to 1.95 M logic cells, 96 × 28 Gbps transceivers; **target: 100G OTN, ASIC prototyping, radar.**

**4. UltraScale (20 nm) and UltraScale+ (16 nm FinFET+):**
- **Kintex / Virtex UltraScale+** – 4 to 8.4 M logic cells; 32.75 Gbps / 58 Gbps GTY transceivers; **target: 5G, defence, AI fabric.**

**5. SoC FPGAs:**
- **Zynq-7000 (28 nm)** – ARM Cortex-A9 dual + 7-series fabric.
- **Zynq UltraScale+ (16 nm)** – Cortex-A53 quad + Cortex-R5 dual + Mali-400 GPU + fabric; **target: ADAS, industrial robotics.**

**6. Versal ACAP (7 nm):**
- **Versal AI Edge, Prime, Premium, HBM** – combines Scalar (Cortex-A72), Adaptable (FPGA), Intelligent (AI Engine / DSP) and Connectivity engines. 7 nm FinFET, up to 21.5 M logic cells, 112 Gbps PAM4.

*Mark split: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 7 marks.*

---

**(b) Explain the main Altera (Intel) FPGA families with their typical applications.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Intel (formerly Altera) organises its devices into **four major groups**:

**1. Cyclone Series — Cost & Low Power:**
- **Cyclone IV (60 nm)** – up to 150 K LEs; legacy.
- **Cyclone V (28 nm)** – up to 301 K LEs; integrated transceivers in GX variant.
- **Cyclone 10 LP (20 nm)** – sub-1 W static power; **target: industrial control, motor drive, IoT.**

**2. Arria Series — Mid-Range:**
- **Arria V (28 nm)** – up to 220 K LEs; hardened PCIe Gen2.
- **Arria 10 (20 nm)** – up to 560 K LEs, 17.4 Gbps transceivers, **hard floating-point DSP blocks**; **target: military radar, broadcast encoders, medical imaging.**

**3. Stratix Series — High Performance:**
- **Stratix IV (40 nm)** and **Stratix V (28 nm)** – 28 Gbps / 14.1 Gbps transceivers.
- **Stratix 10 (14 nm)** – up to 10.2 M LEs, **integrated HBM2**, 57.8 Gbps transceivers, 2nd-gen Hyperflex routing; **target: 400G optical transport, HPC, ASIC emulation.**

**4. Agilex Series — Latest Generation (10 nm SuperFin / Intel 7):**
- **Agilex 5** – mid-range, RISC-V soft + ARM hard, hardware tensor block.
- **Agilex 7** – up to 2.4 M LEs, 116 Gbps transceivers, **Compute Express Link (CXL)**.
- **Agilex 9** – 1.9 M LEs + HBM2e + Compute; **target: data-centre AI acceleration, network function virtualisation.**

**SoC Variants:** Cyclone V SoC, Arria 10 SoC and Stratix 10 SoC all integrate **ARM Cortex-A9 / A53** hard processor systems with FPGA fabric using a coherent AXI bus.

*Mark split: 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks.*

---

### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Pattern]`

**(a) Discuss the various Actel (Microchip) FPGA families and their distinguishing characteristics.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Actel (Microchip) specialises in **non-volatile, low-power, radiation-tolerant FPGAs**. Families are classified by programming technology:

**1. Antifuse (One-Time Programmable, OTP):**
- **Axcelerator (350 nm)** and **RTAX-S (250 nm)** – configuration cell is a one-time-blown antifuse. The bit cell is **immune to radiation-induced SEU**; **target: GEO-orbit satellites, missile guidance, deep-space probes** (used in Mars rovers Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity).
- **RT-ProASIC3** – flash-based, but with rad-tol packaging.

**2. Flash-Based (Non-Volatile, Reprogrammable):**
- **ProASIC3 / ProASIC3E (130 nm)** – 30 K to 3 M system gates; live at power-up; **target: industrial automation, automotive.**
- **ProASIC3L** – low-power variant.
- **IGLOO (130 nm)** and **IGLOO2 (65 nm)** – **standby current as low as 2 µW**; **target: battery-powered IoT, wearables, energy harvesting.**
- **SmartFusion 2 (65 nm)** – integrates **ARM Cortex-M3 + FPGA + 5 Gbps transceivers**; **target: motor + sensor fusion in industrial robotics.**

**3. Modern Mid-Range:**
- **PolarFire (28 nm)** – up to 481 K logic elements, 12.7 Gbps transceivers; **total on-chip power ≈ ½ of equivalent 7-series Xilinx**; supports DDR4, PCIe Gen2.
- **PolarFire SoC** – adds **RISC-V RV64IMAC quad-core + Linux-capable MSS**; **target: secure edge, defence Linux SBC.**

**4. Radiation-Tolerant:**
- **RTG4 (65 nm)** – up to 150 K logic elements, **SEU-hardened flip-flops, on-chip EDAC, triple-mode redundancy (TMR)**; **target: LEO/MEO satellites, space-grade SDR.**

*Mark split: 2 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 7 marks.*

---

**(b) Compare the three commercial FPGA vendors (Xilinx, Altera, Actel) on the basis of process technology, programming technology, and target application.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Xilinx (AMD) | Altera (Intel) | Actel (Microchip) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Current process** | 7 nm (Versal), 16 nm (UltraScale+) | 10 nm (Agilex), 14 nm (Stratix 10) | 28 nm (PolarFire), 65 nm (RTG4) |
| **Logic element** | 6-input LUT (Logic Cell) | 4-input LUT (Logic Element) | 3- or 4-input VersaTile |
| **Programming** | SRAM (volatile) | SRAM (volatile) | Flash / Antifuse (non-volatile) |
| **Configuration** | Needs external boot flash | Needs external boot flash | **Single-chip, secure at power-up** |
| **Max logic** | 21.5 M (Versal Premium) | 10.2 M (Stratix 10) | 481 K (PolarFire) |
| **Max transceiver** | 112 Gbps PAM4 | 116 Gbps | 12.7 Gbps |
| **SoC FPGAs** | Zynq-7000, ZynqMP, Versal | Cyclone V SoC, Arria 10 SoC | SmartFusion 2 (M3), PolarFire SoC (RISC-V) |
| **AI Acceleration** | Versal AI Engine, DPU IP | Agilex Tensor Block, OpenVINO | VectorBlox AI IP on PolarFire |
| **Static power** | Moderate | Moderate | **Best (Flash, low-leakage)** |
| **Rad-hard variant** | Xilinx QML-V (limited) | Limited | **Industry leader (RTG4, RTAX-S)** |
| **Primary market** | Telecom, defence, AI edge, ASIC prot. | Data centre, HPC, broadcast | Aerospace, military, medical, low-power IoT |
| **Tool flow** | Vivado, Vitis, Vitis AI | Quartus Prime, OpenCL | Libero SoC, SoftConsole |

**Inference (1 mark for conclusion):** Xilinx leads in *raw performance and density*, Altera (Intel) leads in *integrated HBM and data-centre throughput*, and Actel (Microchip) is **unrivalled in non-volatile, low-power, and radiation-hardened** segments. For KTU industrial applications, the Cyclone / Spartan families dominate low-cost designs; for aerospace projects, Actel is the de-facto choice.

*Mark split: 1 (table) + 1 (table) + 1 (table) + 1 (table) + 1 (table) + 1 (table) + 1 (inference) = 7 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing *series* with *generation*:** Spartan-7 and Kintex-7 are both 7-*generation* (same silicon process) but are *different series* (cost vs mid-range). The 7-series is a generation; Spartan, Artix, Kintex, Virtex are series within it.
> 2. **Writing "Xilinx" and "Altera" instead of "Xilinx (AMD)" and "Altera (Intel)":** Lose ½ mark for not acknowledging the parent company — KTU 2024 emphasises current industry ownership.
> 3. **Missing *transceiver* speed** when comparing high-end devices: 16 Gbps vs 28 Gbps vs 58 Gbps vs 112 Gbps — examiners expect exact numbers in Mbps/Gbps.
> 4. **Forgetting *SoC FPGA* family:** Many students describe the 7-series but omit Zynq-7000 / Zynq UltraScale+ / Cyclone V SoC / SmartFusion 2 / PolarFire SoC. This costs a full 1–2 mark.
> 5. **No mention of *process node*:** Always state it (28 nm, 16 nm, 7 nm, etc.) — at least one compulsory sub-question tests it.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and read 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Three vendors:** Xilinx (AMD), Altera (Intel), Actel (Microchip).
- [ ] **Xilinx main series:** **Spartan** (cost), **Artix** (low-power mid), **Kintex** (balanced), **Virtex** (high-end), **Zynq-7000 / ZynqMP** (SoC), **Versal** (7 nm ACAP).
- [ ] **Altera main series:** **Cyclone** (cost), **Arria** (mid), **Stratix** (high), **Agilex** (latest 10 nm), **Cyclone V / Arria 10 SoC** (SoC).
- [ ] **Actel main families:** **ProASIC3** (flash), **IGLOO / IGLOO2** (low-power), **SmartFusion 2** (M3 SoC), **PolarFire / PolarFire SoC** (28 nm modern), **RTG4** (rad-tol), **RTAX-S / Axcelerator** (rad-hard antifuse).
- [ ] **LUT types:** Xilinx 6-LUT, Altera 4-LE, Actel VersaTile (3- or 4-input).
- [ ] **Programming tech:** Xilinx = SRAM (volatile), Altera = SRAM (volatile), Actel = Flash/Antifuse (non-volatile).
- [ ] **Process nodes:** Xilinx reaches **7 nm**, Intel **10 nm**, Actel max **28 nm** (rad variants 65 nm).
- [ ] **Highest density:** Xilinx Versal Premium = **21.5 M** logic cells.
- [ ] **Highest transceiver speed:** Xilinx Versal Premium = **112 Gbps** PAM4; Agilex 9 = **116 Gbps**.
- [ ] **HBM integration:** Stratix 10, Agilex 9 (Intel only).
- [ ] **Rad-hard leadership:** Actel (RTG4, RTAX-S).
- [ ] **Cortex CPUs in SoCs:** Zynq-7000 = A9 dual, ZynqMP = A53 quad, Versal = A72, Cyclone V SoC = A9 dual, SmartFusion 2 = **M3**, PolarFire SoC = **RISC-V quad**.
- [ ] **Tool flows:** Vivado (Xilinx), Quartus Prime (Intel), Libero SoC (Microchip).
- [ ] **Key market positioning:** Xilinx = perf leader, Intel = data-centre + HBM, Microchip = aerospace + ultra-low power.
- [ ] **Always mention:** Process node + LUT type + programming technology + target application + parent company.
